title: Homogenization of point interactions
abstract: We consider a non-relativistic quantum particle in $\mathbb{R}^d$, $d=2$ or $d = 3$, interacting with singular zero-range potentials concentrated on a large collection of points. We analyze the homogenization regime where the intensities of the singular potentials and the distances between the points simultaneously go to zero as their number grows, while the total interaction strength remains finite. Assuming that the singular potentials have negative scattering lengths and are uniformly distributed, we prove the strong resolvent convergence as $N \to \infty$ of the family of operators to a Schrödinger operator with a regular electrostatic potential. The result is obtained via $Γ$-converge of the associated quadratic forms. Moreover, in presence of an external trapping potential, the convergence is lifted to uniform resolvent sense.
one_liner: Shows that dense zero-range point interactions converge to an effective regular potential in 2D and 3D quantum systems.
why_it_matters: It gives a rigorous route from many singular scatterers to continuum effective-media models relevant for engineered wave and quantum-material platforms.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a quantum particle in 2D or 3D interacting with many zero-range scatterers whose strengths and spacings shrink as their number grows. Under uniform spatial distribution and negative scattering length assumptions, the authors prove strong resolvent convergence to a Schrödinger operator with a regular electrostatic potential via Gamma-convergence of quadratic forms. With an external trapping potential, they strengthen the result to uniform resolvent convergence. This provides a mathematically controlled effective-medium limit for dense singular-scatterer systems.
platform_or_system: Dense arrays of zero-range quantum scatterers
main_result: A family of Hamiltonians with many shrinking point interactions is proven to converge to a regular-potential Schrödinger operator, with uniform resolvent convergence in trapped settings.
application_angle: The result supports effective design rules for structured media where many microscopic scatterers are replaced by tractable continuum potentials.

title: Coupled Plasmonic-Waveguide Resonance Geometry for Enhanced Infrared Absorption in Semiconductor Solar Cells
abstract: Thin films are preferred for high photocurrent conversion efficiency, but strong photon absorption at photon energies below the bandgap (near and shortwave infrared) typically requires thicker semiconductor layers. To address this tradeoff, various optical approaches have been proposed, including light scattering within the active layer, reducing surface reflection, and using resonant structures to improve light confinement, trapping, and coupling. However, resonant structures often operate over a narrow spectral range, limiting their use of the full solar spectrum, and can involve complex fabrication and careful structural design. In this work, I propose a new method to enhance absorption in semiconductor solar cells across wide angular and spectral ranges for both polarization states (transverse electric (TE) and transverse magnetic (TM)). The method is based on a coupled plasmonic waveguide resonance (CPWR) configuration excited in a planar layered structure that can be fabricated using simple deposition techniques. Using the proposed approach, as an example, the thickness of the required Silicon (Si) layer can be reduced from approximately 130 to 180 μm (the typical Si thickness in commercial solar cells) to only a few microns. The method enables efficient harvesting of the infrared portion of the solar spectrum. By exciting CPWRs, the method overcomes the sharp drop in the absorption spectrum of conventional Si solar cells at wavelengths longer than 1100 nm. Field calculations demonstrate that light is efficiently absorbed in the Si layer at the resonant wavelengths. The proposed approach is general and can be applied to different types of semiconducting and prism materials. To maintain high absorption at wavelengths below 1100nm, an additional semiconductor metal semiconductor configuration is proposed, in which a thinner Si layer is added beneath the metal layer.
one_liner: Proposes a coupled plasmonic-waveguide resonance stack that boosts infrared absorption in thin semiconductor solar cells.
why_it_matters: It targets broadband, angle-tolerant light trapping with simple planar fabrication, potentially cutting absorber thickness to only a few microns.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a planar coupled plasmonic waveguide resonance geometry to enhance TE and TM absorption over wide spectral and angular ranges. In the silicon example, the concept is aimed at recovering strong absorption beyond the conventional 1100 nm drop-off while reducing the needed Si thickness from commercial-scale values to a few microns. Field calculations indicate resonant confinement and absorption inside the semiconductor layer. An added semiconductor-metal-semiconductor stack is proposed to preserve high sub-1100 nm absorption.
main_result: The proposed CPWR architecture enables strong infrared absorption in ultrathin semiconductor layers while remaining compatible with simple layered fabrication.
application_angle: This could lower material use and expand near-IR harvesting in practical thin-film photovoltaic and optoelectronic devices.

title: Enhanced Emission from Boron-Vacancy Center in Rhombohedral Boron Nitride
abstract: Various stacking combinations of the two-dimensional (2D) boron nitride (BN) honeycomb lattice can significantly modify the properties of the resulting 2D BN crystal. Here, we demonstrate through first-principles calculations that the brightness of the negatively charged boron-vacancy center (V$_\text{B}^{-}$) is enhanced by at least one order of magnitude in rhombohedral BN (rBN) compared to hexagonal BN (hBN), while the spin properties remain either comparable or even improved. This enhancement arises from the reduced symmetry of the crystal field in rBN. Our results suggest that room-temperature single-spin coherent control of V$_\text{B}^{-}$ is feasible in rBN, enabling its application as a single-spin quantum sensor in this 2D host. These findings demonstrate that engineered stacking of BN layers provides a powerful means to tailor the properties of embedded quantum defects.
one_liner: First-principles modeling predicts much brighter boron-vacancy emission in rhombohedral BN than in hexagonal BN.
why_it_matters: A brighter yet spin-coherent 2D defect center would improve room-temperature quantum sensing and single-photon device performance.
detailed_summary: Using first-principles calculations, the authors compare the negatively charged boron-vacancy center in rhombohedral and hexagonal boron nitride. They predict at least an order-of-magnitude enhancement in optical brightness in rhombohedral BN, attributed to reduced crystal-field symmetry, while maintaining comparable or improved spin properties. The analysis further suggests room-temperature single-spin coherent control is feasible in the rhombohedral host. The work highlights layer stacking as a practical knob for defect-property engineering in 2D materials.
platform_or_system: VB- defects in rhombohedral boron nitride
main_result: Rhombohedral BN is predicted to boost VB- optical emission by at least 10x while preserving or improving spin performance relative to hexagonal BN.
application_angle: Brighter defect emission directly helps scalable readout and sensitivity in integrated 2D quantum sensors and single-photon photonic components.

title: A Framework for Closed-Loop Robotic Assembly, Alignment and Self-Recovery of Precision Optical Systems
abstract: Robotic automation has transformed scientific workflows in domains such as chemistry and materials science, yet free-space optics, which is a high precision domain, remains largely manual. Optical systems impose strict spatial and angular tolerances, and their performance is governed by tightly coupled physical parameters, making generalizable automation particularly challenging. In this work, we present a robotics framework for the autonomous construction, alignment, and maintenance of precision optical systems. Our approach integrates hierarchical computer vision systems, optimization routines, and custom-built tools to achieve this functionality. As a representative demonstration, we perform the fully autonomous construction of a tabletop laser cavity from randomly distributed components. The system performs several tasks such as laser beam centering, spatial alignment of multiple beams, resonator alignment, laser mode selection, and self-recovery from induced misalignment and disturbances. By achieving closed-loop autonomy for highly sensitive optical systems, this work establishes a foundation for autonomous optical experiments for applications across technical domains.
one_liner: Demonstrates closed-loop robotic construction, alignment, and recovery of a precision free-space laser cavity from loose parts.
why_it_matters: Automating alignment-intensive optics could materially improve uptime, reproducibility, and deployment of quantum communication and photonics labs.
detailed_summary: The paper presents a robotics framework combining hierarchical computer vision, optimization, and custom tools for autonomous optical assembly and maintenance. As a representative experiment, the system builds a tabletop laser cavity from randomly distributed components and performs beam centering, multi-beam spatial alignment, resonator tuning, mode selection, and recovery from induced misalignment. The emphasis is on maintaining closed-loop operation under tight spatial and angular tolerances. This establishes an automation stack for precision optical subsystems rather than a one-off scripted demo.
platform_or_system: Autonomous robotic free-space optical assembly platform
main_result: The framework autonomously assembles and aligns a tabletop laser cavity and can self-recover after disturbances using closed-loop visual and optimization feedback.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to scalable assembly and maintenance of quantum-optical communication, sensing, and photonics hardware.

title: Optimal Compilation of Syndrome Extraction Circuits for General Quantum LDPC Codes
abstract: Quantum error correcting codes (QECC) are essential for constructing large-scale quantum computers that deliver faithful results. As strong competitors to the conventional surface code, quantum low-density parity-check (qLDPC) codes are emerging rapidly: they offer high encoding rates while maintaining reasonable physical-qubit connectivity requirements. Despite the existence of numerous code constructions, a notable gap persists between these designs -- some of which remain purely theoretical -- and their circuit-level deployment. In this work, we propose Auto-Stabilizer-Check (ASC), a universal compilation framework that generates depth-optimal syndrome extraction circuits for arbitrary qLDPC codes. ASC leverages the sparsity of parity-check matrices and exploits the commutativity of X and Z stabilizer measurement subroutines to search for optimal compilation schemes. By iteratively invoking an SMT solver, ASC returns a depth-optimal solution if a satisfying assignment is found, and a near-optimal solution in cases of solver timeouts. Notably, ASC provides the first definitive answer to one of IBM's open problems: for all instances of bivariate bicycle (BB) code reported in their work, our compiler certifies that no depth-6 syndrome extraction circuit exists. Furthermore, by integrating ASC with an end-to-end evaluation framework -- one that assesses different compilation settings under a circuit-level noise model -- ASC reduces circuit depth by approximately 50% and achieves an average 7x-8x suppression of the logical error rate for general qLDPC codes, compared with as-soon-as-possible (ASAP) and coloration-based scheduling. ASC thus substantially reduces manual design overhead and demonstrates its strong potential to serve as a key component in accelerating hardware deployment of qLDPC codes.
one_liner: Introduces an SMT-based compiler that finds depth-optimal syndrome-extraction circuits for arbitrary qLDPC codes.
why_it_matters: Shorter extraction circuits reduce accumulated noise and make high-rate qLDPC codes more plausible on real hardware.
detailed_summary: ASC is a universal compilation framework that exploits parity-check sparsity and commuting X/Z stabilizer structure to search for optimal syndrome-extraction schedules. By iteratively invoking an SMT solver, it returns depth-optimal solutions when satisfiable and near-optimal ones under timeout conditions. The authors report about 50% circuit-depth reduction and roughly 7x-8x logical-error suppression versus ASAP and coloration-based scheduling in circuit-level noise studies. The tool also certifies that depth-6 extraction circuits do not exist for the reported IBM bivariate bicycle code instances.
main_result: ASC generates optimal or near-optimal extraction schedules that substantially cut depth and improve logical performance for broad classes of qLDPC codes.
application_angle: It reduces manual compilation effort and improves the hardware viability of high-rate fault-tolerant quantum processors.

title: High-yield integration design of fixed-frequency superconducting qubit systems using siZZle-CZ gates
abstract: Fixed-frequency transmon qubits, characterized by simple architectures and long coherence times, are promising platforms for large-scale quantum computing. However, the rapidly increasing frequency collisions, which directly reduce the fabrication yield, hinder scaling, especially in cross-resonance (CR) gate-based architectures, wherein the restricted drive frequency severely limits the available design space. We investigate the Stark-induced ZZ by level excursions (siZZle) gate, which relaxes this limitation by allowing arbitrary drive-frequency choices. Extensive numerical analyses across a broad parameter range -- including the far-detuned regime that has received negligible prior attention -- reveal wide operating windows that support controlled-Z (CZ) fidelities >99.6%. Leveraging these windows, we design lattice architectures containing >1000 qubits, showing that even under 0.25% fabrication-induced frequency dispersion, the zero-collision yields in square and heavy-hexagonal lattices reach 80% and 100%, respectively. Thus, the siZZle-CZ gate is a scalable and collision-robust alternative to the CR gate, offering a viable route toward high-yield fixed-frequency transmon quantum processors.
one_liner: Designs high-yield fixed-frequency transmon lattices using siZZle-CZ gates with wide collision-robust operating windows.
why_it_matters: Frequency-collision tolerance is a central manufacturing bottleneck, so improved yield directly affects scalability of superconducting quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The work analyzes Stark-induced ZZ by level excursions (siZZle) CZ gates for fixed-frequency transmons across a broad parameter space, including far-detuned regimes. It identifies wide operating windows supporting CZ fidelities above 99.6% while allowing arbitrary drive-frequency choices, relaxing constraints that hurt cross-resonance architectures. Using those windows, the authors design lattices with more than 1000 qubits and estimate zero-collision yields of 80% for square lattices and 100% for heavy-hex layouts under 0.25% fabrication-induced frequency dispersion. The result frames siZZle-CZ as a scalable alternative for dense transmon integration.
main_result: siZZle-CZ gate design enables >99.6% simulated fidelities and very high zero-collision yield in large fixed-frequency transmon arrays.
application_angle: The method addresses fabrication-yield constraints that limit scale-up of superconducting quantum hardware in production-like settings.

title: Resonance-Suppression Principle for Prethermalization beyond Periodic Driving
abstract: Non-equilibrium dynamics of strongly and rapidly driven quantum many-body systems is poorly understood beyond periodic driving, where heating is exponentially slow in the drive frequency (Floquet Prethermalization). In contrast, non-periodic drives were found to exhibit widely different heating scalings with no unifying principle. This work identifies a resonance-suppression principle governing slow heating up to a prethermal lifetime $τ_*$: When the drive's spectral arithmetic structure restricts multiphoton resonances, $τ_*$ is controlled by low-frequency spectral suppression. The principle distinguishes (i) Single-photon suppression, quantified by a low-frequency suppression law $f(Ω)$ for the drive's Fourier Transform weight near $Ω=0$, from (ii) Multi-photon suppression, where nested commutators remain controlled if exceptional arithmetic structure satisfies a subadditive property. Remarkably, if multi-photon suppression holds, $τ_*$ scaling with drive speed $λ$ is governed by $f(Ω)$. This law of $τ_*$ is found through a small-divisor mechanism in this work's iterative rotating frame scheme. Multi-photon suppression breakdown separates $λ$-scaling of $τ_*$ in linear response and non-perturbative theory, shown by a case study of Quasi-Floquet driving. The principle is applied to (i) Resolve inconsistencies in literature on non-periodic driving, and (ii) Provide design principles for engineering prethermal phases of matter in programmable quantum simulators, exemplified by new non-periodic `Factorial' drives with tunable $τ_*$.
one_liner: This work gives a unifying resonance-suppression rule for extending prethermal lifetimes under non-periodic fast driving.
why_it_matters: It turns scattered heating results for aperiodic drives into actionable design rules for longer-lived driven quantum phases in programmable platforms.
detailed_summary: The paper identifies a resonance-suppression principle that controls slow heating in strongly driven many-body systems beyond standard Floquet settings. It separates single-photon suppression, set by the low-frequency Fourier weight near zero frequency, from multi-photon suppression, governed by arithmetic constraints that keep nested commutators under control. Using an iterative rotating-frame construction, the authors connect the prethermal lifetime to a small-divisor mechanism and show when linear-response and nonperturbative lifetime scalings diverge. They also introduce non-periodic factorial drives as a concrete route to engineer tunable long-lived prethermal regimes.
main_result: The authors derive a general criterion showing that prethermal lifetime under non-periodic driving is set by low-frequency spectral suppression when multiphoton resonances are sufficiently constrained.
application_angle: The framework provides drive-design rules for programmable quantum simulators seeking long coherent nonequilibrium phases without relying on strict periodic modulation.

title: PRISM: Breaking the O(n) Memory Wall in Long-Context LLM Inference via O(1) Photonic Block Selection
abstract: Long-context LLM inference is bottlenecked not by compute but by the O(n) memory bandwidth cost of scanning the KV cache at every decode step -- a wall that no amount of arithmetic scaling can break. Recent photonic accelerators have demonstrated impressive throughput for dense attention computation; however, these approaches inherit the same O(n) memory scaling as electronic attention when applied to long contexts. We observe that the real leverage point is the coarse block-selection step: a memory-bound similarity search that determines which KV blocks to fetch. We identify, for the first time, that this task is structurally matched to the photonic broadcast-and-weight paradigm -- the query fans out to all candidates via passive splitting, signatures are quasi-static (matching electro-optic MRR programming), and only rank order matters (relaxing precision to 4-6 bits). Crucially, the photonic advantage grows with context length: as N increases, the electronic scan cost rises linearly while the photonic evaluation remains O(1). We instantiate this insight in PRISM (Photonic Ranking via Inner-product Similarity with Microring weights), a thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) similarity engine. Hardware-impaired needle-in-a-haystack evaluation on Qwen2.5-7B confirms 100% accuracy from 4K through 64K tokens at k=32, with 16x traffic reduction at 64K context. PRISM achieves a four-order-of-magnitude energy advantage over GPU baselines at practical context lengths (n >= 4K).
one_liner: PRISM uses microring photonics to perform O(1) block ranking for long-context LLM inference instead of O(n) cache scans.
why_it_matters: It targets the true long-context bottleneck, memory traffic, with a photonic primitive that scales favorably as context grows and sharply cuts energy use.
detailed_summary: The authors map coarse KV-block selection for sparse attention onto a photonic broadcast-and-weight architecture implemented with thin-film lithium niobate microring weights. In this scheme, the query is fanned out optically to all candidate blocks, and low-precision analog ranking is sufficient because only top-k ordering matters. Hardware-impaired evaluations on Qwen2.5-7B show 100% needle-in-a-haystack accuracy from 4K to 64K tokens at k=32, alongside a 16x reduction in traffic at 64K context. The analysis claims the photonic selector keeps ranking cost effectively constant with context length while offering orders-of-magnitude energy advantage over GPU baselines.
main_result: PRISM demonstrates accurate top-k KV block selection up to 64K-token contexts with constant-time photonic ranking and a claimed four-order-of-magnitude energy advantage versus GPUs.
application_angle: It points to a practical photonic accelerator role in AI systems by offloading memory-bound retrieval primitives that dominate long-context inference efficiency.

title: Systematic construction of digital autonomous quantum error correction for state preparation and error suppression via conditional Gaussian operations
abstract: In continuous-variable quantum computing, autonomous quantum error correction (QEC) can dissipatively steer a noisy quantum state into a target state or manifold, enabling robust quantum information processing without explicit syndrome measurements and feedback. Here, we propose a nullifier-based digital autonomous QEC enabled by conditional Gaussian operations. By designing jump operators for target nullifiers and compiling the resulting Lindbladian into a Trotterized sequence of elementary conditional Gaussian operations, we demonstrate two use cases: (i) deterministic preparation of non-Gaussian resource states for universal computation, including finitely squeezed cubic phase states and approximate trisqueezed states, and (ii) autonomous suppression of dephasing error for cat and squeezed cat states. We provide explicit gate decompositions for the required conditional Gaussian operations and numerically evaluate the performance under realistic imperfections, including photon loss in the bosonic mode and ancillary-qubit decoherence. Our results clarify the resource requirements and trade-offs, such as circuit depth, time-step choices, and the required set of conditional Gaussian operations, for scalable, gate-level implementations of autonomous state preparation and error suppression.
one_liner: A digital autonomous QEC scheme uses conditional Gaussian operations to prepare non-Gaussian CV states and suppress bosonic dephasing.
why_it_matters: It translates autonomous error correction into gate-level operations compatible with realistic continuous-variable hardware constraints and resource tradeoffs.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates nullifier-based autonomous QEC for continuous-variable systems by designing jump operators and compiling the induced Lindbladian into Trotterized conditional Gaussian gate sequences. Two use cases are studied: dissipative preparation of non-Gaussian resource states such as finitely squeezed cubic-phase and approximate trisqueezed states, and autonomous dephasing suppression for cat and squeezed-cat encodings. The authors provide explicit decompositions for the required conditional Gaussian primitives and numerically analyze imperfections including bosonic photon loss and ancilla-qubit decoherence. The results expose concrete tradeoffs among timestep size, circuit depth, and operation set needed for scalable implementations.
platform_or_system: Bosonic continuous-variable mode with ancilla qubit
main_result: The work shows that digital sequences of conditional Gaussian operations can realize autonomous state preparation and error suppression for useful non-Gaussian bosonic states under realistic noise models.
application_angle: It offers a hardware-oriented route to stabilizing bosonic resources for fault-tolerant continuous-variable computing without measurement-based feedback loops.

title: Neural network approach to mitigating intra-gate crosstalk in superconducting CZ gates
abstract: The potential of quantum computing is fundamentally constrained by the inherent susceptibility of qubits to noise and crosstalk, particularly during multi-qubit gate operations. Existing strategies, such as hardware isolation and dynamical decoupling, face limitations in scalability, experimental feasibility, and robustness against complex noise sources. In this manuscript, we propose a physics-guided neural control (PGNC) framework to generate robust control pulses for superconducting transmon qubit systems, specifically targeting crosstalk mitigation. By combining a hardware aware parameterization with a Hamiltonian-informed objective that accounts for condition-dependent crosstalk distortions, PGNC steers the search toward smooth and physically realizable pulses while efficiently exploring high dimensional control landscapes. Numerical simulations for the CZ gate demonstrate superior fidelity and pulse smoothness compared to a Krotov baseline under matched constraints. Taken together, the results show consistent and practically meaningful improvements in both nominal and perturbed conditions, with pronounced gains in worst-case fidelity, supporting PGNC as a viable route to robust control on near-term transmon devices.
one_liner: A physics-guided neural controller designs smoother, more robust transmon CZ pulses that mitigate intra-gate crosstalk.
why_it_matters: Improving worst-case two-qubit gate fidelity under realistic crosstalk directly addresses a major scaling bottleneck in superconducting processors.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a physics-guided neural control framework for superconducting transmons that embeds hardware-aware pulse parameterization and Hamiltonian-based objectives into the optimization loop. The method explicitly models condition-dependent crosstalk distortions during CZ gates, steering the search toward experimentally plausible smooth pulses rather than unconstrained controls. In simulation, the learned controls outperform a Krotov baseline under matched constraints in nominal, perturbed, and worst-case scenarios. The improvement is especially notable for robustness metrics relevant to calibrating gates in crowded multi-qubit devices.
main_result: Numerical results show the neural control framework delivers higher-fidelity and smoother CZ pulses than a standard optimal-control baseline when intra-gate crosstalk is present.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to calibration stacks for near-term superconducting hardware where robust entangling gates are limited by cross-coupling and control complexity.

title: Gate-based Readout and Cooling of Neutral Atoms
abstract: Neutral atom arrays have seen tremendous progress in quantum simulation, quantum metrology, and fault-tolerant quantum computing. However, hardware constraints such as atom loss and heating remain significant challenges. In this work, we introduce a comprehensive ancilla-based toolbox for optical tweezer experiments that utilizes high-fidelity Rydberg entangling gates and ancilla atoms to mitigate these physical limitations. First, we demonstrate repeated ancilla-based atom readout, achieving improved detection fidelity over multiple rounds with minimal perturbation to data atoms. Second, leveraging the quantized motional states in tweezer-trapped strontium atoms, we transduce quantum information from the electronic to the motional manifold. This enables us to perform mid-circuit ancilla-based atom loss detection in a coherence-preserving fashion. Finally, we demonstrate algorithmic cooling, a circuit-based sequence that deterministically cools data atoms by transferring their motional entropy to the electronic states of ancilla atoms. We observe a marked reduction in the atomic temperature of data atoms. These tools offer a pathway to continuous operation in tweezer clocks and complement recent developments in continuous reloading experiments.
one_liner: Ancilla-assisted Rydberg gates enable repeated readout, coherence-preserving loss detection, and algorithmic cooling in neutral-atom tweezers.
why_it_matters: It addresses atom loss and heating with gate-based tools that support continuous operation and more fault-tolerant neutral-atom architectures.
detailed_summary: This work develops an ancilla-based toolbox for strontium optical tweezers using high-fidelity Rydberg entangling gates. The authors demonstrate repeated ancilla-mediated atom readout with improved cumulative detection fidelity and limited disturbance to data atoms. They also map internal-state information into quantized motional states to perform mid-circuit atom-loss detection without destroying coherence, and then use ancilla atoms to remove motional entropy via algorithmic cooling. Experiments show a measurable temperature reduction in the data atoms, indicating active mitigation of heating inside the computational array.
platform_or_system: Strontium optical tweezers with Rydberg ancillas
main_result: The paper experimentally demonstrates gate-based readout, coherence-preserving loss detection, and deterministic algorithmic cooling in a neutral-atom tweezer platform.
application_angle: These capabilities are practical building blocks for continuously operated tweezer clocks and scalable neutral-atom processors that must manage loss and motional heating in situ.

title: Quantitative Dynamic Phase Mapping via Single-Arm Field-Correlation Ghost Imaging
abstract: We demonstrate a single-arm optical platform for phase-retrieval-free, quantitative dynamic phase mapping of continuous transparent media via field-correlation ghost imaging. By modeling the medium as a dynamic pure-phase object, we spatially encode and compress its two-dimensional (2D) complex transmittance into a single bucket detector. Balanced heterodyne detection downconverts the optical frequencies for direct digitization. Crucially, by mapping spatial information into the temporal domain, this single-pixel architecture exploits high-speed digitization to continuously resolve 2D phase dynamics, effectively bypassing the frame-rate bottlenecks of traditional array sensors. Coupled with intermediate-frequency spectral analysis, this establishes a direct linear mapping from the recorded signal to the physical phase. The complex amplitude is thus deterministically extracted via field-correlation, enabling the spatial reconstruction of 2D acoustic pressure distributions using a pseudo-inverse algorithm. Experimental validations in an acoustic levitator confirm that the optically extracted acoustic wavelengths strictly match theoretical dispersion models, exhibiting a robust linear correlation between the retrieved phase shift and local sound pressure levels. This deterministic methodology provides a real-time-capable metrological tool for characterizing rapidly evolving phenomena, including transient aeroacoustic flows, shockwaves, and microfluidic biological dynamics.
one_liner: Single-arm field-correlation ghost imaging enables quantitative real-time phase mapping of dynamic transparent media with one pixel.
why_it_matters: It replaces camera frame-rate limits with high-speed temporal encoding, opening a practical route to fast optical phase metrology in harsh dynamic settings.
detailed_summary: The authors build a single-arm ghost-imaging platform that reconstructs the complex transmittance of a dynamic pure-phase object without iterative phase retrieval. Spatial information is encoded into the temporal domain and read out through balanced heterodyne detection, allowing direct digitization and intermediate-frequency spectral analysis. A deterministic linear inversion recovers the phase map, and experiments on an acoustic levitator reconstruct two-dimensional acoustic pressure distributions from the retrieved optical phase. The measured acoustic wavelengths match dispersion predictions, and the recovered phase shifts scale linearly with local sound pressure.
main_result: The system quantitatively reconstructs dynamic 2D phase fields and corresponding acoustic pressure distributions using single-pixel field-correlation measurements.
application_angle: It is well suited for fast metrology of transient flows, shockwaves, and microfluidic or biological dynamics where conventional image sensors are too slow.

title: All-optical quantum memory using bosonic quantum error correction codes
abstract: Reliable quantum memory is essential for scalable quantum networks and fault-tolerant photonic quantum computing. We present a quantitative analysis of an all-optical quantum memory architecture in which a Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) encoded qubit is stored in a fibre loop and periodically stabilized using teleportation-based error correction. By modelling fibre propagation as a pure-loss channel and representing each correction round as an effective logical map acting on the Bloch vector, we obtain a compact description of the full multi-round memory channel. We show that syndrome decoder optimization plays a crucial role in the experimentally relevant finite-squeezing regime. The optimal decoder deviates from standard square-grid GKP decoder in both tile-size and tile-shape, leading to significant improved logical performance. Using this optimized decoding strategy, we identify a squeezing-dependent optimal spacing between correction nodes that maximizes the memory lifetime. Remarkably, this optimal segment length is largely independent of the desired storage time, providing a simple and practical design rule for fibre-loop quantum memory. We further find a squeezing threshold of approximately 6.7 dB below which intermediate error correction becomes counterproductive, while above threshold the achievable storage time increases approximately exponentially with squeezing. For example, at 17 dB squeezing, storage times exceeding 400 ms can be achieved with logical infidelity below 1%. These results establish clear performance benchmarks and reveal the fundamental trade-off between photon loss, squeezing, and correction frequency in continuous-variable architectures. Our findings provide actionable design principles for near-term photonic quantum memory and clarify the path toward scalable all-optical fault-tolerant quantum storage.
one_liner: An all-optical GKP memory in fiber loops is quantitatively optimized for long-lived storage via teleportation-based error correction.
why_it_matters: It gives concrete design rules for photonic quantum memory, including squeezing and repeater-spacing thresholds relevant to near-term quantum networks.
detailed_summary: The paper models a fiber-loop GKP memory with periodic teleportation-based correction as a multi-round logical channel acting on the encoded Bloch vector. In the finite-squeezing regime, the authors show that decoder optimization beyond the standard square-grid GKP rule materially improves logical performance, including nontrivial tile size and shape. They identify an optimal spacing between correction nodes that is largely independent of target storage time, and a threshold near 6.7 dB squeezing below which intermediate correction hurts. At 17 dB squeezing, the analysis predicts storage beyond 400 ms with logical infidelity below 1%.
main_result: The work derives squeezing-dependent optimal correction spacing and predicts >400 ms storage with <1% logical infidelity at 17 dB squeezing.
application_angle: These benchmarks translate directly into engineering rules for deployable photonic memories in quantum repeaters and fault-tolerant optical processors.

title: Highly-efficient, narrow-linewidth Brillouin microlasers implemented in compact thin-film lithium niobate microresonators
abstract: Stimulated Brillouin microlasers offer chip-scale light sources with high spectral purity and low phase noise--key attributes for applications spanning precision metrology, quantum technologies, and coherent information processing. However, simultaneously bringing both pump and scattered waves into resonance often compromises photon confinement or modal volume, resulting in limited conversion efficiency and elevated thresholds. In this work, a novel approach is proposed to generate Brillouin microlasers with high efficiency, low threshold, and narrow linewidth, by combining a cross-polarized stimulated Brillouin scattering scheme with intentional Stokes mode splitting to compensate for mode detuning. Triple-resonance and phase-matching conditions are simultaneously achieved in a 114-um-diameter thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) microresonator, enabling precise alignment with both the ~10-GHz Brillouin shift and the ~100-MHz narrow gain bandwidth. The resulting Brillouin microlaser achieves a narrow intrinsic linewidth of 2.88 Hz, a short-term integral linewidth of 185 Hz, an on-chip conversion efficiency of 57.92%, and a pump threshold as low as 1.03 mW. Both the conversion efficiency and the lasing threshold represent record-high performance for the TFLN platform to date.
one_liner: Thin-film lithium niobate microresonators deliver Brillouin microlasing with hertz-level linewidth, low threshold, and record efficiency.
why_it_matters: Compact ultra-pure chip lasers are enabling components for precision photonics, quantum control, and coherent signal processing.
detailed_summary: The authors engineer a cross-polarized stimulated Brillouin scattering scheme with intentional Stokes-mode splitting to satisfy triple resonance and phase matching in a 114 um TFLN resonator. This aligns both the approximately 10 GHz Brillouin shift and the approximately 100 MHz gain bandwidth without sacrificing confinement. The resulting device shows 2.88 Hz intrinsic linewidth, 185 Hz short-term integral linewidth, 57.92% on-chip conversion efficiency, and 1.03 mW threshold. Both efficiency and threshold are reported as record performance for the TFLN Brillouin platform.
main_result: The device achieves simultaneous narrow linewidth, 57.92% conversion efficiency, and 1.03 mW threshold in a compact thin-film lithium niobate microlaser.
application_angle: Such sources can serve as integrated low-noise lasers for metrology, coherent links, and photonic quantum systems requiring stable narrowband light.

title: Neural Belief-Matching Decoding for Topological Quantum Error Correction Codes
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC) is critical for scalable fault-tolerant quantum computing. Topological codes, such as the toric code, offer hardware-efficient architectures but their Tanner graphs contain many girth-4 cycles that degrade the performance of belief-propagation (BP) decoding. For this reason, BP decoding is typically followed by a more complex second stage decoder such as minimum-weight perfect matching. These combined decoders achieve a remarkable performance, albeit at the cost of increased complexity. In this paper we propose two key improvements for the decoding of toric code. The first one is replacing the BP decoder by a neural BP decoder, giving rise to the neural belief-matching decoder which substantially decreases the average decoding complexity. The main drawback of this approach is the high cost associated with the training of the neural BP decoder. To address this issue, we impose a convolutional architecture on the neural BP decoder, enabling weight sharing across the spatially homogeneous structure of the code's factor graph. This design allows a model trained on a modest-size topological code to be directly transferred to much larger instances, preserving decoding quality while dramatically lowering the training burden. Our numerical experiments on toric-code lattices of various sizes demonstrate that this technique does not result in a noticeable loss in performance.
one_liner: A convolutional neural belief-matching decoder cuts toric-code decoding complexity while transferring across larger lattices.
why_it_matters: Lower-complexity scalable decoding is essential for practical fault-tolerant hardware where classical processing can bottleneck QEC cycles.
detailed_summary: The work addresses the failure modes of standard belief propagation on toric-code Tanner graphs with many short cycles by replacing BP with a neural BP stage. This neural belief-matching decoder preserves the matching-based correction structure while reducing average decoding complexity. A convolutional architecture exploits spatial homogeneity so weights learned on modest code sizes can be reused on much larger lattices. Numerical results indicate this transfer incurs little to no observable performance loss.
main_result: The proposed decoder maintains toric-code decoding quality while substantially reducing average complexity and enabling training transfer across code sizes.
application_angle: It makes real-time QEC more tractable for scalable quantum processors by reducing classical overhead without requiring size-specific retraining.

title: Hyperspectral imaging solutions for brain tissue metabolic and haemodynamic monitoring: an updated perspective
abstract: Since the publication of our review article Hyperspectral imaging solutions for brain tissue metabolic and hemodynamic monitoring: past, current and future developments in 2018, the technological and applicational landscape of the use of hyperspectral imaging (HSI) in brain sciences has evolved and transformed significantly. The number of studies and works where HSI has been deployed in its many forms to map and monitor the haemodynamic and metabolic states of cerebral tissues have grown exponentially, to such a point where an update on the cur-rent state of the art is timely, and we believe would be desirable for both long-term experts in the field, as well as for any new researcher approaching it for the first time. In this commentary, we provide a renewed perspective on the newest and latest developments in brain haemodynamic and metabolic monitoring with HSI over the past eight years. Our hope is that even greater breakthroughs and broader, more numerous novel applications will come forward in the future for the technology, that may benefit from this new overview, as they did from the original one.
one_liner: An updated perspective surveys how hyperspectral imaging is advancing brain metabolic and hemodynamic monitoring.
why_it_matters: It consolidates a fast-growing sensing field with direct relevance to noninvasive brain monitoring and intraoperative decision support.
detailed_summary: This commentary updates the 2018 review of hyperspectral imaging for cerebral metabolic and hemodynamic assessment, focusing on developments over the last eight years. It maps the expanding range of HSI implementations used to monitor oxygenation, perfusion, and metabolic state in brain tissue. Rather than introducing a single new device, it synthesizes how the technical landscape and application space have evolved. The paper positions HSI as an increasingly mature optical sensing modality for brain science and clinical translation.
main_result: The article shows that HSI-based brain monitoring has expanded rapidly in both technological approaches and biomedical applications since 2018.
application_angle: The synthesis helps guide development of clinically relevant optical monitoring tools for neuroscience, surgery, and bedside cerebral assessment.

title: Temporal analysis and control of Raman scattering dynamics
abstract: Raman scattering underlies a broad range of spectroscopic and light-generation techniques, yet its conventional description, based on the Raman gain spectrum, accurately describes only long-pulse, steady-state dynamics. We present and develop a time-domain theoretical approach that provides a unified and physically-transparent description of Raman interactions across all temporal regimes. It enables direct visualization of Raman temporal dynamics and accounts for spectrotemporal aspects of Raman phenomena. We apply this theory specifically to Raman-shifting with ultrashort light pulses in gases, where the excitation is in the impulsive Raman regime and dephasing of Raman transitions is weak. The analysis, for the first time, exposes temporal and spectral distortions that arise from Raman scattering and which impact frequency-shifting performance detrimentally. Crucially, it also identifies how these distortions can be suppressed through temporal control of the nonlinear response. Numerical simulations of the soliton self-frequency shift (SSFS) in gas-filled hollow-core fibers show that molecules with strong Raman responses do not yield efficient frequency conversion, and predict that reducing the relative Raman contribution (compared to the electronic response) enhances the process. Experiments using gas mixtures with tunable Raman fraction of the nonlinear response confirm these predictions. An analytic expression for impulsive SSFS in gases, which departs significantly from the well-known formula for glasses, predicts the observed behavior when Raman-induced temporal distortion is suppressed. The new time-domain framework uncovers phenomena and provides physical insight that are inaccessible through the decades-old frequency-domain treatment of Raman scattering.
one_liner: A time-domain theory of Raman scattering reveals and suppresses distortions that limit ultrafast Raman-based frequency shifting.
why_it_matters: Better control of Raman dynamics can improve nonlinear photonic frequency conversion, ultrafast sources, and spectroscopic performance.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a unified time-domain framework for Raman interactions that remains valid from steady-state to impulsive regimes and makes the temporal dynamics directly visible. Applied to ultrashort-pulse Raman shifting in gases, it identifies temporal and spectral distortions that degrade frequency conversion and shows how temporal control can suppress them. Simulations of soliton self-frequency shift in gas-filled hollow-core fibers predict that reducing the Raman fraction relative to the electronic response improves conversion efficiency. Experiments with tunable gas mixtures confirm these predictions and support a new analytic formula for impulsive SSFS in gases.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that controlling the temporal Raman response suppresses distortion and improves ultrafast Raman frequency shifting in gases.
application_angle: This provides a practical route to cleaner and more efficient tunable ultrafast light sources and Raman-enabled photonic devices.

title: Nonlinear Electro-Optic Visible Photonic Circuits for Solid-State Quantum Defects
abstract: Integrated visible photonic engines for solid-state quantum defects provide a foundation for scalable quantum networks. While miniaturization is advancing, active manipulation remains limited by the difficulty of achieving simultaneous milliwatt-scale visible light generation and high-contrast modulation. Despite extensive efforts, the concurrent chip-scale realization of nonlinear frequency conversion and fast temporal gating for high-fidelity quantum control has remained elusive. Here, we demonstrate a monolithic thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) platform integrating periodically poled frequency conversion with GHz-bandwidth electro-optic (EO) switching. The device delivers off-chip green-light power exceeding 1 mW with an extinction ratio (ER) of 42.2 dB, enabling coherent spin control and time-resolved lifetime measurements of individual nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond through nanosecond gating. System performance is validated through pulsed optically detected magnetic resonance (ODMR), Rabi oscillations, and Ramsey interference, supported by time-tagged photon counting with nanosecond resolution. By unifying sufficient nonlinear light generation with high-speed active manipulation, this platform establishes a scalable framework for the realization of high-rate quantum communication nodes.
one_liner: Monolithic visible TFLN circuits combine green-light generation and GHz electro-optic gating for controlling single NV centers.
why_it_matters: Integrated visible nonlinear and fast-switching photonics is a key missing piece for scalable solid-state quantum network nodes.
detailed_summary: The authors integrate periodically poled nonlinear conversion and GHz-bandwidth electro-optic switching on a monolithic thin-film lithium niobate platform operating in the visible. The chip produces more than 1 mW off-chip green power with 42.2 dB extinction ratio, sufficient for coherent control and nanosecond-gated readout of single diamond NV centers. They validate system performance using pulsed ODMR, Rabi oscillations, Ramsey interference, and time-tagged photon counting with nanosecond resolution. The work closes a key gap between on-chip light generation and active temporal control for defect-based quantum photonics.
platform_or_system: monolithic TFLN visible photonic circuit with NV centers
main_result: The platform simultaneously delivers milliwatt-scale green output and high-contrast GHz electro-optic gating for coherent single-NV experiments.
application_angle: It is directly relevant to compact quantum repeater and sensor nodes that need integrated excitation, modulation, and readout around solid-state defects.

title: Global Optimization for Parametrized Quantum Circuits
abstract: In the absence of error correction, noisy intermediate-scale quantum devices are operated by training parametrized quantum circuits (PQCs) so as to minimize a suitable loss function. Finding the optimal parameters of those circuits is a hard optimization problem, where global guarantees are known only for highly structured cases of limited practical relevance, and first-order methods can fail to find even local minima due to the presence of barren plateaus. In this work, we study the training of practical classes of PQCs, namely polynomial-depth circuits with a constant number of trainable parameters. This captures widely used PQC families, including fixed-depth QAOA, hardware-efficient ansätze, and Fixed Parameter Count QAOA. Our main technical result is a fully polynomial randomized approximation scheme (FPRAS), which, for every $ε>0$, returns an $ε$-approximate solution to the problem's global optimum with high probability, and has runtime and query complexity polynomial in $1/ε$ and the number of qubits. Unlike the standard hybrid quantum-classical training loop in variational algorithms, where the quantum device is queried repeatedly throughout the training, our approach separates the computation into two distinct stages: (1) an initial quantum data-acquisition phase, followed by (2) a classical global-optimization phase based on the trigonometric moment/sum-of-squares hierarchies. Under a standard flat-extension condition, which can be checked numerically, the method also supports the extraction of optimal circuit parameters. The existence of an FPRAS implies that the promise problem associated with the optimization of poly-depth constant-parameter PQC is in BQP. This imposes a limitation on the expressive power of the class, namely, it cannot encode combinatorial optimization problems whose objective values are separated by an inverse-polynomial gap.
one_liner: Shows global training of constant-parameter, polynomial-depth PQCs admits an FPRAS with quantum data collection followed by classical optimization.
why_it_matters: It gives rare global guarantees for practical variational circuits and clarifies what classes of optimization tasks such PQCs can and cannot encode.
detailed_summary: The paper studies polynomial-depth parametrized quantum circuits with a constant number of trainable parameters, covering fixed-depth QAOA, hardware-efficient ansatze, and related variational families. Its main result is a fully polynomial randomized approximation scheme that estimates an epsilon-approximate global optimum with runtime and query complexity polynomial in qubit count and 1/epsilon. The method separates an initial quantum measurement stage from a purely classical optimization stage based on trigonometric moment and sum-of-squares hierarchies, and under a numerically checkable flat-extension condition it can also extract optimal parameters. The authors further argue that the associated promise optimization problem lies in BQP, limiting the expressive power of this PQC class for inverse-polynomial-gap combinatorial problems.
main_result: A global epsilon-approximate optimizer for constant-parameter poly-depth PQCs is obtained via an FPRAS with polynomial runtime and query complexity.
application_angle: The result is directly relevant to near-term variational hardware because it suggests a more reliable offline training workflow for widely used low-parameter ansatze.

title: Fiber-optic quantum interface with an array of more than 100 individually addressable atoms on an optical nanofiber
abstract: Integrating the scalability of individually addressable arrays of optical-tweezer-trapped single atoms with the efficient light-matter interface provided by nanophotonic waveguides has been a long-standing challenge in quantum technologies based on atoms and photons. Here we realize a quantum interface between photons guided in an optical nanofiber with a diameter of 310 nm and an array of on average 155 individually addressable atoms. Using a spatial light modulator and an objective lens with NA = 0.45, single cesium atoms are trapped in a one-dimensional array of 200 optical tweezer spots with micrometer-scale trap sizes on the nanofiber. Individual atoms are addressed by spatially scanning an excitation laser beam, focused to a spot size comparable to that of the traps through the same objective lens, along the nanofiber. We confirm the single-atom nature of the individual trapping sites through photon-correlation measurements of the guided fluorescence, observing strong photon antibunching with $g^{(2)}(0) \approx 0.26$. We measure trap lifetimes of a few hundred milliseconds, with a maximum value of 460 ms, at an atom-surface separation of 670 nm without active cooling, representing an order-of-magnitude improvement over previous nanofiber traps. This platform opens a new regime for atom-photon interfaces, paving the way for scalable distributed quantum computing and quantum networks, as well as for the exploration of collective radiative effects in waveguide QED with individually addressable atoms.
one_liner: Demonstrates a nanofiber-coupled array with over 100 individually addressable optical-tweezer-trapped cesium atoms.
why_it_matters: It combines scalable neutral-atom control with efficient fiber-integrated light-matter coupling, a key capability for networked quantum nodes and waveguide QED.
detailed_summary: The authors realize a fiber-coupled atom-photon interface using a 310 nm diameter optical nanofiber and an average of 155 individually addressable cesium atoms trapped in a one-dimensional array of 200 optical tweezer sites. A spatial light modulator and NA = 0.45 objective create micrometer-scale traps near the fiber, while a scanned excitation beam addresses sites one by one. Guided-fluorescence photon-correlation measurements show strong antibunching with g^(2)(0) about 0.26, confirming single-atom occupancy, and the traps reach lifetimes of a few hundred milliseconds with a maximum of 460 ms at 670 nm from the surface without active cooling. This substantially improves nanofiber-trap performance while preserving site-resolved control.
platform_or_system: Cesium atoms trapped near an optical nanofiber
main_result: The work demonstrates an optical nanofiber interface to an individually addressable array averaging 155 single atoms with antibunched guided fluorescence and up to 460 ms trap lifetime.
application_angle: This is a practical architecture for scalable quantum repeater nodes, distributed atom-photon processors, and controlled waveguide-QED experiments.

title: Numerical security framework for quantum key distribution with bypass channels
abstract: Satellite based quantum key distribution (QKD) aims to establish secure key exchange over long distances despite significant technological challenges. To alleviate some of these challenges, Ghalaii et al. [PRX Quantum 4, 040320 (2023)] proposed that any airborne eavesdropper up to a certain size can be detected by classical monitoring techniques, limiting the transmission efficiencies of any undetected Eve. This creates a new QKD scenario in which some of the transmitted signal from Alice to Bob bypasses Eve entirely. In this manuscript, we develop a general framework for computing key rates in this "bypass" scenario for discrete variable protocols. We first numerically support a conjecture that the performance of BB84 with single photons does not improve under bypass constraints, and go on to find new regimes that do. Specifically, we find improvements when the receiver's detectors have an efficiency mismatch and when BB84 is implemented using weak coherent pulses under certain squashing assumptions. Technically, our framework is realized by including marginal constraints on the source to account for bypass effects, combined with existing numerical approaches for minimizing the key rate and squashing and dimension reduction techniques to handle photonic states of unbounded dimension.
one_liner: Develops a numerical security framework for QKD when part of the Alice-to-Bob channel bypasses Eve entirely.
why_it_matters: It extends security analysis to realistic satellite-style geometries and identifies cases where bypass constraints materially improve achievable key rates.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates a general numerical method for discrete-variable QKD in a bypass scenario, where some transmitted light is constrained to avoid the eavesdropper due to classical monitoring of airborne intrusions. The framework incorporates source marginal constraints representing bypass effects and combines them with numerical key-rate minimization, squashing models, and dimension-reduction tools for photonic states of unbounded dimension. The authors numerically support the conjecture that single-photon BB84 does not benefit in this setting, but identify improvements for BB84 with detector-efficiency mismatch and for weak coherent pulse implementations under suitable squashing assumptions. This gives a more realistic security treatment for nonideal channels relevant to free-space and satellite deployments.
main_result: A computable security framework is introduced for bypass-channel QKD, revealing parameter regimes where detector mismatch or weak coherent pulses yield higher key rates.
application_angle: The method can guide protocol design and security margins for satellite and long-distance free-space QKD systems with monitored geometric constraints on Eve.

title: Formation and propagation of stable high-dimensional soliton molecules and breather molecules in a cold Rydberg atomic gas
abstract: We investigate the mechanisms of formation of stable (2+1)-dimensional optical soliton molecules (SMs) and breather molecules (BMs) in a Rydberg atomic gas, highlighting the distinct roles of nonlocality. The underlying giant, nonlocal nonlinearity induced via Rydberg electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT), supports diverse, large-size lattice SMs (rhombic, square, checkerboard, hexagonal lattice SMs). Crucially, we identify two distinct formation regimes: In the nonlocal regime, long-range interactions alone stabilize the SMs without requiring initial motion. In contrast, within the strongly nonlocal regime, an initial velocity is essential to generate a centrifugal force that counteracts the strong attraction, resulting in rotating SMs. Furthermore, specific initial velocities can induce a periodic breathing instability, leading to the formation of BMs. Our study offers a new scheme for engineering SMs with diverse configurations and opens new avenues for data processing and transmission in optical systems.
one_liner: Predicts stable 2+1D soliton and breather molecules in Rydberg-EIT media enabled by giant nonlocal optical nonlinearities.
why_it_matters: It proposes a controllable route to robust multidimensional nonlinear light structures that could support optical information handling in strongly interacting media.
detailed_summary: Using a cold Rydberg atomic gas under electromagnetically induced transparency, the paper analyzes how giant nonlocal nonlinearities support stable high-dimensional optical bound states. In the nonlocal regime, long-range interactions alone stabilize large lattice soliton molecules including rhombic, square, checkerboard, and hexagonal configurations. In the strongly nonlocal regime, finite initial transverse velocity is needed to generate centrifugal balance against attraction, producing rotating soliton molecules, and certain velocities trigger periodic breathing that yields breather molecules. The work maps distinct formation mechanisms and identifies control knobs for engineering stable propagating nonlinear structures.
main_result: The study predicts stable lattice soliton molecules and velocity-controlled breather molecules in a 2+1D Rydberg medium across distinct nonlocality regimes.
application_angle: Such controllable nonlinear photonic structures could be useful for optical routing, reconfigurable information transport, and light-based pattern processing in atomic media.

title: Structured-light propagation in a medium with uniform torsion: polarization textures, geometric birefringence, and beam-resolved optical activity
abstract: We investigate finite-width optical-beam propagation in a medium with uniform torsion described by the geometric theory of a continuous distribution of screw dislocations. Starting from the Riemann--Cartan framework that yields torsion-induced circular birefringence for local plane waves, we construct a minimal paraxial beam model in which the same contortion-driven helicity splitting remains explicit. We show that uniform torsion breaks the degeneracy between the two circular-polarization sectors and induces a geometric rotation of the polarization that scales with both the propagation distance and the radial position in the beam. As a consequence, a finite-width beam develops spatially varying polarization textures across its transverse profile, naturally described by the Stokes parameters. We introduce beam-level observables based on the integrated Stokes vector, the transverse inhomogeneity of the polarization texture, and the number of resolved radial polarization domains, thereby connecting the torsion parameter to experimentally accessible beam diagnostics. The paper combines two complementary levels of description: an analytic short-distance regime, used to isolate the geometric mechanism, and full paraxial propagation including diffraction, used to test the robustness of the predicted textures. Within the cylindrically symmetric minimal model, the most robust structured-light signature of uniform torsion is beam-resolved polarization structuring, whereas strong orbital-angular-momentum conversion is not expected without additional azimuthal structure. We also identify the geometric ingredient required for genuine torsion-assisted spin--orbit conversion beyond the minimal radial model: an effective azimuthal geometric connection.
one_liner: Shows that uniform torsion can imprint measurable beam-resolved polarization textures and geometric birefringence on structured light.
why_it_matters: It translates an abstract geometric-material effect into concrete optical observables that could enable torsion-sensitive photonic diagnostics.
detailed_summary: Starting from a Riemann-Cartan description of screw-dislocation-induced torsion, the authors derive a paraxial beam model in which contortion causes helicity-dependent propagation and circular birefringence. For finite-width beams, the two circular polarization sectors split and produce a polarization rotation that depends on both propagation distance and radial coordinate. The resulting spatially varying polarization textures are characterized through local and integrated Stokes observables, transverse inhomogeneity metrics, and counts of resolved radial polarization domains. Full paraxial simulations indicate that beam-resolved polarization structuring is the most robust signature, while strong OAM conversion would require an added azimuthal geometric connection.
platform_or_system: Structured-light beams in a torsional medium
main_result: Uniform torsion is predicted to generate robust, radially resolved polarization textures and measurable geometric birefringence in propagating finite-width beams.
application_angle: The proposed observables provide a practical photonic route for sensing torsional defects or engineered geometric responses in structured optical media.

title: Hyperloss from coherent spatial-mode mixing in quantum-correlated networks
abstract: Quantum-correlated networks distribute quantum resources such as squeezed and entangled states. These states are central to modern quantum technology, including photonic quantum computing, quantum communications, non-destructive biological sensing and gravitational-wave detection. Even for squeezed states of light - the most robust quantum-correlated resource - loss-induced decoherence remains the dominant obstacle to strong quantum advantage in in large-scale interferometric and networked quantum systems. Common design assumption in these applications is treating mismatches between spatial modes as a small, incoherent loss. Here we show that this picture can fail: coherent spatial-mode mixing with higher-order spatial modes can produce an apparent loss exceeding 100% relative to the initial squeezing, a regime we term hyperloss. We experimentally demonstrate hyperloss in a minimal two-node quantum network: with only 8% mode mismatch, a 5.8dB squeezed state is converted into an effectively thermal state with no quadrature squeezing, eliminating the quantum advantage. Because the effect is coherent, it is controllable: lost correlations can be recovered by tuning differential spatial-mode phases (e.g., Gouy-/propagation-phase). We demonstrate this recovery experimentally, not only eliminating the hyperloss, but even significantly suppressing the mode mismatch loss, with 15% geometric mismatch acting like only ~2.8% effective loss. Hyperloss is a design-limiting mechanism for all quantum networks with squeezed light, from from photonic quantum processors to large-scale interferometers and distributed quantum-sensing networks. Our results provide a practical route to avoid hyperloss and turn mode mismatch into an explicit, phase-aware design parameter for future quantum technologies.
one_liner: Identifies and demonstrates hyperloss, where coherent spatial-mode mixing can destroy squeezing far beyond an equivalent incoherent-loss picture.
why_it_matters: It exposes a hidden systems-level limit for squeezed-light networks while also showing a concrete phase-control method to recover lost quantum correlations.
detailed_summary: The paper shows that coherent mixing between a target squeezed mode and higher-order spatial modes can create an apparent loss exceeding 100 percent of the initial squeezing, a regime the authors call hyperloss. In a two-node experimental network, only 8 percent mode mismatch converts a 5.8 dB squeezed state into an effectively thermal state with no observed quadrature squeezing. Because the effect is coherent rather than purely dissipative, tuning differential spatial-mode phases such as Gouy or propagation phase can recover correlations; experimentally, 15 percent geometric mismatch is reduced to about 2.8 percent effective loss. The work reframes spatial mismatch as a phase-aware design parameter rather than a small scalar loss term.
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrate hyperloss from coherent spatial-mode mixing and recover the lost squeezing by adjusting differential spatial-mode phase.
application_angle: This directly impacts photonic quantum processors, quantum communication links, and distributed sensing systems where maintaining squeezing across imperfect mode-matched networks is critical.

title: Fabrication and study of femtosecond laser micromachined few-mode elliptical core waveguides
abstract: Femtosecond laser micromachining (FLM) fabricated waveguides inherently form elliptical cores due to differences in focal spot size and the Rayleigh range of the microscope objective. Consequently, it is essential to study their propagation characteristics, which differ from those of conventional circular-core waveguides. In this work, we present the results of a parametric optimization of these waveguides to identify fabrication parameters that lead to minimal loss. A propagation loss characterization study revealed that, for a laser wavelength of 1030 nm, a pulse width of $\sim$300 fs, a pulse energy of 600 nJ, a scan speed of 2 mm/s, and a repetition rate of 100 kHz, a transparent and micro-bubble-free waveguide with a propagation loss of $\sim$0.4 dB/cm was formed. The modal analysis further demonstrated that the V-number depends on the core aspect ratio. The waveguide modes were compared with computationally generated modes, revealing a correlation that aligns well with existing literature.
one_liner: Femtosecond-laser-written elliptical few-mode waveguides were optimized to reach about 0.4 dB/cm loss and mode behavior consistent with simulations.
why_it_matters: It gives practical fabrication windows for low-loss few-mode integrated waveguides, useful for compact photonic routing and mode-engineering chips.
detailed_summary: The paper studies femtosecond laser micromachined waveguides whose cores are intrinsically elliptical rather than circular, making their modal properties fabrication-sensitive. By varying writing conditions, the authors identify a parameter set at 1030 nm, ~300 fs, 600 nJ, 2 mm/s, and 100 kHz that yields transparent, micro-bubble-free guides with ~0.4 dB/cm propagation loss. They further show that the waveguide V-number depends on the core aspect ratio and compare measured guided modes against computed mode profiles, finding good agreement.
main_result: An optimized femtosecond-writing recipe produced few-mode elliptical-core waveguides with ~0.4 dB/cm loss and experimentally validated modal behavior.
application_angle: Low-loss few-mode written waveguides are directly relevant to integrated mode multiplexing, photonic interconnects, and custom on-chip quantum/optical circuits.

title: Ultra-high THz-field-confinement at LaAlO3 twin walls
abstract: The control and steering of light at nanometre length scales is crucial for the development of both fundamental science and nanophotonic technologies. Recent advancements have been achieved by exploiting various crystalline anisotropies, allowing for subdiffractional and diffraction-less canalisation of energy. These studies in particular benefit from stacking and twisting of 2D materials, whereas corresponding capabilities of anisotropic bulk crystals are rather unexplored. In this work, we show that ferroelastic twin walls - crystallographically perfect 2D-sheets that separate regions of differently oriented domains - in the distorted perovskite LaAlO3 provide a natural platform for broadband lateral confinement and superb canalisation of light at the nanoscale. Without fabrication processes, the electromagnetic fields localised at such walls exhibit lateral optical sizes up to 260 times smaller than the free-space wavelength. Depending on the adjacent domain orientation and frequency, the twin wall pattern preferentially concentrates or repels the electromagnetic energy, constituting a natural building block towards broadband MIR and THz nanophotonics for polaritonic circuitry.
one_liner: LaAlO3 ferroelastic twin walls confine THz and MIR fields to widths up to 260 times below the free-space wavelength without nanofabrication.
why_it_matters: Natural crystal twin walls offer an ultra-compact route to broadband THz/MIR field guiding and concentration for nanophotonic circuitry and sensing.
detailed_summary: The authors show that ferroelastic twin walls in distorted perovskite LaAlO3 act as crystallographically perfect two-dimensional sheets that laterally confine broadband electromagnetic fields. Depending on domain orientation and frequency, these walls either attract or repel energy, enabling strong canalization without lithographic structuring. The reported lateral mode sizes can be as much as 260 times smaller than the free-space wavelength, positioning twin-wall patterns as natural elements for MIR and THz polaritonic circuitry.
main_result: Ferroelastic twin walls in bulk LaAlO3 were shown to provide broadband MIR/THz confinement and canalization with extreme subwavelength lateral localization.
application_angle: Such fabrication-free confinement could enable compact THz links, near-field sensing, and polaritonic interconnects in integrated communication and spectroscopy hardware.

title: Identical, independent quantum weak measurements violate objective realism
abstract: We demonstrate violation of objective realism in quantum world using unconstrained weak measurements. Instead of limited Leggett-Garg approach with artificial bounds on the observed values, we assume two identical and indepenent weak detectors and final conditioning. The experimental verification has been performed on public quantum computers, IBM and IonQ. Thanks to sufficiently large statistics, the violation is observed at the level of 10 standard deviations. The tests confirmed also high quality of parametric two-qubit gates offered by main quantum hardware providers.
one_liner: Weak-measurement tests run on IBM and IonQ hardware showed a 10-sigma violation of objective realism using two independent detectors and postselection.
why_it_matters: It turns cloud quantum processors into testbeds for foundational protocols while also benchmarking the quality of native two-qubit operations.
detailed_summary: This work formulates an objective-realism test based on two identical, independent weak detectors plus final conditioning, avoiding the bounded-observable assumptions of Leggett-Garg-style approaches. The protocol was implemented experimentally on public IBM and IonQ quantum computers. With sufficient sampling, the measured statistics show a violation at the 10 standard deviation level, while also indicating high-quality parametric two-qubit gate performance on both platforms.
main_result: Public superconducting and trapped-ion quantum hardware was used to demonstrate a statistically strong violation of objective realism via unconstrained weak measurements.
application_angle: The protocol is useful as a hardware-accessible validation benchmark for weak-measurement workflows, gate quality, and nonclassical behavior on real quantum devices.

title: Superbunched random fiber laser
abstract: Photon superbunching, distinguished by second-order coherence values far exceeding the Gaussian thermal limit, represents a highly desirable resource for quantum optics and correlation-based imaging technologies. However, existing approaches typically rely on fragile experimental platforms, inefficient nonlinear conversion processes, or mechanically complex optical architectures. Here, we demonstrate a fully fiber-integrated superbunched random fiber laser (SRFL) in which intrinsic Rayleigh scattering cooperatively interacts with cascaded stimulated Brillouin scattering and quasi-phase-matched four-wave mixing to tailor extreme photon statistics. The SRFL generates a multi-wavelength comb, in which individual spectral components exhibit widely tunable photon bunching, with the second-order coherence g(2)(0) continuously controlled from ~1 to ~26 by tuning the pump power, spectral order and diffusion length. Moreover, we establish a direct correlation between photonic phase transitions (quantified by the Parisi overlap order parameter) and the emergence of superbunching, thereby bridging macroscopic disorder physics and microscopic photon statistics. Finally, we employ the superbunched emission for temporal ghost imaging, realizing high-fidelity temporal object reconstruction with a substantial reduction in required ensemble averaging. These findings validate random fiber lasers as a robust, scalable, and integrated platform for generating extreme photon statistics and unlock new avenues for correlation-enhanced photonic sensing and quantum optics investigations in complex photonic systems.
one_liner: A fully fiber-integrated random fiber laser produced tunable superbunching up to g2(0) about 26 and enabled efficient temporal ghost imaging.
why_it_matters: It provides a robust, scalable source of extreme photon statistics for correlation-based imaging, sensing, and quantum-optics experiments.
detailed_summary: The authors realize a superbunched random fiber laser in which Rayleigh scattering, cascaded stimulated Brillouin scattering, and quasi-phase-matched four-wave mixing jointly shape the photon statistics. Across a multi-wavelength comb, the second-order coherence g(2)(0) is tuned continuously from about 1 to 26 by adjusting pump power, spectral order, and diffusion length. They also relate the onset of superbunching to photonic phase transitions via the Parisi overlap parameter and demonstrate temporal ghost imaging with reduced ensemble averaging.
main_result: The system generated multi-wavelength emission with controllable superbunching up to g(2)(0) ~26 and used it to perform high-fidelity temporal ghost imaging.
application_angle: An all-fiber superbunched source is attractive for deployable correlation imaging, photonic sensing, and integrated quantum-optics setups that need stable nonclassical-like intensity fluctuations.

title: The color code, the surface code, and the transversal CNOT: NP-hardness of minimum-weight decoding
abstract: The decoding problem is a ubiquitous algorithmic task in fault-tolerant quantum computing, and solving it efficiently is essential for scalable quantum computing. Here, we prove that minimum-weight decoding is NP-hard in three quintessential settings: (i) the color code with Pauli $Z$ errors, (ii) the surface code with Pauli $X$, $Y$ and $Z$ errors, and (iii) the surface code with a transversal CNOT gate, Pauli $Z$ and measurement bit-flip errors.
