Our results show that computational intractability already arises in basic and practically relevant decoding problems central to both quantum memories and logical circuit implementations, highlighting a sharp computational complexity separation between minimum-weight decoding and its approximate realizations.
one_liner: Minimum-weight decoding is proven NP-hard for key color-code and surface-code settings, including transversal CNOT with measurement errors.
why_it_matters: It sharply defines the algorithmic limits of exact decoding in fault-tolerant quantum computing and motivates approximate or heuristic decoders.
detailed_summary: The paper proves NP-hardness of minimum-weight decoding in three practical regimes: color code with Pauli Z errors, surface code with Pauli X/Y/Z errors, and surface code with a transversal CNOT under Pauli Z and measurement bit-flip noise. These are canonical decoding settings for both memory protection and logical gate execution. The results establish a formal complexity separation between exact minimum-weight decoding and the approximate decoders used in real fault-tolerant architectures.
platform_or_system: surface and color quantum error-correcting codes
main_result: Exact minimum-weight decoding was shown to be NP-hard in several central code-capacity and circuit-level fault-tolerance scenarios.
application_angle: This matters directly for quantum-hardware control stacks because scalable systems will need fast approximate decoders rather than exact optimal ones.

title: Deterministic feedforward-based generation of large optical coherent-state superposition
abstract: Large optical coherent-state superpositions are essential to advance quantum sensing, quantum repeaters and error-correction codes. We propose a deterministic feedforward protocol employing qubit-mode dispersive coupling, currently available in cavity quantum electrodynamics (QED). We show this single-mode protocol to outperform the advanced three-mode Gaussian-photon-number-resolving detector scheme both in terms of average fidelity and quantum non-Gaussian phase-space properties, and propose sensitivity to weak displacements of interference fringes as a feasible and conclusive witness of quantum interference. This approach combining QED with electro-optical feedforward is extendable to tailored states for applications and other platforms.
one_liner: A deterministic feedforward protocol in cavity QED can generate large optical coherent-state superpositions with better fidelity than leading probabilistic schemes.
why_it_matters: Deterministic cat-state generation is valuable for quantum sensing, repeaters, and bosonic error correction where probabilistic preparation is a bottleneck.
detailed_summary: The work proposes a single-mode deterministic protocol for producing large optical coherent-state superpositions using qubit-mode dispersive coupling and electro-optical feedforward in cavity QED. Compared with an advanced three-mode Gaussian plus photon-number-resolving detection scheme, it yields higher average fidelity and stronger quantum non-Gaussian phase-space signatures. The authors also suggest using weak-displacement sensitivity of interference fringes as an experimentally feasible witness of the generated quantum interference.
main_result: A deterministic feedforward-based cavity-QED scheme was shown to generate large coherent-state superpositions with improved fidelity and non-Gaussianity over benchmark probabilistic methods.
application_angle: Reliable optical cat-state preparation would support practical quantum metrology, long-distance repeater nodes, and bosonic-encoded communication and computing protocols.

title: Standalone optical frequency-offset locking electronics for atomic physics
abstract: We present a standalone frequency-offset locking system for controlling narrow-linewidth lasers using off-the-shelf electronic components. We lock two frequency-doubled 1560 nm lasers to a stable primary laser operating at 780 nm via their optical beat note. This radio-frequency beat note is fed through a broadband variable divider, a frequency-to-voltage converter, and a proportional-integrator controller to lock each follower laser to a tunable offset frequency relative to the primary. This architecture provides a large capture range ($> 1$ GHz), fast response times ($< 1$ ms), and high linearity. We achieve a frequency resolution of 1.9 kHz and a short-term fractional frequency instability $10^{-11}/\sqrt{τ\rm (s)}$ at 780 nm without the need for a dedicated, precise clock reference. We perform high-resolution spectroscopy of cold $^{87}$Rb atoms to demonstrate the tunability and precision of our locking system. We designed the system to be modular and extensible, making it applicable to a wide variety of atomic physics experiments, including laser cooling, spectroscopy, and quantum sensing with atoms, ions, and molecules.
one_liner: A modular electronics stack offset-locks narrow-line lasers over >1 GHz with kHz-level resolution for atomic experiments.
why_it_matters: It delivers precise, clock-light laser control with broad capture range and sub-ms response, directly useful for spectroscopy, cooling, and atom-based sensing.
detailed_summary: The paper presents a standalone optical frequency-offset locking system built from off-the-shelf RF and control electronics. It locks two frequency-doubled 1560 nm follower lasers to a 780 nm master via the beat note using a broadband variable divider, frequency-to-voltage conversion, and PI feedback. Reported performance includes >1 GHz capture range, <1 ms response, 1.9 kHz frequency resolution, and short-term fractional instability of 1e-11/sqrt(tau) at 780 nm. High-resolution spectroscopy on cold 87Rb validates tunable, precise operation in an atomic physics setting.
main_result: The system demonstrates tunable offset locking of narrow-linewidth lasers with GHz-scale capture range, kHz-scale resolution, and atomic-spectroscopy validation.
application_angle: This is practical lab hardware for stabilizing and tuning lasers in quantum sensors, laser-cooling setups, ion/atom experiments, and precision spectroscopy.

title: An alternative representation of multichannel Rydberg spectra: a modified Lu-Fano plot, applied to manganese spectroscopy
abstract: The well-known graphical representation called the Lu-Fano plot was originally developed for multi-channel Rydberg spectroscopy, especially in quantum defect theory. The present study shows some of the limitations of this traditional Lu-Fano plot that are desirable to improve on, when there are closely split ionization thresholds as in many current generation quantum information applications involving hyperfine-split thresholds, or when there are more than two ionization threshold energies. The modified representation introduced here is especially simplifying in the situation where one is exploring the bound states lying very far below those closely split thresholds. Moreover, it overcomes one limitation, namely that in contrast to the traditional Lu-Fano plot, the modified representation developed here can be utilized for problems where there more than two ionization thresholds. An example application to Rydberg series of the manganese atom illuminates its use in a practical problem.
one_liner: A modified Lu-Fano plot simplifies multichannel Rydberg-spectrum analysis near closely spaced and multiple ionization thresholds.
why_it_matters: Better spectral representations can improve modeling of complex Rydberg manifolds relevant to neutral-atom control and spectroscopy-heavy quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The work reformulates the standard Lu-Fano plot used in multichannel Rydberg spectroscopy to address cases with closely split thresholds and more than two ionization limits. The modified representation is particularly advantageous for analyzing bound states far below such thresholds, where the conventional plot becomes cumbersome. An application to manganese Rydberg series illustrates the method on a realistic atomic spectroscopy problem. The contribution is methodological, but directly relevant to interpreting complex Rydberg spectra in modern atomic platforms.
main_result: The authors introduce a generalized spectral representation that remains useful with multiple or closely spaced ionization thresholds and demonstrate it on manganese.
application_angle: Improved visualization and analysis of Rydberg structure can support calibration and state engineering in neutral-atom quantum devices and precision spectroscopy workflows.

title: Post-selective attack with multi-mode projection onto Fock subspace
abstract: In this work we present a comprehensive analysis of a post-selective attack on quantum key distribution protocols employing phase-encoded linearly independent coherent states (or similar alternatives). The attack relies on multimode projection onto a Fock subspace and enables probabilistic extraction of information by an eavesdropper. We derive analytical expressions for the information accessible to the adversary and show that it depends only on three protocol parameters: the mean photon number of the signal states, the phase separation in the information basis, and the expected optical loss of the quantum channel. Several optical realizations of phase-encoded quantum key distribution protocols are analyzed to illustrate the applicability of the results. Possible countermeasures against the proposed attack are also discussed.
one_liner: This work analyzes a post-selective multimode Fock-subspace attack on phase-encoded coherent-state QKD protocols.
why_it_matters: It quantifies a concrete eavesdropping route using only a few protocol parameters, helping tighten security analysis for practical coherent-state quantum links.
detailed_summary: The paper studies an attack on phase-encoded QKD schemes based on linearly independent coherent states, where the adversary performs multimode projection onto a Fock subspace and post-selects favorable outcomes. The authors derive analytical expressions for accessible information, showing dependence only on mean photon number, phase separation in the encoding basis, and expected channel loss. Several optical implementations of phase-encoded QKD are examined to demonstrate the scope of the attack. Potential countermeasures are also discussed, making the work directly relevant to implementation security.
main_result: The authors provide a closed-form characterization of an eavesdropper's information gain under a multimode post-selective Fock-subspace attack across phase-encoded QKD variants.
application_angle: The results can inform parameter selection and countermeasure design in deployed or near-term coherent-state quantum communication systems.

title: Low Latency GNN Accelerator for Quantum Error Correction
abstract: Quantum computers have the potential to solve certain complex problems in a much more efficient way than classical computers. Nevertheless, current quantum computer implementations are limited by high physical error rates. This issue is addressed by Quantum Error Correction (QEC) codes, which use multiple physical qubits to form a logical qubit to achieve a lower logical error rate, with the surface code being one of the most commonly used. The most time-critical step in this process is interpreting the measurements of the physical qubits to determine which errors have most likely occurred - a task called decoding. Consequently, the main challenge for QEC is to achieve error correction with high accuracy within the tight $1μs$ decoding time budget imposed by superconducting qubits. State-of-the-art QEC approaches trade accuracy for latency. In this work, we propose an FPGA accelerator for a Neural Network based decoder as a way to achieve a lower logical error rate than current methods within the tight time constraint, for code distance up to d=7. We achieved this goal by applying different hardware-aware optimizations to a high-accuracy GNN-based decoder. In addition, we propose several accelerator optimizations leading to the FPGA-based decoder achieving a latency smaller than $1μs$, with a lower error rate compared to the state-of-the-art.
one_liner: An FPGA GNN decoder reaches sub-microsecond latency for surface-code error correction while improving error rate over prior methods.
why_it_matters: Meeting the ~1 us decoding budget is a core systems bottleneck for superconducting quantum processors, so this directly targets deployable fault-tolerant control.
detailed_summary: This paper presents an FPGA accelerator for a graph-neural-network decoder aimed at surface-code quantum error correction. The design applies hardware-aware optimizations to preserve decoder accuracy while fitting the stringent latency constraints of superconducting-qubit control loops, targeting code distances up to d=7. The reported accelerator achieves latency below 1 microsecond and lower error rates than state-of-the-art low-latency alternatives. The work is a concrete hardware-software co-design result for real-time QEC deployment.
main_result: The accelerator demonstrates sub-1 us decoding for GNN-based QEC with improved logical-error performance relative to prior low-latency decoders.
application_angle: It advances a practical control-stack component needed to run fault-tolerant superconducting quantum hardware in real time.

title: Revisiting Quantum Code Generation: Where Should Domain Knowledge Live?
abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled the automation of an increasing number of programming tasks, including code generation for scientific and engineering domains. In rapidly evolving software ecosystems such as quantum software development, where frameworks expose complex abstractions, a central question is how best to incorporate domain knowledge into LLM-based assistants while preserving maintainability as libraries evolve. In this work, we study specialization strategies for Qiskit code generation using the Qiskit-HumanEval benchmark. We compare a parameter-specialized fine-tuned baseline introduced in prior work against a range of recent general-purpose LLMs enhanced with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and agent-based inference with execution feedback. Our results show that modern general-purpose LLMs consistently outperform the parameter-specialized baseline. While the fine-tuned model achieves approximately 47% pass@1 on Qiskit-HumanEval, recent general-purpose models reach 60-65% under zero-shot and retrieval-augmented settings, and up to 85% for the strongest evaluated model when combined with iterative execution-feedback agents -representing an improvement of more than 20% over zero-shot general-purpose performance and more than 35% over the parameter-specialized baseline. Agentic execution feedback yields the most consistent improvements, albeit at increased runtime cost, while RAG provides modest and model-dependent gains. These findings indicate that performance gains can be achieved without domain-specific fine-tuning, instead relying on inference-time augmentation, thereby enabling a more flexible and maintainable approach to LLM-assisted quantum software development.
one_liner: A benchmark study shows modern general LLMs with retrieval or execution feedback outperform fine-tuned models on Qiskit code generation.
why_it_matters: It suggests maintainable inference-time augmentation can beat domain-specific fine-tuning for evolving quantum software stacks used in hardware workflows.
detailed_summary: The authors compare specialization strategies for quantum code generation using the Qiskit-HumanEval benchmark. They evaluate a previously proposed fine-tuned baseline against recent general-purpose LLMs under zero-shot prompting, retrieval augmentation, and iterative agentic execution with feedback. General models reach roughly 60-65% pass@1 in zero-shot or RAG settings and up to 85% with execution-feedback agents, clearly exceeding the ~47% fine-tuned baseline. The main conclusion is that inference-time tools, not static fine-tuning, are the most robust way to encode quantum software knowledge as frameworks evolve.
main_result: General-purpose LLMs augmented with execution feedback substantially outperform a domain-fine-tuned baseline on Qiskit coding tasks.
application_angle: Better quantum code assistants can speed programming, debugging, and workflow integration around real quantum hardware and experimental control software.

title: Latent Style-based Quantum Wasserstein GAN for Drug Design
abstract: The development of new drugs is a tedious, time-consuming, and expensive process, for which the average costs are estimated to be up to around $2.5 billion. The first step in this long process is the design of the new drug, for which de novo drug design, assisted by artificial intelligence, has blossomed in recent years and revolutionized the field. In particular, generative artificial intelligence has delivered promising results in drug discovery and development, reducing costs and the time to solution. However, classical generative models, such as generative adversarial networks (GANs), are difficult to train due to barren plateaus and prone to mode collapse. Quantum computing may be an avenue to overcome these issues and provide models with fewer parameters, thereby enhancing the generalizability of GANs. We propose a new style-based quantum GAN (QGAN) architecture for drug design that implements noise encoding at every rotational gate of the circuit and a gradient penalty in the loss function to mitigate mode collapse. Our pipeline employs a variational autoencoder to represent the molecular structure in a latent space, which is then used as input to our QGAN. Our baseline model runs on up to 15 qubits to validate our architecture on quantum simulators, and a 156-qubit IBM Heron quantum computer in the five-qubit setup is used for inference to investigate the effects of using real quantum hardware on the analysis. We benchmark our results against classical models as provided by the MOSES benchmark suite.
one_liner: A style-based quantum Wasserstein GAN for molecular generation is benchmarked in latent space and tested with IBM quantum hardware.
why_it_matters: It explores whether quantum generative models can become useful tools for drug-design pipelines while exposing hardware effects beyond simulator-only studies.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes a latent-space drug-design pipeline combining a variational autoencoder with a style-based quantum Wasserstein GAN. The QGAN injects noise at every rotational gate and uses a gradient penalty to reduce training instabilities such as mode collapse, and the architecture is validated on simulators with up to 15 qubits. A five-qubit version is also executed for inference on IBM's 156-qubit Heron processor to probe the impact of real hardware. Results are benchmarked against classical baselines using the MOSES molecular-generation suite.
platform_or_system: VAE plus QGAN on IBM Heron
main_result: The authors demonstrate a style-based quantum Wasserstein GAN for molecular latent-space generation and run inference on real IBM quantum hardware.
application_angle: This points toward hybrid quantum-classical generative workflows for early-stage molecular discovery, with an explicit path from simulation to hardware execution.

title: Dicke materials as a resource for quantum squeezing
abstract: We study magnetic materials whose low energy physics can be effectively described by a Dicke model, which we term Dicke materials. We show how a Dicke model emerges in such materials due to a coexistence of fast-dispersing and slow-dispersing spins, which are strongly coupled. Analogous to the paradigmatic Dicke model describing light-matter interactions, these materials also exhibit signatures of a superradiant phase transition. The ground state near the superradiant phase transition is expected to be squeezed, making Dicke materials a resource for quantum metrology and witnessing entanglement in solid-state systems. However, as an entanglement measure, squeezing can be sensitive to perturbations that are otherwise irrelevant for usual correlation functions and order parameters. Motivated by the prospect of observing squeezing in such Dicke materials, we study the robustness of ground state squeezing under ubiquitous imperfections such as finite temperature, disorder, and local interactions. Using analytical and numerical techniques, we show that the squeezing obtained is perturbatively stable against these imperfections and quantitatively evaluate regimes promising for experimental observation.
one_liner: The paper identifies Dicke-like magnetic solids as robust sources of ground-state squeezing near a superradiant transition.
why_it_matters: It links solid-state magnetic materials to metrologically useful squeezing and shows that the effect survives realistic temperature, disorder, and interaction perturbations.
detailed_summary: The authors derive an effective Dicke-model description for magnetic materials containing strongly coupled fast- and slow-dispersing spin sectors. They analyze the squeezed ground state expected near the superradiant phase transition and quantify how finite temperature, disorder, and local interactions degrade the squeezing. Using analytical and numerical methods, they show the squeezing is perturbatively stable over experimentally relevant parameter ranges. The work frames these solids as realistic platforms for entanglement witnessing and metrology in many-body condensed-matter systems.
main_result: They show that Dicke-material ground-state squeezing near the superradiant transition remains quantitatively observable despite common imperfections.
application_angle: Robust spin squeezing in a solid-state material could enable compact quantum-enhanced sensing and entanglement diagnostics without requiring isolated atomic platforms.

title: Performance of BB84 without decoy states under varying announcement structures
abstract: In phase-randomized weak coherent pulse (WCP) implementations of Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) BB84 protocol, the decoy method is often used to compensate BB84's vulnerability against photon number splitting (PNS) attacks. However, this typically introduces extra complexities and requirements on experimental devices. In this paper, we are therefore interested in phase-randomized WCP implementations without the decoy method. We examine the performance of three QKD protocols with different classical announcement structures, namely BB84, SARG04, and No Public Announcement of Basis (NPAB) BB84, using numerical security proof techniques. We compare secure key rates of the three protocols in asymptotic and finite-size regimes for lossy and noisy channels. The three protocols show different relative advantages depending on the channel behaviour. Canonical BB84 shows robustness against errors and depolarization, SARG04 demonstrates resilience against high loss channels, and NPAB BB84 shows potential advantages against physical misalignment between QKD devices.
one_liner: This work maps when non-decoy BB84 variants can outperform each other under loss, noise, and misalignment.
why_it_matters: It clarifies viable operating regimes for simpler weak-coherent-pulse QKD systems that avoid decoy-state overhead while retaining security.
detailed_summary: The paper studies phase-randomized weak coherent pulse QKD without decoy states using numerical security-proof methods. It compares canonical BB84, SARG04, and NPAB BB84 in both asymptotic and finite-size settings under lossy and noisy channels. The results show protocol-dependent tradeoffs: BB84 is more robust to depolarization and errors, SARG04 performs better in high-loss regimes, and NPAB BB84 can benefit under device misalignment. This gives a concrete design map for non-decoy QKD deployments.
main_result: The main result is a comparative secure-key-rate benchmark showing distinct performance advantages for BB84, SARG04, and NPAB BB84 under different channel impairments.
application_angle: The analysis supports lower-complexity QKD implementations by identifying when decoy-free protocols remain practically competitive for real links.

title: Development of Biphoton Entangled Light Spectroscopy (BELS) using Bell pairs
abstract: We introduce Biphoton Entanglement Light Spectroscopy (BELS), a quantum spectroscopic technique that employs polarization entangled Bell pairs and two photon interference to probe material properties. In BELS, the measured signal arises not from single photon intensities but from changes in the joint polarization and path correlations of biphoton Bell pairs transmitted through or scattered by a sample and analyzed via cross channel coincidences. A key concept of BELS is the explicit mapping between Jones matrix operations and transformations within the Bell state manifold. Optical elements that are equivalent under classical polarization optics can produce qualitatively distinct signatures in the coincidence landscape when interrogated with entangled photons. We demonstrate that linear birefringence and Faraday rotation generate orthogonal admixtures of Bell states, yielding experimentally distinguishable coincidence channels within a single measurement. We measure birefringence in an anisotropic dielectric and Faraday rotation in $\text{Tb}_3\text{Ga}_5\text{O}_{12}$. By mapping the changes to the photonic entanglement, BELS establishes a new framework for future entanglement enhanced spectroscopy, a potentially powerful approach in characterizing quantum materials, nanophotonic devices, and light matter interactions perhaps eventually at a fundamentally quantum level.
one_liner: BELS uses Bell-pair coincidence signatures to distinguish optical material responses that look equivalent in classical polarization spectroscopy.
why_it_matters: It introduces an experimentally demonstrated entanglement-based spectroscopy modality with direct relevance to quantum materials and nanophotonic characterization.
detailed_summary: The authors formulate Biphoton Entangled Light Spectroscopy by mapping sample-induced Jones operations onto transformations within the Bell-state manifold. Instead of relying on single-photon intensities, the method reads out changes in joint polarization-path correlations through cross-channel coincidence counts. They experimentally show that linear birefringence and Faraday rotation populate orthogonal Bell-state admixtures, allowing them to distinguish these effects in one measurement. Measurements on an anisotropic dielectric and on terbium gallium garnet validate the approach.
main_result: They demonstrate that Bell-pair coincidence landscapes can directly resolve birefringence and Faraday rotation through distinct Bell-state mixing signatures.
application_angle: This could become a high-information spectroscopy tool for quantum materials, magneto-optic devices, and nanophotonic components where correlation-level observables reveal more than classical optics.

title: Benchmarking Dual-Polarization Silicon Nitride Photonic Integrated Circuits for Trapped-Ion Quantum Technologies
abstract: Trapped ions are one of the most advanced platforms for quantum technologies, with applications ranging from quantum computing to precision timekeeping. A crucial step towards more compact and scalable systems involves integrating photonic integrated circuits (PICs) into surface ion traps to enable on-chip light delivery and optical addressing of individual ions. Currently, most implementations rely solely on transverse-electric (TE) mode grating couplers, where the emitted light is polarized in the plane of the chip. In this work, we design, fabricate and characterize silicon nitride (Si\(_3\)N\(_4\)) PIC components, including incoupling structures, splitters, and grating couplers that support both TE and transverse-magnetic (TM) modes with comparable optical losses. We benchmark the PIC at 760\,nm, which is a typical wavelength for Yb$^{+}$-applications. The fabricated grating couplers enable the outcoupling of collimated free-space beams for both polarizations, exhibiting distinct emission angles. This dual-polarization capability gives more flexibility in polarization control and expands the accessible optical design space for trapped-ion quantum technologies.
one_liner: A silicon nitride PIC for trapped-ion systems is benchmarked at 760 nm with comparable TE and TM routing and outcoupling performance.
why_it_matters: Dual-polarization integrated photonics expands the optical control space needed for scalable trapped-ion quantum computing and networking hardware.
detailed_summary: The work designs, fabricates, and characterizes Si3N4 photonic integrated components including input couplers, splitters, and grating outcouplers that support both TE and TM modes. Measurements at 760 nm, relevant to Yb+ ion operations, show comparable losses for the two polarizations and collimated free-space emission from both. The dual-polarization grating couplers emit at distinct angles, increasing flexibility for beam delivery and ion addressing in surface-trap architectures. This is a practical hardware benchmark rather than a purely simulated design study.
platform_or_system: Si3N4 photonic integrated circuits for trapped ions
main_result: The main result is an experimentally benchmarked 760 nm dual-polarization SiN PIC platform with functional TE and TM grating outcouplers for ion-facing free-space beams.
application_angle: Integrated dual-polarization optics can reduce bulk optical complexity and improve scalability for trapped-ion processors, clocks, and photonic interconnects.

title: Optimal filtering for a giant cavity in waveguide QED systems
abstract: In waveguide quantum electrodynamics (QED) systems, a giant cavity can be engineered to interact with quantum fields by multiple distant coupling points so that its non-Markovian dynamics are quite different from traditional quantum optical cavity systems. Towards feedback control this system, this paper designs an optimal filter for the giant cavity systems to estimate its state evolution under continuous quantum measurements. Firstly, the Langevin equation in the Heisenberg picture are derived, which is a linear continuous-time system with both states and inputs delays resulting from the unconventional distant couplings. Compared to existing modeling approaches, this formulation effectively preserves the nonlocal coupling and multiple delay dynamic characteristics inherent in the original system. In particular, the presence of coupling and propagation delays leads to noncommutativity among the system operators at different times, which prevents the direct application of existing quantum filtering methods. To address this issue, an optimal filter is designed, in which the delayed-state covariance matrices are computed. By iteratively evaluating the delayed-state covariance over successive time intervals, the resulting optimal filter can be implemented in an interval-wise backward recursion algorithm. Finally, numerical simulations are conducted to evaluate the tracking performance of the proposed optimal filter for the giant cavity. By comparing between the evolutions of Wigner functions of coherent and cat states and the filter, the effectiveness of the optimal filter is validated.
one_liner: The authors develop an optimal continuous-measurement filter for giant-cavity waveguide QED systems with intrinsic time delays.
why_it_matters: State estimation is a prerequisite for feedback control in non-Markovian quantum photonic hardware where standard filters break down.
detailed_summary: The paper models a giant cavity coupled to a waveguide through multiple distant interaction points, yielding delayed Langevin dynamics in the Heisenberg picture. Because coupling and propagation delays induce noncommuting operators across times, standard quantum filtering approaches cannot be directly applied. The authors derive delayed-state covariance equations and build an optimal filter implemented via interval-wise backward recursion. Numerical tests tracking coherent and cat-state Wigner-function evolution validate the estimator's performance.
main_result: They provide and numerically validate an optimal quantum filter that can estimate the state evolution of delayed, non-Markovian giant-cavity systems under continuous measurement.
application_angle: The method enables feedback and control design for distributed waveguide-QED photonic devices where finite propagation delays are a core engineering constraint.

title: Sub-nanometer resolution of the nitrogen-vacancy center by Fourier magnetic imaging
abstract: Solid-state spins in diamond are promising building blocks for quantum computing and quantum sensing, both of which require precise nanoscale addressing of individual spins. To explore the resolution limit of this approach, we demonstrate Fourier magnetic imaging of nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond under state-of-the-art conditions. We constructed a highly compact experimental platform featuring thermal drift compensation under ambient conditions and generated a pulsed magnetic field gradient of up to 13.5 G/$μ$m. By implementing the Fourier magnetic imaging protocol, we achieved localization of a single nitrogen-vacancy center with a spatial resolution of 0.28 $\pm$ 0.10 nm and a magnetic field measurement deviation of 9 nT. This technique holds potential for applications such as localizing spins within proteins and cells.
one_liner: Fourier magnetic imaging localizes a single NV center with 0.28 nm resolution under ambient conditions.
why_it_matters: Sub-nanometer spin localization materially improves quantum sensing and nanoscale spin-addressing for biological and solid-state targets.
detailed_summary: The experiment combines a compact ambient-condition platform, thermal drift compensation, and pulsed magnetic field gradients up to 13.5 G/um to push Fourier magnetic imaging of NV centers. Using the protocol, the authors localize a single NV center with 0.28 +/- 0.10 nm spatial resolution and report a magnetic-field measurement deviation of 9 nT. The result probes the practical resolution limit of spin imaging under realistic conditions rather than cryogenic or highly specialized operation. It directly targets the precision needed for nanoscale spin mapping.
main_result: The main result is ambient Fourier magnetic imaging of a single NV center with sub-nanometer localization precision and nanotesla-scale field accuracy.
application_angle: Such resolution could support locating and addressing spins in dense solid-state devices or even within proteins and cells for quantum-enabled bioimaging and sensing.

title: Interference-induced state engineering and Hamiltonian control for noisy collective-spin metrology
abstract: Interference provides a fundamental mechanism for generating and manipulating entanglement in many-body quantum systems. Here, we develop an interference framework in which the nonlinear dynamics of collective spin-$\tfrac{1}{2}$ ensembles are mapped onto phase accumulation and self-interference in phase space, providing a direct and physically transparent description of entanglement formation. Within this framework, one-axis twisting produces Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ) states, while two-axis twisting generates multi-component GHZ superpositions relevant for multiparameter quantum metrology. Building on this interference-based description, we analyze metrological performance under realistic Markovian noise, including local and collective emission, pumping, and dephasing, and examine the role of Hamiltonian control based on linear and nonlinear interactions. We show that while control can enhance single-parameter sensitivity in a noise-dependent regime, the achievable precision in multiparameter estimation is fundamentally constrained. These results establish interference as a unifying principle linking nonlinear dynamics, entanglement generation, and metrological performance, and reveal intrinsic limitations of multiparameter quantum sensing. Our framework provides broadly applicable insight into the design of robust quantum-enhanced measurement protocols in noisy many-body systems.
one_liner: An interference picture links collective-spin entanglement generation, control, and metrology limits under realistic Markovian noise.
why_it_matters: It gives a practical design framework for noisy many-body quantum sensors and clarifies when control can or cannot preserve quantum-enhanced precision.
detailed_summary: The paper maps nonlinear collective-spin dynamics onto phase accumulation and self-interference in phase space, providing an intuitive description of entanglement formation in spin-1/2 ensembles. Within this framework, one-axis twisting yields GHZ states, while two-axis twisting produces multi-component GHZ superpositions suited to multiparameter sensing. The authors then incorporate local and collective emission, pumping, and dephasing, and analyze how linear and nonlinear Hamiltonian controls modify attainable precision. They find control-dependent gains for single-parameter estimation in certain noise regimes, but identify fundamental limits that restrict multiparameter advantages.
main_result: It establishes an interference-based formalism that predicts entanglement generation and metrological performance under realistic noise, including intrinsic limits for multiparameter estimation.
application_angle: The framework can guide control design for robust spin-based quantum sensors operating in realistic decohering environments.

title: Charging efficiency bursts in a quantum battery with cyclic indefinite causal order
abstract: Enhancement of quantum battery performance is a popular subject in quantum thermodynamics. An interesting phenomenon is the quick charging effect [Phys. Rev. Res. 6, 023136 (2024)], which has been explored by utilizing a quantum interferometric technique known as superposition of trajectories. A similar technique used to boost quantum battery performance is indefinite causal order. Here, we propose a new charging protocol that utilizes cyclic indefinite causal order, whereby $N$ charging sequences are superposed when utilizing $N$ chargers. We observe charging efficiency bursts when implementing our cyclic indefinite charging protocol. The duration of these bursts increase with $N$. Additionally, we present a circuit model to implement our charging protocol for the two-charger scenario and perform proof-of-concept demonstrations on IonQ, Quantinuum and IBMQ quantum processors. The results validate the existence of charging efficiency bursts as shown by our theoretical analysis and numerical simulations.
one_liner: A cyclic indefinite-causal-order charging protocol produces burst-like gains in quantum battery charging efficiency and is tested on real quantum processors.
why_it_matters: It turns a foundational quantum-information concept into a hardware-demonstrated route for improving charging performance in near-term quantum battery protocols.
detailed_summary: The authors propose a charging protocol in which N charging sequences are coherently superposed using cyclic indefinite causal order across N chargers. Their analysis predicts charging-efficiency bursts whose duration increases with the number of chargers, indicating a structured performance enhancement beyond fixed-order schemes. For the two-charger case, they give an explicit circuit construction and run proof-of-concept experiments on IonQ, Quantinuum, and IBM quantum hardware. The experimental data agrees with theory and numerics in showing the predicted charging bursts.
platform_or_system: gate-based quantum processors implementing quantum-battery circuits
main_result: The work demonstrates that cyclic indefinite causal order can generate experimentally observable charging-efficiency bursts, with burst duration improving as more chargers are included.
application_angle: It suggests a concrete control primitive for boosting energy-transfer protocols on near-term quantum devices and future engineered quantum batteries.

title: Understanding Bugs in Quantum Simulators: An Empirical Study
abstract: Quantum simulators are a foundational component of the quantum software ecosystem. They are widely used to develop and debug quantum programs, validate compiler transformations, and support empirical claims about correctness and performance. In the absence of large-scale quantum hardware, simulator outputs are often treated as ground truth for algorithm development and system evaluation. However, quantum simulators also introduce unique implementation challenges. They must faithfully emulate quantum behavior while executing on classical hardware, requiring complex representations of quantum state evolution, operator composition, and noise modeling. Yet, we still lack a large-scale and in-depth study of failures in quantum simulators. To bridge this gap, this work presents a comprehensive empirical study of bugs in widely used open-source quantum simulators. We analyze 394 confirmed bugs from 12 simulators and manually categorize them based on root causes, failure manifestations, affected components, and discovery mechanisms. Our study reveals several key findings. First, bug discovery is largely user-driven, with most crashes, exceptions, and resource-related failures not detected by automated testing and identified after deployment. Second, logical correctness failures are widespread and often silent, producing plausible but incorrect outputs without triggering crashes or explicit error signals. Third, many critical failures originate in classical simulator infrastructure, such as memory management, indexing, configuration, and dependency compatibility, rather than in core quantum execution logic. These findings provide new insights into the reliability challenges of quantum simulators and highlight opportunities to improve testing and validation practices in the quantum software ecosystem.
one_liner: A large-scale study of 394 confirmed bugs shows quantum simulators often fail silently in classical infrastructure rather than quantum logic alone.
why_it_matters: Reliable simulators are essential for validating quantum hardware and sensing workflows, so understanding their failure modes directly affects experimental confidence.
detailed_summary: This empirical study analyzes 394 confirmed bugs across 12 open-source quantum simulators and categorizes them by root cause, manifestation, affected components, and discovery method. It finds that many failures are discovered post-deployment by users, while logical correctness bugs frequently produce plausible but wrong outputs without crashes. A significant fraction of critical issues stem from classical infrastructure such as memory handling, indexing, configuration, and dependency compatibility rather than only quantum-state evolution code. The paper highlights structural weaknesses in current testing and validation practice for the quantum software stack.
main_result: The main result is a systematic bug taxonomy showing that silent correctness failures and classical infrastructure faults are pervasive in widely used quantum simulators.
application_angle: The findings are directly useful for improving simulator trustworthiness in hardware validation, control development, and quantum sensing software pipelines.

title: Encoded Quantum Signal Processing for Heisenberg-Limited Metrology
abstract: Entangled quantum probes can achieve Heisenberg-limited measurement precision, but this advantage is typically destroyed by noise. We address this issue by introducing a framework that we call encoded quantum signal processing, which unifies quantum error detection and quantum signal processing into an effective single-qubit framework, and provides a paradigm for constructing logical sensors that are robust to noise while remaining sensitive to the signal of interest. We show that encoding sensor qubits into a repetition code and using syndrome measurements as a signal-processing primitive restores Heisenberg scaling under realistic noise, without applying recovery operations. We prove that product-state sensing with syndrome post-processing is fundamentally limited to standard quantum limit (SQL) scaling, and develop four protocols that overcome this barrier through entanglement or sequential signal amplification, achieving Heisenberg-limited precision with exponential error suppression in code distance. For spatially inhomogeneous fields, Bayesian marginalization preserves Heisenberg scaling provided noise decreases sufficiently with system size. The underlying mechanism, which we formalize as encoded quantum signal processing, reduces multi-qubit metrology to an effective single-qubit problem where syndrome measurement implements nonlinear signal transformations. Numerical simulations validate the theoretical predictions: syndrome-based inference achieves near-Heisenberg scaling at noise levels where bare probes approach the SQL, and a concatenated protocol maintains this scaling under joint transverse noise and longitudinal inhomogeneities.
one_liner: Encoded quantum signal processing combines error detection and signal processing to recover near-Heisenberg metrology under realistic noise.
why_it_matters: It offers a concrete route to noise-robust logical sensing with exponential error suppression instead of losing quantum advantage at modest noise levels.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces encoded quantum signal processing, which folds quantum error detection and quantum signal processing into an effective single-qubit sensing description. By encoding probes in a repetition code and using syndrome measurements as a nonlinear signal-processing primitive, the scheme restores Heisenberg scaling without recovery operations under realistic noise. The authors prove that product-state sensing with only syndrome post-processing cannot beat SQL scaling, then develop four protocols based on entanglement or sequential amplification that achieve Heisenberg-limited precision with exponential suppression in code distance. Numerical simulations show near-Heisenberg performance even where bare probes revert toward SQL behavior, including under joint transverse noise and longitudinal inhomogeneity.
main_result: It demonstrates that syndrome-based logical sensing can achieve Heisenberg-limited precision with exponential noise suppression in realistic noisy metrology settings.
application_angle: The approach is relevant for building fault-aware precision sensors that remain useful in noisy photonic or qubit-based measurement platforms.

title: Toward scalable and bias-stable optical phased arrays on lithium tantalate
abstract: Ferroelectric materials are an ideal platform for high-speed reconfigurable photonic integrated circuits (PICs) for classical and quantum photonic computations, communications, and sensing. Most reconfigurable PIC devices achieve their functionalities via interference and are therefore highly sensitive to phase errors. Under static bias, carrier drift in ferroelectric waveguides induces continuous phase drift, creating a severe bottleneck for both PIC functionality and scalability. Here we propose achieving bias-stable and scalable ferroelectric PICs by exploiting the intrinsically low carrier drift of lithium tantalate (LT). Taking one of the PIC devices that is most sensitive to phase drift, the optical phased array (OPA), as an example, we designed and fabricated an integrated LT OPA that can keep the far-field main lobe 8 dB higher than side lobes for over 4 hours, representing at least a two-order-of-magnitude improvement over the state of the art. We demonstrated our device's capability in generating arbitrary spatiotemporal waveforms with a modulation frequency as low as 0.1 Hz, leading to practical applications in optical tweezers, trapped-ion quantum computers, adaptive optics for astronomy, AR, 3D printers, LiDAR, and free-space optical communications. Beyond OPA, our work establishes LT as a bias-stable, scalable, and high-speed PIC platform for large-scale classical and quantum photonic systems.
one_liner: Lithium-tantalate optical phased arrays show strongly improved bias stability, maintaining an 8 dB main-lobe advantage over sidelobes for more than 4 hours.
why_it_matters: Bias-stable phase control is a major bottleneck in large reconfigurable photonic chips, and this work points to a scalable ferroelectric platform for quantum and classical systems.
detailed_summary: The work targets phase drift in ferroelectric photonic integrated circuits, where static bias normally causes carrier-drift-induced phase instability that degrades interference-based devices. Using lithium tantalate, the authors design and fabricate an integrated optical phased array that keeps its far-field main lobe 8 dB above sidelobes for over 4 hours, representing at least a two-order-of-magnitude improvement over prior art. They also demonstrate arbitrary spatiotemporal waveform generation down to 0.1 Hz modulation, indicating stable operation over slow control timescales. The results position LT as a high-speed, bias-stable platform for large interferometric photonic systems.
main_result: The main result is a fabricated LT phased array with hours-long stable beamforming and substantially reduced phase drift relative to existing ferroelectric PIC platforms.
application_angle: This stability directly benefits scalable free-space optical links, trapped-ion beam delivery, LiDAR, and reconfigurable quantum photonic interconnects.

title: STAR-Magic Mutation: Even More Efficient Analog Rotation Gates for Early Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computer
abstract: We introduce STAR-magic mutation, an efficient protocol for implementing logical rotation gates on early fault-tolerant quantum computers. This protocol judiciously combines two of the latest state preparation protocols: transversal multi-rotation protocol and magic state cultivation. It achieves a logical rotation gate with a favorable error scaling of $\mathcal{O}(θ_L^{2(1-Θ(1/d))}p_{\text{ph}})$, while requiring only the ancillary space of a single surface code patch. Here, $θ_L$ is the logical rotation angle, $p_{\text{ph}}$ is the physical error rate, and $d$ is the code distance. This scaling marks a significant improvement over the previous state-of-the-art, $\mathcal{O}(θ_L p_{\text{ph}})$, making our protocol particularly powerful for implementing a sequence of small-angle rotation gates, like Trotter-based circuits. Notably, for $θ_L \lesssim 10^{-5}$, our protocol achieves a two-order-of-magnitude reduction in both the execution time and the error rate of analog rotation gates compared to the standard $T$-gate synthesis using cultivated magic states. Building upon this protocol, we also propose a novel quantum computing architecture designed for early fault-tolerant quantum computers, dubbed ``STAR ver.~3". It employs a refined circuit compilation strategy based on Clifford+$T$+$φ$ gate set, rather than the conventional Clifford+$T$ or Clifford+$φ$ gate sets. We establish a theoretical bound on the feasible circuit size on this architecture and illustrate its capabilities by analyzing the spacetime costs for simulating the dynamics of quantum many-body systems. Specifically, we demonstrate that our architecture can simulate biologically-relevant molecules or lattice models at scales beyond the reach of exact classical simulation, with only a few hundred thousand physical qubits, even assuming a realistic error rate of $p_{\text{ph}}=10^{-3}$.
one_liner: STAR-magic mutation enables much more efficient logical small-angle rotations, reducing time and error for early fault-tolerant quantum computing.
why_it_matters: Small-angle rotations dominate many useful algorithms, so improving them can materially lower the hardware cost of early fault-tolerant applications.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces STAR-magic mutation, a logical rotation protocol that combines transversal multi-rotation state preparation with magic state cultivation while using only one ancillary surface-code patch. It achieves an error scaling of O(theta_L^{2(1-Theta(1/d))} p_ph), improving on prior O(theta_L p_ph) behavior and making very small logical-angle rotations substantially cheaper. For theta_L below about 10^-5, the authors report roughly two orders of magnitude reductions in both execution time and error relative to standard T-gate synthesis with cultivated magic states. They further propose the STAR ver. 3 architecture based on a Clifford+T+phi gate set and argue, through spacetime estimates, that a few hundred thousand physical qubits could support classically hard many-body simulations at p_ph = 10^-3.
main_result: It presents a more efficient logical analog-rotation method and an associated architecture that sharply lowers the cost of small-angle gates in early fault-tolerant machines.
application_angle: The protocol is especially relevant for Trotterized chemistry and many-body simulation workloads where dense sequences of precise small rotations dominate runtime and error budgets.

title: Dynamical Evolution of Quantum Correlations and Decoherence in Coupled Oscillators Interacting with a Thermal Reservoir
abstract: We investigate the dynamical evolution of quantum discord, entanglement and purity in an open quantum system of two coupled asymmetric harmonic oscillators interacting with a thermal environment. Using the Kossakowski-Lindblad master equation we analyze the time evolution starting with a squeezed vacuum state. In contrast to our previous study on entanglement evolution in asymmetric oscillators, the present work introduces XY-type position-position coupling together with a systematic joint analysis of quantum discord and purity alongside entanglement. We examine the combined effects of the squeezing parameter, asymmetry parameter, coupling constant, dissipation rate and temperature. We find that quantum discord and entanglement exhibit, in general, a non-monotonic decrease over time. Increasing temperature consistently accelerates the degradation of both quantum correlations and purity, whereas increasing dissipation accelerates the degradation of quantum correlations but leads to higher steady-state purity. Increasing the squeezing parameter provides a protective effect by enhancing initial correlations and prolonging entanglement survival time, while increasing the coupling constant leads to higher quantum correlations. The asymmetry parameter exhibits only a weak influence on the correlation evolution. Our analysis reveals that quantum discord demonstrates stronger resilience than entanglement, which can present more complex behaviour including entanglement sudden death and possible temporary revivals and re-suppressions. These findings provide valuable insights for developing robust quantum information protocols and strategies for preserving quantum correlations in realistic open quantum systems, with potential extensions to non-Markovian regimes and multi-mode architectures.
one_liner: Open-system analysis maps how discord, entanglement, and purity decay in coupled asymmetric oscillators under thermal noise.
why_it_matters: It identifies tunable knobs that prolong useful quantum correlations in bosonic hardware relevant to photonic and continuous-variable protocols.
detailed_summary: The paper studies two asymmetric harmonic oscillators with XY-type position-position coupling, initialized in a squeezed vacuum and evolved with a Kossakowski-Lindblad master equation in a thermal reservoir. It jointly tracks entanglement, quantum discord, and purity versus squeezing, asymmetry, coupling, dissipation, and temperature. Higher temperature speeds degradation of all figures of merit, while larger squeezing and coupling improve initial correlations and extend entanglement lifetime. Quantum discord is found to be more robust than entanglement, including regimes with entanglement sudden death and transient revivals.
platform_or_system: Coupled bosonic oscillators in a thermal bath
main_result: The main result is a parameter-resolved demonstration that squeezing and inter-oscillator coupling can partially protect correlations, while discord remains substantially more resilient than entanglement under decoherence.
application_angle: The analysis provides design guidance for preserving continuous-variable resources in realistic photonic quantum information and sensing architectures.

title: Connection-topology--dependent energy transport and ergotropy in quantum battery networks with reciprocal and nonreciprocal couplings
abstract: The realization of scalable quantum battery architectures requires concern not only with how much energy can be stored, but also with how energy is transported, distributed, and converted into extractable work across connected battery nodes. While previous studies mainly focused on collective charging in multi-cell quantum batteries, the topology-dependent transport law and the corresponding work-oriented performance of quantum battery networks remain largely unexplored. In this work, we investigate quantum battery networks with engineered reciprocal and nonreciprocal couplings and compare different connection topologies, including cascaded and parallel architectures, within a unified transport framework. In the nonreciprocal regime, the optimal coupling follows distinct scaling laws for the two connection topologies, namely $J_{\rm op}^{c}\propto N$ for cascaded transport and $J_{\rm op}^{p}\propto N^{-1/2}$ for parallel charging in the large-$N$ limit. In reciprocal cascaded networks, a parity-dependent spectral response produces an odd-even transport effect that is absent in the nonreciprocal and parallel configurations. We further analyze the role of thermal and squeezed reservoirs and show that thermal noise mainly increases passive energy, whereas squeezing enhances ergotropy and thus the useful fraction of stored energy. These results shift the emphasis from charging enhancement to transport engineering and provide architecture-level design principles for quantum battery networks.
one_liner: Quantum battery networks show topology-dependent transport and ergotropy scaling under reciprocal and nonreciprocal couplings.
why_it_matters: It turns network connectivity and reservoir engineering into concrete design rules for scalable energy transport in quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: This work compares cascaded and parallel quantum battery networks within a unified transport framework, considering both reciprocal and nonreciprocal couplings. In the nonreciprocal regime, the optimal coupling scales differently with network size: linearly for cascaded transport and as inverse square root for parallel charging in the large-N limit. Reciprocal cascaded systems exhibit an odd-even spectral transport effect that disappears in nonreciprocal and parallel cases. The authors also show that thermal reservoirs mainly add passive energy, whereas squeezed reservoirs increase ergotropy, i.e. the extractable work content.
main_result: The paper establishes topology-specific scaling laws for optimal coupling and shows that squeezing preferentially boosts useful stored work rather than just total energy.
application_angle: These results are directly relevant to architecture-level optimization of mesoscopic quantum energy-storage and distribution hardware, including photonic implementations with engineered dissipation.

title: Zero-Uncertainty States Relative to Observable Algebras
abstract: We study zero-uncertainty states with quantum memory from an operator-algebraic perspective, which naturally accommodates degenerate projective-valued measurements. In the equal-dimension setting, we prove a rigidity theorem for purity and maximal entanglement. We then analyze two mechanisms by which this rigidity can fail: one arising from proper observable subalgebras, and the other from allowing larger memory dimensions. In these cases, we give corresponding algebraic decomposition and representation-theoretic descriptions, and compare their mathematical structure with their physical interpretation. Finally, we present an example from quantum steering to illustrate how our framework helps resolve a concrete physical question in a specific setting.
one_liner: An operator-algebraic framework characterizes zero-uncertainty states with quantum memory and when maximal-entanglement rigidity breaks down.
why_it_matters: It sharpens the mathematical structure behind measurement-memory correlations used in secure communication, steering, and entanglement certification.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze zero-uncertainty states relative to observable algebras, explicitly accommodating degenerate projective measurements. They prove a rigidity theorem in the equal-dimension case linking zero uncertainty to purity and maximal entanglement, then identify two ways this rigidity fails: proper observable subalgebras and enlarged memory dimensions. For those non-rigid cases, they provide algebraic decompositions and representation-theoretic characterizations. A quantum steering example shows how the formalism resolves a concrete physical question beyond the nondegenerate textbook setting.
main_result: The main result is a complete structural characterization of when zero-uncertainty states enforce maximal entanglement and when algebraic or dimensional extensions permit broader state classes.
application_angle: The framework is useful for designing and validating quantum communication primitives that rely on uncertainty with memory, especially in realistic systems with degeneracies or side information.

title: Structured Single-photon Metasource
abstract: Structured quantum light is crucial for high-dimensional quantum information processing, yet its direct generation from quantum emitters remains challenging due to their intrinsic locality and omnidirectional radiation. Metasurfaces have been adopted for quantum-light wavefront shaping, typically in cascaded or stacked configurations that suffer from low efficiency and limited resolution. Here, we demonstrate a semiconductor metasource that directly embodies single quantum dots in a nonlocal GaAs metasurface. Spontaneous emission from quantum dot is efficiently funneled into an extended quasi-bound-state-in-the-continuum mode while sustaining strong mode-emitter overlap. A lateral core-barrier heterostructure tunes mode volume and spatial distribution to balance Purcell enhancement and holographic resolution. Using spatially modulated geometric phase, our compact metasource enables deterministic generation of diverse single-photon radiation patterns, including orbital-angular-momentum beams and holographic images. Our work brings versatile single-photon wavefront control into the nanoscale cavity quantum electrodynamics regime, offering a scalable route toward integrated sources of structured quantum light.
one_liner: A GaAs metasurface with embedded quantum dots directly emits structured single photons, including OAM beams and holographic images.
why_it_matters: It integrates single-photon generation and wavefront shaping into one nanoscale device, removing lossy external optics from quantum photonic sources.
detailed_summary: The device embeds single semiconductor quantum dots into a nonlocal GaAs metasurface that funnels spontaneous emission into an extended quasi-bound-state-in-the-continuum mode. A lateral core-barrier heterostructure is used to tune mode volume and field distribution, balancing Purcell enhancement against holographic spatial resolution. Spatially patterned geometric phase then converts the emitted light into target single-photon wavefronts, including orbital-angular-momentum beams and holographic images. The approach performs source and wavefront engineering within the same cavity-QED-scale structure rather than in cascaded optics.
platform_or_system: Quantum dots in a GaAs metasurface
main_result: The paper demonstrates deterministic direct generation of multiple structured single-photon emission patterns from an integrated semiconductor metasource.
application_angle: This is a practical route to compact on-chip sources for high-dimensional quantum communication, imaging, and integrated photonic quantum processors.

title: A High-frequency Geodetic VLBI Experiment for Optical Clock Comparison
abstract: An intercontinental metrological clock comparison between Italy and the Republic of Korea was performed by means of geodetic K-band VLBI observations. The comparison involved the hydrogen masers (H-masers) used at Medicina and Sejong radio telescopes. The same clocks were simultaneously compared by a satellite link and by high-precision optical clocks maintained at the National Metrology Institutes, KRISS in Korea and INRIM in Italy, and delivered to VLBI antennas via optical fiber. The H-masers frequency difference was estimated by extrapolating the clock rate from VLBI data using two geodetic VLBI software. This was subsequently compared with clock differences derived by satellite link and by local optical clocks. Results obtained with different approaches were in agreement at the level of $10^{-15}$ s/s. This pilot study demonstrates that standard high-frequency (K-band) geodetic VLBI campaigns could be a viable approach to conduct intercontinental clock comparisons, now only possible via satellite links. This uncertainty can be reduced thanks to the planned installation of new-generation, broadband, high-frequency receivers on the involved telescopes. K/Q/W-band geodetic observations will allow an improvement of the accuracy of the resulting group delays through broad bandwidth synthesis from 20 to 100 GHz. Furthermore, the Frequency Phase Transfer (FPT) method will also be explored together with the use of PCAL systems installed at the radio telescopes to improve phase stability and thus allow a better estimation of the station clock parameters.
one_liner: Intercontinental K-band geodetic VLBI reaches 10^-15-level agreement for clock comparison against satellite and optical-clock references.
why_it_matters: It points to a new long-baseline timing-transfer channel for precision networks without relying solely on dedicated satellite links.
detailed_summary: The experiment compares hydrogen masers at the Medicina and Sejong radio telescopes using intercontinental geodetic K-band VLBI, cross-checked against satellite links and optical clocks delivered to the sites over fiber. Clock-rate differences extracted with two independent VLBI software chains agree with the other comparison methods at the 10^-15 s/s level. The authors outline a path to better precision using broadband K/Q/W-band receivers, broader bandwidth synthesis from 20 to 100 GHz, Frequency Phase Transfer, and PCAL systems. The study shows that standard geodetic observing campaigns can support high-grade clock metrology across continents.
main_result: The main result is a pilot demonstration that standard high-frequency geodetic VLBI can perform intercontinental clock comparison with agreement at the 10^-15 level.
application_angle: The technique could expand precision timing and frequency-transfer infrastructure for distributed quantum networks, navigation, and fundamental metrology.

title: Optical modelling of shaped laser pulses in plasma
abstract: This work provides a brief review of the numerical methods for modelling the optical propagation of an ultrashort, intense laser pulse in plasmas and ionizable gases. These methods are implemented in an open-source simulation toolkit Axiprop, which is now actively used for design studies in the context of laser plasma acceleration of electrons (LPA). We present two examples of such studies: optical plasma waveguide generation, and phase-locked flying-focus LPA. These tools enable the identification of complex effects impacting both energy deposition during waveguide formation, and the pulse propagation dynamics that governs wakefield structure, ultimately determining the properties of the accelerated electron bunches. The developed tools and presented simulation designs are relevant to experiments on laser plasma interactions at modern high power laser facilities.
one_liner: Open-source optical propagation models capture shaped ultrashort laser pulse dynamics in plasma waveguides and flying-focus accelerators.
why_it_matters: It provides simulation tools needed to engineer laser-plasma stages that underpin compact accelerator and high-field photonics experiments.
detailed_summary: This review and methods paper describes numerical techniques implemented in the open-source Axiprop toolkit for modeling intense ultrashort laser pulses propagating through plasmas and ionizable gases. Two application studies are highlighted: optical plasma waveguide generation and phase-locked flying-focus laser-plasma acceleration. The simulations resolve effects governing energy deposition during channel formation and pulse-evolution dynamics that set the wakefield structure and final electron-bunch properties. The framework is positioned as a practical design tool for experiments at modern high-power laser facilities.
main_result: The work delivers validated open-source modeling workflows for shaped-pulse propagation in plasma, including use cases directly relevant to waveguide formation and flying-focus acceleration.
application_angle: These tools enable experiment design and optimization for laser-plasma hardware where pulse shaping and propagation control determine usable accelerator performance.

title: Dark Matter Detection through Rydberg Atom Transducer
abstract: Ultralight bosonic dark matter with masses in the meV range, corresponding to terahertz (THz) Compton frequencies, remains largely unexplored due to the difficulty of achieving both efficient signal conversion and single-photon-sensitive detection at THz frequencies. We propose a hybrid detection architecture that integrates a dielectric haloscope, Rydberg-atom transducer, and superconducting nanowire single-photon detection within a unified cryogenic platform operating at $\lesssim 1\,\text{K}$. The dielectric haloscope converts dark matter into THz photons via phase-matched resonant enhancement, achieving form factors $C \sim 0.4$ and loaded quality factors $Q_L \sim 10^4$. A cold $^{87}$Rb ensemble then coherently up-converts the THz signal to the optical domain through six-wave mixing among Rydberg states. The intrinsic directionality and narrow bandwidth ($Δν_{\mathrm{atomic}} \sim 1\,\text{MHz}$) of this process provide extra suppression of isotropic thermal backgrounds. With 10 days of integration at $0.3\,\text{K}$, we project sensitivity to the axion-photon coupling $g_{aγγ} \sim 10^{-13}\,\mathrm{GeV}^{-1}$ at $m_a \sim 0.4\,\text{meV}$, reaching the QCD axion band and opening the THz window for searches of both axion and dark photon dark matter.
one_liner: A cryogenic hybrid detector uses Rydberg atoms to up-convert meV-scale dark-matter-induced THz photons for single-photon optical readout.
why_it_matters: It outlines a realistic path to QCD-axion sensitivity in the hard-to-access THz band by combining resonant conversion, atomic transduction, and SNSPD detection.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes an end-to-end architecture for meV ultralight bosonic dark matter detection that couples a dielectric haloscope to a cold 87Rb Rydberg ensemble and optical single-photon detectors in a sub-kelvin environment. The haloscope resonantly converts dark matter into THz photons with form factor around 0.4 and loaded Q around 10^4, while six-wave mixing among Rydberg states coherently up-converts the THz field into the optical domain. The atomic transducer adds intrinsic directionality and MHz-scale bandwidth filtering, which suppresses isotropic thermal background. With 10 days of integration at 0.3 K, the projected sensitivity reaches g_agammagamma about 10^-13 GeV^-1 near 0.4 meV.
main_result: The main result is a detector concept projected to reach QCD-axion-band sensitivity around 0.4 meV using THz-to-optical conversion in a unified cryogenic platform.
application_angle: This could open the THz dark-matter search window with hardware-compatible optical readout and single-photon sensitivity.

title: Two-parameter Family-Vicsek scaling in a dissipative XXZ spin chain
abstract: Family-Vicsek (FV) scaling provides an understanding for the growth and finite-size saturation of fluctuations in classical systems. Here, we extend the FV roughness to transferred segment magnetization after quantum quenches in a dissipative XXZ spin chain with homogeneous gain and loss, starting from a nonequilibrium steady state with finite magnetization. In the non-interacting limit, we derive a closed-form expression for the roughness in the presence of dissipation. It displays two-parameter FV scaling and smoothly interpolates between the clean ballistic behavior and the dissipation dominated scalings. For interacting chains, tensor-network simulations show that the non-dissipative ballistic growth at finite magnetization is robust, whereas the full Lindblad evolution is generically controlled by the dissipative relaxation time and exhibits a dissipation-dominated collapse.
one_liner: Transferred magnetization in a dissipative XXZ chain obeys two-parameter Family-Vicsek scaling that bridges ballistic and dissipation-dominated regimes.
why_it_matters: It gives a compact scaling framework for nonequilibrium transport under gain, loss, and interactions, useful for interpreting open-system quantum simulator data.
detailed_summary: The authors generalize Family-Vicsek roughness scaling to the transferred segment magnetization after quenches from a nonequilibrium steady state in a dissipative XXZ chain with homogeneous gain and loss. In the noninteracting limit they derive a closed-form roughness expression showing explicit two-parameter scaling, smoothly connecting clean ballistic growth to dissipation-controlled dynamics. Tensor-network simulations for interacting chains show that finite-magnetization ballistic growth survives without dissipation, but the full Lindblad evolution collapses onto scaling set by the dissipative relaxation time. The work provides a quantitative route to classify transport fluctuations in open many-body systems beyond equilibrium settings.
main_result: The main result is a two-parameter scaling law for magnetization roughness that captures the crossover from ballistic to dissipation-dominated dynamics in open XXZ chains.
application_angle: The framework can help analyze transport and fluctuation data from engineered open quantum simulators and driven spin platforms.

title: Quantum simulation of Motzkin spin chain with Rydberg atoms
abstract: Motzkin spin chain is a well-known mathematical model with connections to symmetry-protected topological phases, such as the Haldane phase, as well as to concepts in the AdS/CFT correspondence. They exhibit highly entangled ground states that violate the area law and are exceptionally difficult to simulate with conventional numerical methods. Numerical simulations of the Motzkin ground state become further challenging at large system sizes due to their high-dimensional spin structure, rendering it a natural test bed for quantum simulation with ultra-cold systems. Here, we propose a Rydberg-atom based quantum simulation scheme that effectively realizes Motzkin spins using an experimentally accessible set of parameters. We show that the resulting effective Motzkin ground state reproduces the characteristic entanglement scaling and the block-structure properties of the reduced density matrix associated with the ideal Motzkin state. Our results establish a pathway toward a concrete experimental realization of Motzkin spins beyond purely mathematical constructions, opening avenues for exploring other similar exotic non-area-law entangled phases in programmable Rydberg simulators.
one_liner: A Rydberg-atom scheme emulates the Motzkin spin chain and reproduces its unusual non-area-law entanglement structure with realistic parameters.
why_it_matters: It turns an analytically exotic but numerically hard spin model into an experimentally addressable target for programmable neutral-atom simulators.
detailed_summary: This work maps the Motzkin spin chain onto an effective model accessible in programmable Rydberg arrays, targeting a class of highly entangled states that are difficult to treat numerically at large size. Using experimentally motivated parameters, the authors show that the effective ground state reproduces hallmark features of the ideal Motzkin state, including characteristic entanglement scaling and the block structure of reduced density matrices. The proposal provides a concrete route to explore symmetry-protected and non-area-law phases in a controlled analog simulator. It also broadens the scope of Rydberg hardware toward models originating in mathematical physics rather than standard condensed-matter Hamiltonians.
main_result: The main result is an experimentally feasible neutral-atom protocol that realizes Motzkin-chain physics while preserving its distinctive entanglement signatures.
application_angle: It expands the reachable model space of Rydberg simulators and enables hardware studies of exotic entangled phases that strain classical computation.

title: A multi-ion optical clock with $\mathbf{5 \times 10^{-19}}$ uncertainty
abstract: Today's most accurate clocks are based on laser spectroscopy of electronic transitions in single trapped ions and feature fractional frequency uncertainties below $1\times10^{-18}$. Scaling these systems to multiple, simultaneously interrogated ions reduces measurement times, driving recent advances in multi-ion clocks. However, maintaining state-of-the-art systematic uncertainties while increasing the number of ions remains a central challenge. Here, we report on a multi-ion optical atomic clock with a fractional frequency uncertainty of $5.3\times10^{-19}$ and up to 10 \Sr ions. Ion-resolved state detection enables minimization of position-dependent shifts, with residual effects suppressed below the $10^{-20}$-level. Clock operation with eight to ten ions reduces the measurement time by a factor of 4.8 compared to single-ion operation. A comparison with an established \Yb single-ion clock yields an unperturbed frequency ratio of $0.6926711632159660405(20)$, with a statistical uncertainty of $0.9\times10^{-18}$ and a combined uncertainty of $2.9\times 10^{-18}$. These results demonstrate robust multi-ion clock operation with reduced averaging time and state-of-the-art accuracy.
one_liner: A multi-ion strontium optical clock reaches 5.3×10^-19 fractional uncertainty while using up to 10 ions to cut averaging time nearly fivefold.
why_it_matters: It shows that scaling trapped-ion clocks to many ions can improve throughput without sacrificing state-of-the-art accuracy, a key step for deployable precision timing.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates a multi-ion optical clock based on up to 10 trapped Sr ions with a reported fractional systematic uncertainty of 5.3×10^-19. Ion-resolved state detection is used to minimize position-dependent shifts, pushing residual effects below the 10^-20 level while preserving clock performance across larger ion numbers. Operating with eight to ten ions reduces measurement time by a factor of 4.8 relative to single-ion mode. Comparison against a Yb single-ion reference clock yields a precise unperturbed frequency ratio with sub-10^-18 statistical uncertainty.
