main_result: The main result is a ten-ion optical clock that retains ultra-low systematic uncertainty while substantially improving measurement speed over single-ion operation.
application_angle: This improves the practicality of next-generation frequency standards for precision navigation, relativistic geodesy, and fundamental-physics tests.

title: Enhanced Dark Matter Quantum Sensing via Geometric Phase
abstract: We propose a quantum sensing protocol for coupled qubit-oscillator systems that surpasses the standard quantum limit by exploiting a geometric phase for dark matter searches. Instead of letting the qubit evolve freely under a weak dark matter background, we combine large coherent displacements and squeezing operations within the evolution protocol, thereby mapping the signal onto an enhanced geometric phase. This new protocol increases the quantum Fisher information to surpass standard quantum limit and leads to a substantial improvement in dark photon and axion detection sensitivity, opening a new paradigm for cavity-based dark matter detection.
one_liner: A geometric-phase protocol with squeezing and large displacements boosts qubit-oscillator dark-matter sensing beyond the standard quantum limit.
why_it_matters: It identifies a control-native way to amplify weak axion or dark-photon signatures in cavity-based quantum sensors using operations already central to quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a sensing protocol for coupled qubit-oscillator systems in which weak dark-matter-induced signals are encoded into an enhanced geometric phase rather than simple free evolution. By combining large coherent displacements with squeezing during the interrogation sequence, the scheme increases the quantum Fisher information beyond the standard quantum limit. The analysis indicates improved sensitivity for both dark photon and axion searches in cavity-based architectures. The proposal is hardware-relevant because it relies on controllable bosonic operations available in several photonic and superconducting platforms.
main_result: The main result is a quantum metrology protocol that uses geometric phase engineering to surpass standard-quantum-limit sensitivity in dark-matter searches.
application_angle: It can directly improve cavity-based searches for ultralight dark matter by converting better control over bosonic modes into higher detection sensitivity.

title: Laser-induced creation of coherent V2 centers in bulk-grown silicon carbide
abstract: Solid-state spin defects are promising qubits for quantum network nodes. A key challenge towards larger networks is creating defects with high yield into nanophotonic devices, while maintaining good optical and spin properties. Here, we demonstrate the creation of V2 centers in nanopillars fabricated from commercial bulk-grown 4H-silicon carbide using a pulsed above-bandgap (UV) laser. We observe an eleven-fold increase in the V2 center occurrence after UV laser illumination. These laser-induced V2 centers exhibit narrow optical linewidths and spectral diffusion rates comparable to naturally occurring V2 centers in nanopillars of the same material. Furthermore, we measure a spin coherence time of $T_{2}^{\mathrm{DD}} = 3.6 \pm 0.3~\text{ms}$ under dynamical decoupling, consistent with dephasing by the nuclear-spin bath. This demonstration of the in-situ, post-fabrication generation of coherent V2 centers in nanostructures in widely available bulk-grown 4H-SiC, shows the potential for above-bandgap laser illumination for scalable defect creation in integrated photonic devices.
one_liner: UV laser writing creates coherent V2 centers in bulk-grown 4H-SiC nanopillars with much higher yield and millisecond spin coherence.
why_it_matters: It addresses a core scaling bottleneck for SiC quantum nodes by enabling post-fabrication defect creation inside nanophotonic structures without major coherence loss.
detailed_summary: This paper demonstrates post-fabrication creation of V2 spin defects in nanopillars made from commercial bulk-grown 4H-SiC using pulsed above-bandgap UV laser illumination. The process increases V2 occurrence by roughly eleven times, while the generated centers retain narrow optical linewidths and spectral diffusion comparable to naturally occurring defects in similar structures. Under dynamical decoupling, the authors measure T2 of 3.6 ± 0.3 ms, consistent with nuclear-spin-bath-limited dephasing. The result is significant because it combines scalable defect generation with optical and spin properties compatible with integrated quantum photonic devices.
main_result: The main result is high-yield, in-situ generation of coherent V2 centers in SiC nanostructures with optical quality and millisecond spin coherence suitable for quantum devices.
application_angle: This supports scalable fabrication of SiC-based quantum network nodes, spin sensors, and integrated photonic quantum hardware.

title: Quantum correlations in prepare-and-measure scenarios and their semi-device-independent applications
abstract: A key aspect in quantum information is to understand the advantage offered by quantum systems over classical ones in communication tasks. In recent years, a fundamental approach to this problem has been developed, focusing on quantum correlations in prepare-and-measure scenarios. Inspired by the developments in Bell nonlocality and device-independent information processing, this line of research aims to characterize the possibilities and limits of quantum systems for communication, in particular to precisely capture the advantage they offer over classical systems. In addition to fundamental insights, these ideas also underpin the concept of semi-device-independent quantum information processing. Exploring trade-offs between security, performance and ease-of-implementation, this approach opens promising directions for novel quantum information processing technologies and devices. A number of protocols and proof-of-principle demonstrations have been reported in recent years, in particular for quantum randomness certification and key distribution. Here, we provide a comprehensive introduction to quantum prepare-and-measure correlations and semi-device independent applications.
one_liner: A review of prepare-and-measure quantum correlations that connects communication advantages to semi-device-independent randomness and key-distribution protocols.
why_it_matters: It consolidates the theory behind practical semi-device-independent quantum primitives that relax device trust assumptions while remaining experimentally implementable.
detailed_summary: The paper surveys quantum correlations in prepare-and-measure scenarios, where one party encodes information into constrained quantum systems and another measures them. It emphasizes how dimension bounds and communication constraints enable semi-device-independent security statements without full Bell-nonlocality assumptions. The review ties these correlation frameworks to concrete protocols for certified randomness and quantum key distribution, summarizing known performance, security, and implementation tradeoffs. It is useful as a technical map of the field rather than a single new experiment or theorem.
main_result: The work provides a comprehensive technical framework linking prepare-and-measure quantum correlations to semi-device-independent information-processing applications.
application_angle: It helps guide deployable quantum cryptography and randomness systems that avoid the overhead of fully device-independent architectures.

title: Characterization and Comparison of Energy Relaxation in Fluxonium Qubits
abstract: Fluxonium superconducting qubits have demonstrated long coherence times and high single- and two-qubit gate fidelities, making them a favorable building block for superconducting quantum processors. We investigate the dominant limitations to fluxonium qubit energy relaxation time $T_1$ using a set of eight planar, aluminum-on-silicon qubits. We find that a circuit-based model for capacitive dielectric loss best captures the frequency dependence of $T_1$, which we analyze within both a two-level and a six-level energy relaxation model. We convert the measured $T_1$ into an effective capacitive quality factor $Q_\mathrm{C}^{\mathrm{eff}}$ to compare qubits on equal footing, accounting for independently estimated contributions from $1/f$ flux noise and radiative loss to the control and readout circuitry. We apply this methodology to compare qubits from two fabrication processes: a baseline process and one that applies a fluorine-based wet treatment prior to Josephson junction deposition. We resolve a small improvement of (13.8 $\pm$ 8.4$)\%$ in the process mean $Q_\mathrm{C}^{\mathrm{eff}}$, indicating that the fluorine treatment may have reduced loss from the metal-substrate interface, but did not address the primary source of loss in these fluxonium qubits.
one_liner: An eight-device study shows capacitive dielectric loss best explains fluxonium T1 trends and quantifies a modest benefit from fluorine surface treatment.
why_it_matters: It identifies the dominant relaxation channel in planar fluxonium and offers a normalized comparison method for fabrication-process improvements.
detailed_summary: The authors measure energy relaxation in eight planar aluminum-on-silicon fluxonium qubits and compare the frequency dependence of T1 against circuit-based loss models. A capacitive dielectric-loss model best matches the data, analyzed with both two-level and six-level relaxation treatments. They convert measured T1 values into an effective capacitive quality factor that removes estimated contributions from flux noise and radiative loss, enabling process-level comparison. Using this metric, they find a small 13.8 +/- 8.4% mean improvement from a fluorine-based wet treatment, suggesting reduced interface loss but not elimination of the primary dissipation source.
main_result: Capacitive dielectric loss is identified as the leading explanation for observed fluxonium T1 behavior, with fluorine pretreatment giving only a modest improvement in effective capacitive Q.
application_angle: The analysis informs fabrication and materials choices for scaling higher-coherence superconducting processors based on fluxonium.

title: Rethinking Quantum Networking with Advances in Fiber Technology
abstract: Recent comparisons of quantum repeater protocols have highlighted the strong near-term potential of multiplexed two-way architectures for long-distance quantum communication. At the same time, advances in hollow-core fiber (HCF) technology motivate a re-examination of the physical transmission medium as an architectural lever in quantum network design. In this work, we compare emerging anti-resonant HCFs against conventional silica single-mode fibers (SMFs) in multiplexed two-way quantum repeater networks. We evaluate their performance under both telecom and memory-native transmission, accounting for frequency-conversion overheads, coupling efficiencies, memory decoherence, and operational noise. We find that HCF significantly outperforms SMF across a wide range of regimes. With memory-native transmission, HCF yields up to an order of magnitude improvement in secret-key rate per channel use under realistic conversion efficiencies. Even at telecom wavelengths, HCF enables larger optimal repeater spacing, improving rate--cost tradeoffs and reducing repeater requirements. We further quantify the role of memory quality, hardware efficiency, detector and conversion losses, and two-qubit gate noise in shaping these gains. These results show that recent advances in HCF materially expand the design space of practical terrestrial quantum repeater networks.
one_liner: Hollow-core fiber can materially outperform standard silica fiber in multiplexed quantum repeater networks, especially for memory-native transmission.
why_it_matters: It shows the physical transmission medium itself can shift repeater spacing, rate-cost tradeoffs, and secret-key performance in near-term quantum networks.
detailed_summary: This work compares anti-resonant hollow-core fibers with conventional single-mode silica fibers in multiplexed two-way repeater architectures. The analysis includes telecom and memory-native transmission, frequency-conversion penalties, coupling efficiencies, memory decoherence, detector loss, and gate noise. Across realistic parameter ranges, hollow-core fiber improves network performance, with up to an order-of-magnitude gain in secret-key rate per channel use for memory-native transmission. It also supports larger optimal repeater spacing at telecom wavelengths, reducing repeater count for a given link budget.
main_result: Emerging hollow-core fiber yields substantially better secret-key-rate and repeater-spacing performance than standard single-mode fiber in realistic multiplexed repeater models.
application_angle: The results directly affect terrestrial quantum-network engineering by identifying a transmission medium that can lower hardware overhead while improving secure-link throughput.

title: Very sensitive vapor-cell quasi-DC atomic E-field sensor
abstract: We report several technical approaches that significantly improve the performance of a vapor-cell atomic electrometer operating in the quasi-DC frequency domain ($\ll$ 1 kHz). With a very small active volume of approximately 11 mm$^3$ inside the vapor cell, we demonstrated a noise floor for electric field (E-field) sensitivity ranging from 0.2 to 7.7 mV/m$\sqrt{\rm Hz}$ for a frequency band of 1--100 Hz. Our work utilizes only a bare vapor cell for electrometry, without any metal parts or electrodes, to ensure minimal distortion of the measured E-field and to minimize the effective sensing volume for high spatial resolution. The E-field-sensitive atomic state (Rydberg state) is excited and read out optically, maximizing the simplicity of the system design and enabling the miniaturization of quasi-DC E-field sensors for potential applications, such as diagnostics of electronics without physical contact, communications in and below the super-low frequency (SLF) band, proximity detection, remote activity surveillance, tracing charge signatures, and research in bioscience and geoscience.
one_liner: A bare vapor-cell Rydberg electrometer achieves quasi-DC electric-field sensitivity down to 0.2 mV/m/sqrt(Hz) in a tiny 11 mm^3 sensing volume.
why_it_matters: It advances compact, minimally invasive electric-field sensing for low-frequency diagnostics, surveillance, and contact-free measurement applications.
detailed_summary: The authors improve a vapor-cell atomic electrometer operating far below 1 kHz by combining technical upgrades that suppress noise and preserve a small active volume. Using an optically excited and read out Rydberg state in a bare cell with no electrodes or metal structures, they report 0.2 to 7.7 mV/m/sqrt(Hz) sensitivity across 1-100 Hz. The design minimizes field distortion and keeps the sensor footprint small, enabling high spatial resolution. The all-optical architecture also favors miniaturization and remote deployment.
main_result: The sensor demonstrates sub-mV/m/sqrt(Hz) quasi-DC electric-field sensitivity in a compact, electrode-free vapor-cell platform.
application_angle: This could enable non-contact electronics diagnostics, low-frequency communications, proximity sensing, and remote field monitoring where conventional probes are intrusive.

title: Quantum-classical dynamics of Rashba spin-orbit coupling
abstract: Mixed quantum-classical models are widely used to reduce the computational cost of fully quantum simulations. However, their general applicability across different classes of problems remains an open question. Here, we address this issue for systems featuring spin-orbit coupling. In particular, we study the interaction dynamics of quantum spin-1/2 and classical orbital momentum in one-dimensional models of Rashba nanowires. We tackle this problem by resorting to a new quantum-classical Hamiltonian model that, unlike conventional approaches, retains the Heisenberg principle and captures correlation effects beyond the common Ehrenfest approach. Based on Koopman wavefunctions in classical mechanics, the new model was recently implemented numerically via a particle scheme -- the koopmon method -- which is extended here to treat spin-orbit coupling. We apply the koopmon method to study the quantum-classical dynamics of nanowire models, with and without the presence of a harmonic potential and in both Rashba-dominated (strong coupling) and Zeeman-dominated (weak coupling) regimes. Considering realistic semiconductor parameters, the results are contrasted with both fully quantum and quantum-classical Ehrenfest dynamics. In the absence of external potential, the koopmon method qualitatively reproduces the features of the fully quantum evolution for all coupling regimes. While it exhibits a slight loss in spin accuracy compared to Ehrenfest simulations, the latter fail to capture the orbital dynamics. In the presence of a harmonic potential, the koopmon scheme reproduces the full quantum results with accuracy levels that are unachievable by the Ehrenfest model in both quantum and classical sectors. We conclude by presenting a test case that exhibits the formation of cat-like states.
one_liner: An extended koopmon quantum-classical method tracks Rashba nanowire spin-orbit dynamics more faithfully than Ehrenfest dynamics in key regimes.
why_it_matters: It offers a more reliable reduced-cost simulation tool for spin-orbit-coupled nanostructures relevant to spintronics and quantum-device modeling.
detailed_summary: The paper extends the koopmon quantum-classical framework to one-dimensional Rashba nanowires with spin-1/2 and classical orbital motion. Unlike standard Ehrenfest dynamics, the method preserves Heisenberg constraints and captures correlation effects, then is benchmarked against full quantum simulations with and without harmonic confinement. In free evolution it reproduces the main fully quantum features across both Rashba- and Zeeman-dominated regimes, while Ehrenfest misses important orbital behavior. With a harmonic potential, the koopmon approach matches full quantum results in both spin and orbital sectors and even reproduces a cat-state test case.
main_result: The extended koopmon scheme significantly outperforms Ehrenfest dynamics for coupled spin-orbit nanowire evolution, especially in confined systems.
application_angle: Better approximate simulation of spin-orbit devices can accelerate design of semiconductor quantum hardware and nanoscale sensing architectures without full quantum cost.

title: Coherent multi-dimensional widefield microscopy
abstract: We present a widefield two-dimensional electronic spectroscopy microscope (2DESM) that integrates multidimensional coherent spectroscopy with optical imaging, enabling femtosecond temporal and micrometer spatial resolution. The broadband coverage (1.4-1.8 eV) allows the direct acquisition of spatially resolved two-dimensional electronic spectroscopy (2DES) maps of relevant near infrared excitations without the need for spatial scanning. By capturing both spectral and spatial domains simultaneously, 2DESM overcomes limitations of pump-probe microscopy and scanning 2DES, providing access to decoherence dynamics, inhomogeneous broadening, and coherent coupling in heterogeneous systems. As a proof-of-concept we performed 2DESM measurements on bilayer WSe2 encapsulated in hBN, revealing distinct spatial variations in excitonic dynamics. These results validate the ability of 2DESM to link local environments with ultrafast coherent processes and establish 2DESM as a versatile platform for probing quantum coherence, many-body interactions, and non-local energy transfer in two-dimensional materials, heterostructures, and micrometer-scale optoelectronic devices.
one_liner: A widefield 2D electronic spectroscopy microscope captures femtosecond coherent dynamics with micrometer spatial resolution and no spatial scanning.
why_it_matters: It brings multidimensional coherent spectroscopy into an imaging format suited to heterogeneous quantum materials and optoelectronic devices.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a widefield two-dimensional electronic spectroscopy microscope that simultaneously resolves spectral, temporal, and spatial information. Covering 1.4-1.8 eV, the instrument acquires spatially resolved 2DES maps without raster scanning, enabling direct access to decoherence, inhomogeneous broadening, and coherent coupling across heterogeneous samples. As a proof of concept, measurements on hBN-encapsulated bilayer WSe2 reveal position-dependent excitonic dynamics. The platform therefore merges ultrafast coherent spectroscopy with microscopy in a way conventional pump-probe imaging cannot.
main_result: The system demonstrates scan-free multidimensional coherent imaging with femtosecond and micrometer resolution, resolving spatial variations in excitonic dynamics.
application_angle: It provides a practical tool for characterizing coherence and energy-transfer pathways in quantum materials, heterostructures, and microscale photonic devices.

title: Quantum photonic neural networks in time
abstract: We introduce the architecture and timing algorithm to realize a time-bin-encoded quantum photonic neural network (QPNN): a reconfigurable nonlinear photonic circuit inspired by the brain and trained to process quantum information. Unlike the typical spatially-encoded QPNN, time-encoded networks require the same number of photonic elements (e.g. phase shifters or switches) regardless of their size or depth. Here, we present a model of such a network and show how to include imperfections such as losses, routing errors and most notably distinguishable photons. As an example, we train the QPNN to realize a controlled-NOT gate, based on a hypothetical ideal Kerr nonlinearity. We then extend our model to a realistic two-photon nonlinearity due to scattering from a single, semiconductor quantum dot coupled to a photonic waveguide. We show that, using this realistic nonlinearity, the QPNN can be trained to act as a Bell-state analyzer which operates with a fidelity of 0.96 and at a rate only limited by losses. We further show that time gating can raise this fidelity to over 0.99, while still maintaining an efficiency exceeding 0.9. Overall, this work lays a framework for the first QPNN encoded in time, and provides a clear path to the scaling of these networks.
one_liner: A time-bin quantum photonic neural network is trained for quantum gates and Bell-state analysis using realistic quantum-dot nonlinearities.
why_it_matters: It outlines a hardware-efficient route to scalable quantum photonic processing, with high-fidelity Bell analysis using components compatible with integrated nanophotonics.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a time-encoded quantum photonic neural network architecture in which circuit size and depth do not require more photonic elements, unlike spatially encoded schemes. The model explicitly incorporates loss, routing errors, and photon distinguishability, then trains the network for tasks including a controlled-NOT gate under an ideal Kerr nonlinearity. It further replaces the ideal nonlinearity with a realistic two-photon interaction from a semiconductor quantum dot coupled to a waveguide and shows Bell-state analysis with 0.96 fidelity. With time gating, the fidelity exceeds 0.99 while efficiency remains above 0.9, indicating a practical path toward scalable time-multiplexed quantum photonic processors.
platform_or_system: time-bin integrated photonic network with quantum-dot waveguide nonlinearity
main_result: A realistic time-bin QPNN can implement Bell-state analysis with 0.96 fidelity, improving to above 0.99 with time gating while keeping efficiency over 0.9.
application_angle: The approach reduces hardware overhead for larger quantum photonic circuits and targets deployable integrated modules for quantum information processing and photonic networking.

title: Precision bounds for frequency estimation under collective dephasing and open-loop control
abstract: Dephasing noise is a ubiquitous source of decoherence in current atomic sensors. We address the problem of entanglement-assisted frequency estimation subject to classical dephasing noise with full spatial correlations (collective) and arbitrary temporal correlations. Our contributions are threefold. (i) We derive rigorous, state-independent bounds on the achievable estimation precision, showing how they are entirely determined by the short-time behavior of the decoherence function. For temporally uncorrelated (Markovian) dephasing, precision is limited by a probe-independent constant. For temporally correlated stationary noise, the bound approaches the noiseless limit for classical states, precluding any asymptotic quantum advantage. (ii) We show that these scaling bounds are tight, by constructing generalized Ramsey protocols that saturate them. These optimal protocols use squeezing at the input and before readout, both of which are available in state-of-the-art atomic interferometers. Implementing a perfect-echo protocol, which reaches Heisenberg scaling in the absence of noise, remains optimal in this noisy setting, irrespective of the noise temporal correlations. (iii) We prove that arbitrary collective open-loop control cannot lift the no-go for super-classical precision scaling under either Markovian or colored stationary noise, highlighting the detrimental nature of full spatial correlations. In the latter case, temporal correlations may nonetheless enable constant-factor improvements over the standard quantum limit, which may still be important in practical metrological scenarios.
one_liner: Rigorous precision bounds show how collective dephasing limits entanglement-enhanced frequency estimation even with arbitrary open-loop control.
why_it_matters: It defines realistic metrology limits for atomic sensors under correlated noise and identifies implementable Ramsey-style protocols that actually attain those limits.
detailed_summary: The authors derive state-independent precision bounds for frequency estimation under fully spatially correlated dephasing with arbitrary temporal correlations, showing that short-time decoherence behavior fully determines asymptotic scaling. For Markovian dephasing, the achievable precision is capped by a probe-independent constant, while for stationary colored noise the asymptotic quantum advantage over classical scaling disappears. They also construct generalized Ramsey protocols using input and readout squeezing that saturate these bounds and prove that arbitrary collective open-loop control cannot restore super-classical scaling. The analysis is directly relevant to current squeezed atomic interferometers and clarifies when temporal correlations can still provide useful constant-factor gains.
main_result: Optimal squeezed Ramsey-type protocols saturate the derived precision bounds, and no collective open-loop control can recover asymptotic super-classical scaling under fully correlated dephasing.
application_angle: These results set realistic design targets for precision clocks and atom-based sensors operating in correlated-noise environments common in practical metrology.

title: Visible Spectral-Domain Optical Coherence Tomography for Photonic Integrated Circuits Characterization
abstract: Visible photonic integrated circuits underpin applications ranging from AR/VR to quantum control, yet lack a high-resolution, nondestructive diagnostic comparable to the optical frequency-domain reflectometry used in infrared silicon photonics. Here we adapt spectral-domain optical coherence tomography to measure guided-mode back-reflections in visible PICs. Broadband visible light injected into a circuit generates back-reflections that interfere with a depth-referencing local oscillator, and the resulting spectral fringes are recorded on a spectrometer. We validate the approach by resolving multiple round-trip echoes in a waveguide-coupled ring resonator using only single-port access. We then extend it to circuits integrated with diamond quantum micro-chiplets, clearly resolving input and output facets as well as PIC--QMC transition regions. The system achieves shot-noise-limited sensitivity, 50 dB dynamic range, 8 um axial resolution in silicon nitride, and a 2 mm imaging depth at 6 dB roll-off. SD-OCT therefore provides a practical, high-resolution diagnostic for visible PICs that uses a broadband probe source and requires only single-port optical access, enabling rapid characterization of propagation loss, backscattering, and dispersion.
one_liner: Visible spectral-domain OCT is adapted to noninvasively characterize visible photonic integrated circuits and diamond-chiplet interfaces with single-port access.
why_it_matters: It provides a practical diagnostic for visible PICs and quantum-chip interfaces where high-resolution, nondestructive metrology has been missing.
detailed_summary: This work repurposes spectral-domain optical coherence tomography to measure guided-mode back-reflections in visible photonic integrated circuits using broadband visible illumination and a reference arm. The method resolves multiple round-trip echoes in a waveguide-coupled ring resonator from only one optical port, then extends to PICs integrated with diamond quantum micro-chiplets. It clearly identifies input and output facets and PIC-QMC transition regions while reaching shot-noise-limited sensitivity, 50 dB dynamic range, 8 um axial resolution in silicon nitride, and 2 mm imaging depth at 6 dB roll-off. The technique enables rapid extraction of loss, backscattering, and dispersion in visible quantum-photonic hardware without destructive sectioning.
platform_or_system: visible silicon-nitride PICs with diamond quantum micro-chiplets
main_result: The system demonstrates single-port, shot-noise-limited OCT characterization of visible PICs with 8 um axial resolution, 50 dB dynamic range, and 2 mm imaging depth.
application_angle: It can streamline fabrication feedback and failure analysis for visible and diamond-integrated quantum photonic chips used in sensing, control, and networking.

title: Predicting quantum ground-state energy by data-driven Koopman analysis of variational parameter nonlinear dynamics
abstract: In recent years, the application of machine learning to physics has been actively explored. In this paper, we study a method for estimating the ground-state energy of quantum Hamiltonians by applying data-driven Koopman analysis within the framework of variational wave functions. Koopman theory is a framework for analyzing the nonlinear dynamics of vectors, in which the dynamics are linearized by lifting the vectors to functions defined over the original vector space. We focus on the fact that the imaginary-time Schrödinger equation, when restricted to a variational wave function, is described by a nonlinear time evolution of the variational parameter vector. We collect sample points of this nonlinear dynamics at parameter configurations where the discrepancy between the true imaginary-time dynamics and the dynamics on the variational manifold is small, and perform data-driven continuous Koopman analysis. Within our formulation, the ground-state energy is reduced to the leading eigenvalue of a differential operator known as the Koopman generator. As a concrete example, we generate samples for the four-site transverse-field Ising model and estimate the ground-state energy using extended dynamic mode decomposition (EDMD). Furthermore, as an extension of this framework, we formulate the method for the case where the variational wave function is given by a uniform matrix product state on an infinite chain. By employing computational techniques developed within the framework of the time-dependent variational principle, all the quantities required for our analysis, including error estimation, can be computed efficiently in such systems. Since our approach provides predictions for the ground-state energy even when the true ground state lies outside the variational manifold, it is expected to complement conventional variational methods.
one_liner: A Koopman-analysis framework predicts quantum ground-state energies from nonlinear variational-parameter dynamics beyond the variational manifold itself.
why_it_matters: It offers a data-driven way to improve energy estimation from variational quantum states, potentially extending useful inference when standard variational ansatze are biased.
detailed_summary: The paper maps imaginary-time evolution of variational parameters onto a Koopman-generator problem, where the ground-state energy is inferred from the leading eigenvalue of a linear operator learned from sampled nonlinear dynamics. Sample points are chosen in regions where the variational manifold closely follows the true imaginary-time Schrödinger evolution, and extended dynamic mode decomposition is used for estimation. The method is demonstrated on the four-site transverse-field Ising model and then generalized to uniform matrix product states on infinite chains using time-dependent variational principle machinery for efficient evaluation. A key feature is that it can predict energies even when the exact ground state is outside the chosen variational manifold.
platform_or_system: variational quantum many-body states and uniform MPS
main_result: Ground-state energy can be estimated from learned Koopman-generator spectra of variational imaginary-time dynamics, including cases where the true state lies outside the ansatz manifold.
application_angle: The method could improve practical quantum simulation workflows by extracting better energy estimates from imperfect variational models used in hardware-aware or tensor-network settings.

title: Efficient Preparation of Graph States using the Quotient-Augmented Strong Split Tree
abstract: Graph states are a key resource for measurement-based quantum computation and quantum networking, but state-preparation costs limit their practical use. Graph states related by local complement (LC) operations are equivalent up to single-qubit Clifford gates; one may reduce entangling resources by preparing a favorable LC-equivalent representative. However, exhaustive optimization over the LC orbit is not scalable. We address this problem using the split decomposition and its quotient-augmented strong split tree (QASST). For several families of distance-hereditary (DH) graphs, we use the QASST to characterize LC orbits and identify representatives with reduced controlled-Z count or preparation circuit depth. We also introduce a split-fuse construction for arbitrary DH graph states, achieving linear scaling with respect to entangling gates, time steps, and auxiliary qubits. Beyond the DH setting, we discuss a generalized divide-and-conquer split-fuse strategy and a simple greedy heuristic for generic graphs based on triangle enumeration. Together, these methods outperform direct implementations on sufficiently large graphs, providing a scalable alternative to brute-force optimization.
one_liner: A graph-theoretic QASST method finds lower-cost graph-state preparations and enables linear-scaling constructions for distance-hereditary states.
why_it_matters: It reduces entangling-gate count and depth for graph-state generation, a key bottleneck in measurement-based computing and quantum networking.
detailed_summary: The authors use the quotient-augmented strong split tree to analyze local-complementation orbits of graph states and identify representatives with reduced controlled-Z count or reduced preparation depth. For distance-hereditary graph families, this provides a structured alternative to brute-force optimization over LC-equivalent states. They also introduce a split-fuse construction that prepares arbitrary distance-hereditary graph states with linear scaling in entangling gates, time steps, and auxiliary qubits. Beyond that setting, they outline a generalized divide-and-conquer strategy and a triangle-based greedy heuristic for more generic graphs.
main_result: QASST-based optimization and split-fuse constructions yield scalable graph-state preparation with lower entangling cost and linear-resource scaling for distance-hereditary graphs.
application_angle: The methods can cut resource overhead in distributed entanglement generation and measurement-based protocols where graph-state preparation dominates runtime and error budget.

title: Inverse-Designed Metasurfaces for Compact Optical Skyrmion Generation with High Topological Fidelity
abstract: Optical skyrmions are structured vector fields with nontrivial polarization topology and subwavelength-scale features. One common approach to generating optical skyrmions is the superposition of a zeroth-order Bessel beam and a higher-order Bessel beam carrying orbital angular momentum, with each beam possessing an orthogonal circular polarization state. However, creating such complex beams typically requires bulky free-space optical setups; therefore, recent efforts have focused on compact optical skyrmion generators based on metasurfaces. Nevertheless, achieving the degrees of freedom required for simultaneous phase and polarization control remains challenging because of the limited design flexibility of conventional meta-atoms. Here, we address this challenge by employing an inverse-design approach and demonstrate a single-layer metasurface that generates high-fidelity optical skyrmions. We employ an adjoint-based topology-optimization method to design a silicon metasurface that converts an incident beam into an optical skyrmion without the need for additional optical components. The optimized metasurface generates an optical skyrmion with skyrmion number $(N_\mathrm{sk}) = 0.970$. This work demonstrates that inverse design can be a promising route to compact skyrmion generators, and our approach provides a basis for near-field particle manipulation and the generation of independent topological bits in dense photonic integration.
one_liner: An inverse-designed single-layer silicon metasurface generates compact optical skyrmions with high topological fidelity and no extra optics.
why_it_matters: It shows a manufacturable path to integrated generation of topological optical fields for dense photonic systems, manipulation, and information encoding.
detailed_summary: This work uses adjoint-based topology optimization to design a single-layer silicon metasurface that converts an incident beam directly into an optical skyrmion. Unlike conventional meta-atom approaches, the inverse-designed structure provides the simultaneous phase and polarization control needed for high-fidelity skyrmion formation without bulky free-space optics. The reported device achieves a skyrmion number of 0.970, indicating strong preservation of the intended polarization topology. The result points to compact, integrated sources of structured light with nontrivial topology for on-chip or near-field photonic functions.
main_result: A single-layer inverse-designed silicon metasurface generates optical skyrmions with skyrmion number 0.970 without requiring additional optical components.
application_angle: Compact skyrmion generators could support dense photonic integration for topological bit generation, near-field trapping, and structured-light links in advanced optical systems.

title: Ultrawide Bandwidth Optomechanical Magnetometry Using Flux Concentration
abstract: Low-frequency magnetic fields carry vital information for neuroscience, navigation, and Earth science. However, they are generally weak, making it challenging to measure them with compact, room-temperature magnetometers. To overcome this challenge, we combine an on-chip optomechanical magnetometer with a high-permeability flux concentrator. Beyond boosting sensitivity and bandwidth, exploiting the concentrator's nonlinear response converts low-frequency magnetic fluctuations into higher-frequency signals where the sensor is intrinsically most responsive. This sidesteps the technical noise that has long constrained the application of optomechanical magnetometry at low frequencies. Our measurements show order-of-magnitude improvements in sensitivity and extend performance into the sub-hertz regime, achieving below 20 nT Hz$^{-1/2}$ down to 3 Hz and less than 100 nT Hz$^{-1/2}$ at 0.1 Hz. Because this approach requires no redesign of the underlying architecture, it can be readily applied across magnetometer technologies, opening the way to practical low-frequency sensing for applications from brain activity mapping to undersea navigation and biomedical diagnostics.
one_liner: An optomechanical chip plus a high-permeability flux concentrator enables room-temperature magnetic sensing from sub-hertz to kilohertz bands.
why_it_matters: It pushes compact low-frequency magnetometry into regimes relevant for biomagnetism, navigation, and geophysical sensing without redesigning the core sensor.
detailed_summary: The work combines an on-chip optomechanical magnetometer with a high-permeability flux concentrator to both amplify magnetic fields and exploit nonlinear upconversion of low-frequency fluctuations. That shifts signal content into frequency ranges where the optomechanical device has higher intrinsic response and lower technical noise. Experimentally, the approach improves sensitivity by about an order of magnitude and reaches below 20 nT Hz^-1/2 at 3 Hz and below 100 nT Hz^-1/2 at 0.1 Hz. Because the concentrator is external to the sensor architecture, the method is broadly portable across magnetometer platforms.
platform_or_system: On-chip optomechanical magnetometer with flux concentrator
main_result: The system demonstrates sub-hertz magnetic-field sensing with below 20 nT Hz^-1/2 at 3 Hz and below 100 nT Hz^-1/2 at 0.1 Hz at room temperature.
application_angle: This offers a practical path to compact low-frequency magnetometers for brain-activity mapping, undersea navigation, biomedical diagnostics, and Earth-field measurements.

title: Machine vision with small numbers of detected photons per inference
abstract: Machine vision, including object recognition and image reconstruction, is a central technology in many consumer devices and scientific instruments. The design of machine-vision systems has been revolutionized by the adoption of end-to-end optimization, in which the optical front end and the post-processing back end are jointly optimized. However, while machine vision currently works extremely well in moderate-light or bright-light situations -- where a camera may detect thousands of photons per pixel and billions of photons per frame -- it is far more challenging in very low-light situations. We introduce photon-aware neuromorphic sensing (PANS), an approach for end-to-end optimization in highly photon-starved scenarios. The training incorporates knowledge of the low photon budget and the stochastic nature of light detection when the average number of photons per pixel is near or less than 1. We report a proof-of-principle experimental demonstration in which we performed low-light image classification using PANS, achieving 73% (82%) accuracy on FashionMNIST with an average of only 4.9 (17) detected photons in total per inference, and 86% (97%) on MNIST with 8.6 (29) detected photons -- orders of magnitude more photon-efficient than conventional approaches. We also report simulation studies showing how PANS could be applied to other classification, event-detection, and image-reconstruction tasks. By taking into account the statistics of measurement results for non-classical states or alternative sensing hardware, PANS could in principle be adapted to enable high-accuracy results in quantum and other photon-starved setups.
one_liner: Photon-aware neuromorphic sensing performs image inference with only a few detected photons by training optics and post-processing for shot-noise-dominated data.
why_it_matters: It shows usable machine vision at photon budgets orders of magnitude below conventional systems, relevant to low-light imaging and quantum-limited detection.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces photon-aware neuromorphic sensing (PANS), an end-to-end optimization framework that explicitly incorporates Poisson detection statistics under extremely low photon budgets. The authors experimentally demonstrate low-light classification, achieving 73% and 82% accuracy on FashionMNIST with 4.9 and 17 detected photons per inference, and 86% and 97% on MNIST with 8.6 and 29 photons. The method jointly optimizes the optical front end and digital back end for inference rather than treating sensing and classification separately. Simulation results further indicate applicability to reconstruction and event-detection tasks, including future extensions to nonclassical-light or quantum sensing setups.
main_result: The experiment achieves useful image-classification accuracy with single-digit to tens-of-photons total detections per inference, far surpassing conventional low-light approaches in photon efficiency.
application_angle: The approach is directly relevant to ultra-low-light cameras, scientific instruments, and quantum imaging hardware where photons are scarce and inference must still be reliable.

title: Experimental Demonstration of a Brachistochrone Nonadiabatic Holonomic Quantum-Gate Scheme in a Trapped Ion
abstract: Nonadiabatic holonomic quantum computation (NHQC) offers intrinsic resilience to certain control imperfections. However, conventional nonadiabatic holonomic protocols are constrained by the fixed-pulse-area condition, which limits flexibility and prolongs duration of small-angle gates. Here we experimentally demonstrate a universal brachistochrone nonadiabatic holonomic quantum gate scheme in a trapped 40Ca+ ion, and realized the construction of pX gate under the conventional NHQC, brachistochrone NHQC (BNHQC) and composite BNHQC (CBNHQC) protocols. By characterizing the performance of gate performance in the presence of dissipation, Rabi-frequency errors and detuning errors, we show that BNHQC and CBNHQC outperform conventional NHQC, and BNHQC can offer a favorable balance between operation speed and robustness. It further shows that keeping high fidelity and strong robustness need decrease the accumulated population of excited state in the evolution process. These results highlight nonadiabatic holonomic computation as a practical route toward fast and robust quantum gates in trapped-ion platforms.
one_liner: A trapped-ion experiment demonstrates brachistochrone nonadiabatic holonomic gates that are faster and more robust than standard NHQC protocols.
why_it_matters: Fast, error-resilient holonomic gates address a central hardware bottleneck in trapped-ion quantum computing: improving fidelity without sacrificing speed.
detailed_summary: Using a single trapped 40Ca+ ion, the authors implement a universal brachistochrone nonadiabatic holonomic quantum-gate scheme and compare it against conventional NHQC and composite BNHQC protocols. They realize pX gates and characterize performance under dissipation, Rabi-frequency errors, and detuning errors. The brachistochrone variant improves the speed-robustness tradeoff relative to standard NHQC, while the composite form further enhances robustness. The results also connect high fidelity to reduced accumulated excited-state population during the gate trajectory.
main_result: The experiment shows that brachistochrone nonadiabatic holonomic gates outperform conventional NHQC in robustness while preserving faster operation for small-angle gates.
application_angle: This strengthens trapped-ion hardware by providing a practical gate-design strategy for scalable processors that need both low error rates and short gate times.

title: Numerical field optimization for enhanced efficiency in time-reversible gradient computation of open-source GPU-accelerated FDTD simulations
abstract: Finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) simulations often involve physical quantities spanning multiple orders of magnitude, such as the speed of light or electromagnetic field amplitudes. The standard practice for maintaining numerical accuracy in many FDTD implementations is to use 32-bit or 64-bit floating-point values to represent the electric and magnetic fields. However, this approach is not always optimal when recording field values, particularly during time-reversible gradient computation where electric and magnetic field values need to be saved at the boundary of the simulation domain. Since this memory bottleneck is often the limiting factor in time-reversible inverse design for nanophotonics, we present two field optimizations for enhancing memory efficiency in FDTD simulations. Using a smaller bit-width representation of field values as well as interpolation, we achieve similar accuracy at lower memory cost. This approach is particularly beneficial for GPU-accelerated computing, where reduced-precision data types are increasingly preferred due to their computational efficiency and prevalence in machine learning frameworks. We integrate our approach into FDTDX, an open-source, differentiable FDTD solver that natively supports time-reversible gradient computation. Our approach is especially important for future developments towards large-scale open-source simulations, which are critical for advancing computational nanophotonic applications.
one_liner: Reduced-precision field storage and interpolation cut memory costs for time-reversible adjoint FDTD without materially degrading accuracy.
why_it_matters: Memory is the main bottleneck in large inverse-designed nanophotonic simulations, so this directly improves feasible problem size and GPU efficiency.
detailed_summary: The paper addresses the boundary-field storage bottleneck in time-reversible gradient computation for differentiable FDTD simulations. It proposes two numerical optimizations: lower-bit-width field representations and interpolation-based recording, both aimed at preserving gradient accuracy while reducing memory usage. The methods are integrated into FDTDX, an open-source GPU-accelerated differentiable FDTD solver with native time-reversible gradients. This enables more scalable inverse-design workflows for photonic structures on modern accelerator hardware.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that compressed field recording strategies can significantly reduce memory overhead in time-reversible FDTD while maintaining similar simulation accuracy.
application_angle: The work makes large-scale open-source nanophotonic design and optimization more practical for real devices such as sensors, metasurfaces, and integrated photonic components.

title: Parameter trajectory engineering for state transfer and quantum sensing in non-Hermitian two-level systems
abstract: Exceptional points (EPs) in non-Hermitian systems give rise to enhanced sensitivity and chiral state transfer, which are important for quantum technologies. Although parameter trajectories encircling EPs can control symmetric and chiral state transfer, their robustness against practical perturbations and their role in quantum sensing remain largely unexplored. Here, we study three time-modulated parameter loops in a non-Hermitian two-level system to show how trajectory design governs state-transfer symmetry, robustness, and sensing performance. Trajectories avoiding the EP support robust symmetric transfer, while those encircling the EP yield chiral transfer governed by the topological winding number, whose robustness depends on the distance to the EP and the encircling direction. For quantum sensing, trajectory engineering enables tuning of sensitivity amplitude, time window, and parameter selectivity in both eigenvalue-based and eigenstate-based sensors. Notably, eigenstate-based sensing achieves full parameter selectivity that is unattainable with eigenvalue-based methods. Our results establish a quantitative connection between trajectory topology and system dynamics, providing a unified framework for robust state-transfer protocols and high-performance quantum sensors.
one_liner: Engineered parameter loops in a non-Hermitian two-level system control robust state transfer and tune exceptional-point sensing behavior.
why_it_matters: It turns exceptional-point physics from a fragile effect into a design tool for selective, topology-aware sensing and control protocols.
detailed_summary: The study analyzes three time-dependent parameter trajectories in a non-Hermitian two-level system and links loop topology to transfer symmetry, chirality, and robustness. Paths that avoid the exceptional point support robust symmetric transfer, while EP-encircling loops generate chiral transfer set by winding number and sensitive to loop direction and EP distance. For sensing, the trajectory can shape response amplitude, interrogation window, and parameter selectivity. A key result is that eigenstate-based sensing can achieve full parameter selectivity beyond what eigenvalue-based EP sensors allow.
main_result: Trajectory design is shown to determine whether the system realizes robust symmetric transfer, chiral transfer, or highly selective sensing, with eigenstate-based sensing offering full parameter selectivity.
application_angle: This provides a concrete control framework for designing non-Hermitian sensors and state-transfer devices with tunable robustness and task-specific selectivity.

title: Efficient Many-Body Shadow Metrology via Clifford Lensing
abstract: Quantum probes that enable enhanced exploration and characterization of complex systems are central to modern science, spanning applications from biology to astrophysics and chemical design. In large many-body quantum systems, interactions delocalize phase information across many degrees of freedom, dispersing it away from accessible measurements and limiting the scalability of quantum metrology. Here we show that experimentally accessible Clifford operations acting jointly on quantum states and observables can refocus this distributed information. These operations implement what we term {\it Clifford lensing}--transformations that coherently localize phase information onto a reduced set of degrees of freedom, mapping optimal measurements onto observables of reduced Pauli weight. We establish a correspondence between quantum error-correcting codes and interferometric constructions that enforce deterministic phase kickback, and generalize this to circuits that concentrate many-body phase information onto a controllable subset of qubits. We further develop partial shadow tomography protocols for estimating subsystem-supported phases. We experimentally demonstrate these principles in liquid-state nuclear magnetic resonance systems of up to fifteen qubits, achieving optimal sensing with constrained resources. Our results establish a scalable route to coherent control of information flow in interacting quantum systems, enabling many-body quantum sensing and multimode interferometry across complex architectures.
one_liner: Clifford lensing refocuses many-body phase information onto fewer qubits, enabling scalable metrology and partial-shadow readout in interacting systems.
why_it_matters: It offers an experimentally accessible route to extracting optimal sensing information from large entangled systems without full many-body measurements.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce Clifford lensing, where jointly applied Clifford operations on states and observables coherently localize distributed phase information onto a smaller subsystem. They connect these constructions to quantum error-correcting codes and deterministic phase-kickback interferometric schemes, showing how optimal measurements can be mapped to lower-Pauli-weight observables. They also develop partial shadow-tomography methods to estimate subsystem-supported phases efficiently. The ideas are demonstrated experimentally in liquid-state NMR processors with up to fifteen qubits, validating constrained-resource many-body sensing.
main_result: Experiments on up to 15 qubits show that Clifford lensing and partial shadows can recover optimal sensing information from interacting many-body systems using reduced-complexity measurements.
application_angle: This could make quantum-enhanced sensing and multimode interferometry more scalable on near-term hardware by reducing measurement overhead in complex architectures.

title: Towards Schrödinger Cat States in the Second Harmonic Generation
abstract: We investigate the quantum evolution of the pump field in second-harmonic generation under strong pump depletion. Starting from a coherent state, the pump develops a nonclassical phase-space structure resembling a Schrödinger cat state. This behavior originates from phase instability induced by vacuum fluctuations of the harmonic mode. A rigorous quantum analysis has been performed for mean photon numbers up to $\langle \hat n \rangle = 100$ in pump mode. For larger photon numbers, up to $\langle \hat n \rangle = 10^{7}$, the dynamics have been analyzed using a classical trajectory method with sampled initial conditions that reproduces the main features of the quantum evolution. The results indicate that nonlinear frequency conversion can generate macroscopic superposition-like states of the pump field. Although the resulting state is not pure due to correlations with the second-harmonic wave, it remains non-classical with negative zones of Wigner function. These results indicate that strongly nonlinear frequency conversion can provide a scalable route toward macroscopic nonclassical states of light.
one_liner: Strongly depleted second-harmonic generation drives a pump coherent state into cat-like, Wigner-negative nonclassical light.
why_it_matters: It points to a scalable nonlinear-optics route for generating macroscopic nonclassical states without exotic state-preparation hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper studies the quantum evolution of the pump field in second-harmonic generation when pump depletion is strong, showing that an initial coherent state develops a cat-like phase-space structure. The effect is traced to phase instability seeded by vacuum fluctuations in the harmonic mode and verified with rigorous quantum simulations up to mean pump photon number 100. For much larger photon numbers up to 10^7, a sampled classical-trajectory approach reproduces the key quantum signatures. Although pump-harmonic correlations make the state mixed, the pump retains clear nonclassicality through negative regions in the Wigner function.
main_result: The main result is that strong second-harmonic generation can transform a coherent pump into a macroscopic superposition-like, Wigner-negative nonclassical state.
application_angle: This suggests nonlinear frequency converters could become practical sources of large nonclassical optical states for photonic quantum technologies.

title: In-Line Fiber-Integrated Photon-Pair Generation from van der Waals Crystals
abstract: Miniaturized quantum light sources that operate directly in optical fibers are an attractive platform for optical quantum technologies. However, most miniaturized spontaneous parametric down- conversion (SPDC) sources still rely on objective-lens-based free-space pumping and collection, which limits compactness, robustness, and direct compatibility with fiber-based systems. Here we demonstrate a lens-free in-line SPDC photon-pair source by integrating a van der Waals NbOI2 flake directly onto the end facet of an optical fiber. In this configuration, the generated photon- pairs are efficiently collected into optical fibers, eliminating the need for bulk free-space collection optics. Despite the limited numerical aperture of the single-mode fiber, efficient photon-pair collection with high purity, characterized by a coincidence-to-accidental ratio of up to ~4600, is achieved in an ultracompact configuration. These results establish van der Waals ferroelectric materials as a promising platform for fiber-integrated quantum light sources and provide a pathway toward compact, alignment-free quantum photonic devices.
one_liner: A van der Waals NbOI2 flake on a fiber facet enables lens-free, in-line SPDC photon-pair generation with very high CAR.
why_it_matters: It removes bulk alignment optics from pair sources, making quantum-light generation far more compatible with compact fiber networks and deployable systems.
detailed_summary: The authors demonstrate an in-line spontaneous parametric down-conversion source by integrating a ferroelectric van der Waals NbOI2 flake directly onto the end facet of an optical fiber. In this geometry, the generated photon pairs are collected straight into fiber, eliminating objective-lens-based free-space collection. Despite the limited numerical aperture of single-mode fiber, the device reaches coincidence-to-accidental ratios up to about 4600 while remaining ultracompact. The work establishes van der Waals crystals as a viable platform for alignment-free, fiber-native quantum light sources.
main_result: The main result is a lens-free fiber-integrated photon-pair source that efficiently generates and collects high-purity SPDC pairs directly in optical fiber.
application_angle: The architecture is directly relevant to compact quantum communication modules, fieldable photonic nodes, and plug-and-play fiber quantum optics.

title: Controlling entanglement by phase engineering in giant-atom waveguide
abstract: We investigate the entanglement dynamics of two giant atoms coupled to a common waveguide. By introducing additional phase modulation at each coupling point, every photon propagation path is jointly controlled by two distinct coupling phases, enabling precise and flexible manipulation of the entanglement evolution. This phase engineering induces destructive interference among different paths, leading to entanglement dynamics in nested giant atoms that become equivalent to those of small atoms, as well as dynamical equivalence between separated and braided configurations. Furthermore, the proposed scheme significantly enhances the robustness of entanglement against variations in the phase shift, offering a practical route to generate stable entanglement and enabling quantum devices with programmable propagation and controllable memory effects.
one_liner: Phase modulation at giant-atom coupling points enables programmable and more robust entanglement dynamics in waveguide QED.
why_it_matters: It provides a tunable interference-based handle for stabilizing entanglement and controlling memory effects in distributed quantum photonic devices.
detailed_summary: This work analyzes two giant atoms coupled to a common waveguide, with additional phase shifts introduced at each coupling point to control photon propagation paths. Joint control of path phases creates destructive interference that can make nested giant-atom entanglement dynamics emulate those of small atoms and can also map separated and braided configurations onto each other dynamically. The phase-engineering scheme also improves robustness of the entanglement evolution against phase-shift variations. The result is a programmable way to shape retardation and memory effects in waveguide-mediated interactions.
platform_or_system: giant atoms in a common waveguide
main_result: The main result is that coupling-point phase engineering can precisely tune and stabilize two-atom entanglement dynamics through controllable interference in giant-atom waveguides.
application_angle: Such control is useful for photonic quantum networks and on-chip quantum devices that need stable entanglement generation with engineered delay and memory.

title: Refractive multi-conjugate adaptive optics for wide-field atmospheric turbulence correction
abstract: Multi-Conjugate Adaptive Optics (MCAO) is essential for increasing the corrected Field-of-View (FoV) in astronomical imaging and potentially for free-space optical communications, particularly for small-aperture, transportable systems. We demonstrate the viability and performance of a Refractive-MCAO system utilizing a novel multi-actuator Deformable Lens (DL) as the wavefront correction element. Unlike conventional Deformable Mirrors (DMs), the transmissive nature of the DL simplifies the optical train, making it ideal for compact setups. Using a Shack-Hartmann Wavefront Sensor (SH-WFS) in conjunction with two DLs conjugated to different atmospheric layers, we achieved an extension of the isoplanatic patch up to three times the uncorrected atmospheric isoplanatic angle under moderate turbulence D/r0 = 2. We tested the MCAO system in a setup that emulates a free space optical communication for compact transportable system. In a double-channel, single-mode fiber coupling experiment we demonstrated the efficiency of this method.
one_liner: A refractive multi-conjugate adaptive-optics system with deformable lenses widens turbulence correction and boosts dual-channel fiber coupling.
why_it_matters: Compact transmissive MCAO could make wide-field correction practical for portable free-space optical and quantum communication terminals.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates a refractive multi-conjugate adaptive-optics architecture that replaces deformable mirrors with transmissive multi-actuator deformable lenses. Using a Shack-Hartmann wavefront sensor and two deformable lenses conjugated to different turbulence layers, the setup extends the corrected field of view to roughly three times the uncorrected isoplanatic angle for moderate turbulence with D/r0 = 2. The authors test the system in a free-space optical communication emulator and show effective coupling into two single-mode-fiber channels. The transmissive design simplifies the optical train and is suited to compact implementations.
main_result: The main result is a compact refractive MCAO system that significantly enlarges the corrected turbulence field and improves multi-channel single-mode fiber coupling.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to portable free-space quantum links and optical terminals that need better turbulence tolerance without bulky mirror-based AO hardware.

title: A material-agnostic platform to probe spin-phonon interactions using high-overtone bulk acoustic wave resonators
abstract: Spin-phonon interactions have a dual role in emerging spin-based quantum technologies. While they can be a limitation to device performance through decoherence, they also serve as a critical resource for coherent spin control, detection, and the realization of spin-based quantum networks. However, their direct characterization remains a challenge and is usually material-dependent. Here, we introduce a technique to probe spin-phonon coupling at millikelvin temperatures and gigahertz frequencies, using high-overtone bulk acoustic wave resonators (HBARs) integrated with arbitrary crystals via visco-elastic transfer of thin-film lithium niobate transducers. By tuning the Larmor frequency of dilute spin ensembles into resonance with HBAR modes, we extract the anisotropy and strength of spin-phonon interactions from acoustic dispersion and dissipation measurements. We demonstrate this approach in calcium tungstate (CaWO4) and yttrium orthosilicate (Y2SiO5), achieving cooperativities up to 0.5 for erbium dopant ensembles. Our method enables the study of spin-phonon interactions in complex crystalline materials, with minimal fabrication constraints. These results will facilitate the design of hybrid quantum systems and the quest for ion-matrix combination with enhanced spin-phonon coupling.
one_liner: HBARs with transferred thin-film LiNbO3 transducers provide a material-agnostic millikelvin probe of spin-phonon coupling in crystals.
why_it_matters: It offers a practical characterization tool for selecting and engineering hybrid spin-acoustic materials for quantum memories, transducers, and networks.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a platform based on high-overtone bulk acoustic wave resonators integrated onto arbitrary host crystals via visco-elastic transfer of thin-film lithium niobate transducers. By tuning dilute spin ensembles into resonance with GHz acoustic modes at millikelvin temperatures, they extract spin-phonon interaction strength and anisotropy from acoustic dispersion and loss. The method is demonstrated in CaWO4 and Y2SiO5, with erbium dopant ensembles reaching cooperativities up to 0.5. Because the approach is largely independent of host-material fabrication constraints, it is suited to surveying and optimizing candidate hybrid quantum materials.
main_result: The main result is a broadly compatible cryogenic acoustic-resonator technique that directly measures spin-phonon coupling in different crystals and reaches cooperativity up to 0.5.
application_angle: The platform can accelerate development of spin-based quantum memories, microwave-optical interfaces, and network nodes by identifying favorable ion-host combinations.

title: Correlated Atom Loss as a Resource for Quantum Error Correction
abstract: Atom loss is a dominant error source in neutral-atom quantum processors, yet its correlated structure remains largely unexploited by existing quantum error correction decoders. We analyze the performance of the surface code equipped with teleportation-based loss-detection units for neutral-atom quantum processors subject to circuit-level, partially correlated atom loss and depolarizing noise. We introduce and implement a decoding strategy that exploits loss correlations, effectively converting the \textit{delayed} erasure channels stemming from atom loss to erasure channels. The decoder constructs a loss graph and dynamically updates loss probabilities, a procedure that is highly parallelizable and compatible with real-time operation. Compared to a decoder that assumes independent loss events, our approach achieves up to an order-of-magnitude reduction in logical error probability and increases the loss threshold from $3.2\%$ to $4\%$. Our approach extends to experimentally relevant regimes with partially correlated loss, demonstrating robust gains beyond the idealized fully correlated setting.
one_liner: A decoder that exploits correlated neutral-atom loss converts delayed erasures into usable erasure information for surface-code QEC.
why_it_matters: Using realistic loss correlations materially improves logical performance and thresholds in neutral-atom processors, where atom loss is a dominant hardware error.
detailed_summary: This work studies surface-code error correction for neutral-atom processors with circuit-level depolarizing noise and partially correlated atom loss, using teleportation-based loss-detection units. The proposed decoder builds a loss graph and dynamically updates loss probabilities so that delayed erasures caused by atom loss can be treated more like standard erasures during decoding. Relative to a decoder assuming independent loss, it reduces logical error rates by up to an order of magnitude and raises the loss threshold from 3.2% to 4%. The method remains effective beyond the ideal fully correlated regime and is designed to be highly parallelizable for real-time use.
main_result: The main result is a correlation-aware loss decoder that substantially lowers logical error probability and improves the tolerable atom-loss threshold in neutral-atom QEC.
application_angle: This directly supports scalable fault-tolerant neutral-atom hardware by turning a major native error source into more exploitable decoder information.

title: Time-frequency Talbot effect as Clifford operations on entangled time-frequency GKP states
abstract: The Talbot effect -- a near-field diffraction phenomenon in which a periodic wavefront self-images at regular distances -- can be transposed to the time--frequency domain via the space--time duality between diffraction and dispersive broadening. We exploit this analogy to define the time--frequency (TF) Talbot effect and show that it implements different Clifford operations on TF Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (TF-GKP) qubits (Phys. Rev. 102, 012607), a class of qubit states encoded in the discretised frequency and time-of-arrival degrees of freedom of entangled photon pairs, whose logical basis corresponds to even and odd components of an entangled frequency combs. These states are intrinsically robust against small frequency and temporal displacements, which can be further corrected by linear or nonlinear quantum error-correction schemes. We analyse the role of the comb envelope and peak width relative to the free spectral range, and show that a compromise must be made between the gate fidelity of the Clifford gates induced by TF-Talbot operation and the error-correction capacity of the code. We then demonstrate that the signature of the TF-Talbot effect is directly accessible via the generalised Hong-Ou-Mandel interferometer: all six logical GKP states can be unambiguously distinguished by introducing a frequency shift of half the comb periodicity in one interferometer arm. We conclude with a feasibility analysis based on current experimental technology, identifying the comb finesse as the key figure of merit for both gate performance and correctability. This conclusion extends naturally to quadrature GKP states, where a shear in quadrature phase space is precisely a Talbot effect.
one_liner: Maps the time-frequency Talbot effect onto Clifford gates for entangled time-frequency GKP photonic qubits.
why_it_matters: It links dispersive photonic propagation to encoded logical operations and readout for a hardware-relevant bosonic error-correcting qubit platform.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates a time-frequency Talbot effect for entangled photon-pair frequency combs and shows that the induced dispersive evolution implements Clifford operations on time-frequency GKP qubits. It quantifies how comb envelope, peak width, and free spectral range set a tradeoff between logical gate fidelity and displacement-error correctability. The authors also show that a generalized Hong-Ou-Mandel interferometer with a half-period frequency shift can unambiguously distinguish all six logical GKP states. A feasibility analysis identifies comb finesse as the central experimental figure of merit.
main_result: Dispersive time-frequency Talbot evolution is shown to realize Clifford gates and enable interferometric discrimination of all six logical TF-GKP states.
application_angle: This gives a concrete route to photonic logical gates and readout in frequency-bin encoded bosonic qubits using existing dispersive and interferometric components.

title: Colloidal Nanocrystals Regrowth-Assisted Synthesis of Perovskite Microwire Lasers for Integrated Optoelectronics
abstract: Colloidal perovskite nanocrystals (NCs) are a well-proven platform for growing anisotropic structures. Nanowires (NWs) exhibiting a quantum confinement phenomenon and microwires (MWs), which enable lasing, are of particular interest for optoelectronic devices. Synthesis of the latter is challenging. Herein, we report a straightforward access to high-quality CsPbBr3 MW lasers. We utilize a diphenyl ether (DPE) solvent for the hot-injection synthesis. DPE coordinates strongly to Pb2+ and allows to reduce an excess of oleic acid/oleylamine ligand pair well established for PbBr2 dissolution and inhibition of as-formed NCs regrowth. Therefore, a rapid injection of Cs-oleate into the PbBr2-containing solution yields lead-depleted Cs4PbBr6 NCs which slowly release perovskite precursors and produce CsPbBr3 counterparts. The latter transform into NWs through an oriented-attachment mechanism, which in turn evolve into laser MWs. To demonstrate spectrally tunable lasing in MWs we employ YCl3 for ion exchange in perovskite lattice. Resultant CsPb(Cl,Br)3 MWs show high-Q coherent emission in the 485-540 nm range. To highlight the potential of synthesized MWs for integrated optoelectronics, we assemble a device comprising a CsPb(Cl,Br)3 MW laser coupled to MoO3 lossless nanowaveguide, which delivers coherent light to a CsPbBr3 MW photodetector. The device exhibits a nonlinear optoelectronic response applicable for on-chip neuromorphic computing.
one_liner: Regrowth-assisted synthesis yields tunable perovskite microwire lasers integrated with a waveguide and photodetector on chip.
why_it_matters: It advances scalable on-chip coherent light sources and nonlinear optoelectronic elements for integrated photonics and neuromorphic hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors develop a hot-injection route in diphenyl ether that suppresses excessive ligand stabilization, enabling Cs4PbBr6 nanocrystal intermediates to regrow into CsPbBr3 nanowires and then lasing microwires. By YCl3-mediated ion exchange they tune CsPb(Cl,Br)3 microwire laser emission across 485-540 nm with high-Q coherent output. They further assemble an integrated device where a perovskite microwire laser couples through a MoO3 low-loss nanowaveguide to a CsPbBr3 microwire photodetector. The resulting nonlinear optoelectronic transfer response is positioned for neuromorphic on-chip functionality.
main_result: A simple regrowth-assisted synthesis produces high-quality, spectrally tunable perovskite microwire lasers and an integrated laser-waveguide-detector circuit.
application_angle: The work supports compact integrated optoelectronics by combining coherent sources, routing, and nonlinear detection in a single perovskite-compatible platform.

title: Robust Parametric Quantum Gate Against Stochastic Time-Varying Noise
abstract: The performance of quantum processors in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era is severely constrained by environmental noise and other uncertainties. While the recently proposed quantum control robustness landscape (QCRL) offers a powerful framework for generating robust control pulses for parametric gate families, its application has been practically restricted to quasi-static noise. To address the spectrally complex, time-varying noise prevalent in reality, we propose filter function-enhanced QCRL (FF-QCRL), which integrates filter function formalism into the QCRL framework. The resulting FF-QCRL algorithm minimizes a generalized robustness metric that faithfully encodes the impact of stochastic processes, enabling robust pulse-family generation for parametric gates under realistic time-varying noise. Numerical validation in a representative single-qubit setting confirms the effectiveness of the proposed method.
one_liner: Introduces FF-QCRL, a control-design method that hardens parametric quantum gates against stochastic time-varying noise.
why_it_matters: Robust pulse families under realistic noise spectra are essential for pushing gate fidelity in practical NISQ hardware.
detailed_summary: This work extends the quantum control robustness landscape framework beyond quasi-static noise by incorporating filter-function formalism into the optimization objective. The resulting FF-QCRL algorithm minimizes a generalized robustness metric that explicitly captures stochastic, spectrally structured noise processes. Numerical tests on a representative single-qubit gate family show that the optimized pulses retain robustness under time-varying noise where standard QCRL assumptions break down. The method is framed as a practical route to generating robust parametric gate families rather than isolated pulses.
platform_or_system: Single-qubit parametric control under stochastic noise
main_result: FF-QCRL generates parametric gate pulses with improved robustness to realistic time-varying stochastic noise compared with quasi-static-noise-focused approaches.
