one_liner: Tensor-network solvers compute exciton and trion spectra in CdSe nanoplatelets across the intermediate confinement regime.
why_it_matters: It provides a practical route to model optical states in nanostructures that are too confined for Wannier methods and too correlated for simple factorization.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a real-space tensor-network approach for electron-hole complexes in semiconductor nanoplatelets where neither weak- nor strong-confinement approximations are adequate. Using CdSe nanoplatelets as the test case, it computes exciton and trion ground states together with several excited states, including energies and oscillator strengths over varying platelet sizes. The method addresses the unfactorized high-dimensional Schrödinger problem more efficiently than brute-force approaches. It also outlines general tensor-network strategies for intermediate-confinement systems relevant to low-dimensional photonic materials.
main_result: The authors demonstrate accurate calculation of bound excitonic and trionic states, including excited-state spectra and oscillator strengths, in intermediate-confinement nanoplatelets.
application_angle: Better predictive modeling of optical transitions can guide design of colloidal nanostructures for emitters, absorbers, and other quantum-photonic devices.

title: Maximizing Qubit Throughput under Buffer Decoherence and Variability in Generation
abstract: Quantum communication networks require transmission of high-fidelity, uncoded qubits for applications such as entanglement distribution and quantum key distribution. However, current implementations are constrained by limited buffer capacity and qubit decoherence, which degrades qubit quality while waiting in the buffer. A key challenge arises from the stochastic nature of qubit generation, there exists a random delay (D) between the initiation of a generation request and the availability of the qubit. This induces a fundamental trade off early initiation increases buffer waiting time and hence decoherence, whereas delayed initiation leads to server idling and reduced throughput. We model this system as an admission control problem in a finite buffer queue, where the reward associated with each job is a decreasing function of its sojourn time. We derive analytical conditions under which a simple "no lag" policy where a new qubit is generated immediately upon the availability of buffer space is optimal. To address scenarios with unknown system parameters, we further develop a Bayesian learning framework that adaptively optimizes the admission policy. In addition to quantum communication systems, the proposed model is applicable to delay sensitive IoT sensing and service systems.
one_liner: This work derives when immediate qubit generation is throughput-optimal despite decohering buffers and stochastic generation delays.
why_it_matters: It turns a hardware bottleneck in quantum links into an analyzable control problem and adds adaptive learning when system parameters are unknown.
detailed_summary: The authors model qubit transmission as a finite-buffer admission-control problem where each qubit's reward decreases with sojourn time because stored states decohere. They analyze the tradeoff between generating too early, which increases waiting-induced decoherence, and generating too late, which leaves servers idle and reduces throughput. Analytical conditions are derived under which a simple no-lag policy is optimal, meaning generation should start immediately when a buffer slot opens. For uncertain environments, a Bayesian learning scheme adaptively updates the policy from observed system behavior.
main_result: The paper characterizes when immediate generation maximizes qubit throughput and supplies a Bayesian controller for cases with unknown delay and decoherence parameters.
application_angle: These results can improve scheduling in practical quantum repeaters, QKD links, and other systems where memory lifetimes and source variability limit usable rate.

title: Lattice and PT symmetries in tensor-network renormalization group: a case study of a hard-square lattice gas model
abstract: The tensor-network renormalization group (TNRG) is an accurate numerical real-space renormalization group method for studying phase transitions in both quantum and classical systems. Continuous phase transitions, as an important class of phase transitions, are usually accompanied by spontaneous breaking of various symmetries. However, the understanding of symmetries in the TNRG is well-established mainly for global on-site symmetries like U(1) and SU(2). In this paper, we demonstrate how to incorporate lattice symmetries (including reflection and rotation) and the PT symmetry in the TNRG in two dimensions (2D) through a case study of the hard-square lattice gas with nearest-neighbor exclusion. This model is chosen because it is well-understood and has two continuous phase transitions whose spontaneously-broken symmetries are lattice and PT symmetries. Specifically, we write down proper definitions of these symmetries in a coarse-grained tensor network and propose a TNRG scheme that incorporates these symmetries. We demonstrate the validity of the proposed method by estimating the critical parameters and the scaling dimensions of the two phase transitions of the model. The technical development in this paper has made the 2D TNRG a more well-rounded numerical method.
one_liner: A symmetry-aware tensor-network renormalization scheme incorporates lattice and PT symmetries in 2D coarse graining.
why_it_matters: It strengthens numerical treatment of symmetry-breaking transitions, which is useful for extracting precise critical behavior in quantum and classical sensing-related materials models.
detailed_summary: The paper extends 2D tensor-network renormalization by explicitly embedding reflection, rotation, and PT symmetries into the coarse-grained tensor description. Using the hard-square lattice gas as a benchmark, it treats two continuous phase transitions associated with spontaneous breaking of lattice and PT symmetries. The authors define how these symmetries should act after coarse graining and construct a TNRG workflow that preserves them throughout renormalization. The method reproduces critical parameters and scaling dimensions, validating the symmetry-aware framework.
main_result: The proposed symmetry-preserving TNRG accurately captures two continuous phase transitions and their critical data in a model with lattice and PT symmetry breaking.
application_angle: Improved extraction of critical properties benefits modeling of correlated lattice systems and engineered materials where phase sensitivity can underpin sensing functionality.

title: Send the Key in Cleartext: Halving Key Consumption while Preserving Unconditional Security in QKD Authentication
abstract: Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) protocols require Information-Theoretically Secure (ITS) authentication of the classical channel to preserve the unconditional security of the distilled key. Standard ITS schemes are based on one-time keys: once a key is used to authenticate a message, it must be discarded. Since QKD requires mutual authentication, two independent one-time keys are typically consumed per round, imposing a non-trivial overhead on the net secure key rate. In this work, we present the authentication-with-response scheme, a novel ITS authentication scheme based on $\varepsilon$-Almost Strongly Universal$_2$ ($\varepsilon$-ASU$_2$) functions, whose IT security can be established in the Universal Composability (UC) framework. The scheme achieves mutual authentication consuming a single one-time key per QKD round, halving key consumption compared to the state-of-the-art.
one_liner: A UC-secure QKD authentication protocol achieves mutual authentication with only one one-time key per round.
why_it_matters: It cuts authentication key consumption in half without sacrificing unconditional security, directly improving net key yield in deployed QKD systems.
detailed_summary: The work proposes an authentication-with-response construction based on epsilon-ASU2 hash families for the classical channel in QKD. Unlike standard information-theoretically secure mutual authentication, which consumes two independent one-time keys per round, the new scheme uses a single one-time key while retaining security in the Universal Composability framework. The protocol therefore reduces classical authentication overhead without weakening the unconditional security guarantees required for QKD. Its contribution is architectural rather than physical, but it directly affects achievable secure throughput.
main_result: The scheme provides UC-secure mutual authentication for QKD while halving one-time key consumption relative to standard ITS approaches.
application_angle: Reducing authentication overhead raises effective secret-key efficiency and is immediately relevant to practical QKD deployments with tight key budgets.

title: Stochastic Multipath Routing for High-Throughput Entanglement Distribution in Quantum Repeater Networks
abstract: Quantum repeater networks distribute entanglement over lossy links while many users share a limited pool of entangled pairs. Most existing routing schemes either always use a single best path or rely on global optimizations that are hard to run in real time. Here we propose and analyze a simple alternative: a stochastic multipath rule in which each entanglement request is sent at random along one of several edge-disjoint repeater paths, with a single parameter that controls the bias between shorter and longer routes. Using a distance-dependent lossy network model with finite per-link capacities and probabilistic entanglement swapping, we develop an analytic description of the resulting end-to-end entanglement rate as a function of this bias and validate it with large-scale numerical simulations. We find that an intermediate bias consistently outperforms both deterministic extremes across distances, traffic patterns, attenuation, swapping noise, and congestion, bringing the rate close to simple capacity upper bounds and making link usage more even across networks. These results identify stochastic multipath routing as a lightweight classical control strategy for boosting performance and scalability in near-term quantum repeater networks.
one_liner: A stochastic edge-disjoint multipath rule boosts repeater-network entanglement rates without requiring heavy global optimization.
why_it_matters: It offers a lightweight real-time control strategy that improves throughput, fairness, and scalability in practical quantum repeater networks.
detailed_summary: The paper studies entanglement distribution in lossy repeater networks with finite link capacities and probabilistic swapping under shared multi-user traffic. It introduces a single-parameter stochastic routing rule that randomly selects among edge-disjoint paths with tunable bias toward shorter routes. An analytic model for end-to-end entanglement rate is derived and validated with large-scale simulations across distance, attenuation, congestion, and noise settings. Intermediate bias values consistently outperform deterministic shortest-path and overly diffuse routing while also evening out link utilization.
main_result: A tunable stochastic multipath routing policy achieves near-capacity entanglement rates and more balanced link usage across a wide range of network conditions.
application_angle: The approach is implementable as near-term classical control software for shared quantum networks, improving throughput without expensive global optimization.

title: Robust continuous-variable multipartite entanglement in circular arrays of nonlinear waveguides
abstract: Encoding continuous-variable quantum information in the optical domain has recently enabled the generation of large entangled states, yet robust implementation remains a challenge. Here, we present a straightforward protocol for generating multipartite entanglement based on spontaneous parametric down-conversion in a circular array of quadratic nonlinear waveguides. We provide a rigorous theoretical framework, including comprehensive derivations of the propagation equations and the identification of regimes where analytical solutions are possible. Crucially, our approach identifies the pump and detection configurations required to sustain and measure multipartite full inseparability across arbitrary propagation distances and for any number of waveguides $N=4 n$. This regime, elusive to standard numerical methods, represents a key requirement for scalable quantum protocols. Our scheme is inherently robust as it relies on phase-matched propagation eigenmodes, making it resilient against variations in sample length, coupling, and nonlinearity.
one_liner: Circular quadratic waveguide arrays can generate robust continuous-variable multipartite entanglement over arbitrary propagation distances.
why_it_matters: It identifies a scalable and fabrication-tolerant route to optical CV entanglement generation in integrated nonlinear photonic hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze spontaneous parametric down-conversion in circular arrays of chi(2) waveguides and derive the propagation equations governing multipartite state generation. They identify pump and detection configurations that preserve full inseparability for any propagation distance when the number of guides satisfies N=4n. The robust regime is tied to phase-matched propagation eigenmodes, enabling analytical treatment where standard numerics struggle. The scheme is predicted to tolerate variations in sample length, coupling strength, and nonlinearity.
main_result: The work establishes conditions under which circular nonlinear waveguide arrays sustain and reveal multipartite continuous-variable entanglement robustly for arbitrary propagation lengths.
application_angle: This supports scalable integrated sources for CV quantum networking, metrology, and photonic information processing with relaxed device-tolerance requirements.

title: Structured-Light Magnetometry in a Coherently Controlled Atomic Medium
abstract: A structured-light-based approach for detecting magneto-optical rotation is presented, in which polarization rotation is mapped onto a directly observable spatial degree of freedom. A radially polarized Laguerre-Gaussian beam interacts with cold $^{87}\mathrm{Rb}$ atoms in the presence of a longitudinal magnetic field, where magnetically induced circular birefringence introduces a relative phase shift between the $σ_+$ and $σ_-$ components of the field, manifesting as a rotation of the interference pattern. The MOR angle is extracted directly from the angular displacement of the petal-shaped intensity distribution, eliminating the need for polarizers or Stokes-parameter analysis. This method converts conventional polarization-based magnetometry into a topology-based spatial readout, enabling spatially resolved magnetic-field sensing with potential applications in optical magnetometry and quantum sensing.
one_liner: Structured light turns magneto-optical rotation into a directly imaged spatial rotation for atomic magnetometry.
why_it_matters: It replaces polarization-analysis hardware with an image-based readout that could simplify and spatially enrich quantum magnetometers.
detailed_summary: A radially polarized Laguerre-Gaussian beam probes cold 87Rb atoms under a longitudinal magnetic field, where circular birefringence creates a relative phase shift between sigma-plus and sigma-minus components. That phase shift rotates the beam's petal-like interference pattern, allowing the magneto-optical rotation angle to be read out from image-plane angular displacement. The method removes the need for polarizers or Stokes-parameter reconstruction by mapping polarization evolution onto beam topology. The result is a spatially resolved sensing modality in a coherently controlled atomic medium.
main_result: The paper demonstrates magneto-optical rotation readout via direct imaging of structured-light pattern rotation in a rubidium medium.
application_angle: Image-based spatial readout can simplify optical magnetometers and enable field mapping for quantum sensing and atomic instrumentation.

title: Non-linear Sigma Model for the Surface Code with Coherent Errors
abstract: The surface code is a promising platform for a quantum memory, but its threshold under coherent errors remains incompletely understood. We study maximum-likelihood decoding of the square-lattice surface code in the presence of single-qubit unitary rotations that create electric anyon excitations. We microscopically derive a non-linear sigma model with target space $\mathrm{SO}(2n)/\mathrm{U}(n)$ as the effective long-distance theory of this decoding problem, with distinct replica limits: $n\to1$ for optimal decoding, which assumes knowledge of the coherent rotation angle, and $n\to0$ for suboptimal decoding with imperfect angle information. This exposes a sharp distinction between the two decoders. The suboptimal decoder supports a ``thermal-metal'' phase, a non-decodable regime that is qualitatively distinct from the conventional non-decodable phase of the surface code under incoherent Pauli errors. By contrast, the metal phase cannot arise in optimal decoding, since the metallic fixed-point becomes unstable in the $n\to 1$ replica limit. We argue that optimal decoding may be possible up to the maximally-coherent rotation angle. Within the sigma model description, we show that the decoding fidelity is related to twist defects of the order-parameter field, yielding quantitative predictions for its system-size dependence near the metallic fixed point for both decoders. We examine our analytic predictions for the decoding fidelity as well as other physical observables with extensive numerical simulations. We discuss how the symmetries and the target space for the sigma model rely on the lattice of the surface code, and how a stable thermal metal phase can arise in optimal decoding when the syndromes reside on a non-bipartite lattice.
one_liner: A non-linear sigma-model description reveals decoding phase structure of the surface code under coherent unitary errors.
why_it_matters: It clarifies when coherent noise creates qualitatively new non-decodable behavior, informing decoder design for fault-tolerant hardware and networks.
detailed_summary: The work derives a long-distance non-linear sigma model with target space SO(2n)/U(n) for the square-lattice surface code subject to single-qubit coherent rotations that create electric anyons. Distinct replica limits separate optimal decoding with known rotation angle from suboptimal decoding with imperfect angle knowledge. In the suboptimal case, the theory predicts a thermal-metal phase that is qualitatively different from the usual non-decodable regime for incoherent Pauli noise, while this metallic fixed point is unstable for optimal decoding. Numerical simulations support the analytic scaling predictions for decoding fidelity and related observables near the metallic point.
main_result: The authors show that coherent-error decoding admits a thermal-metal phase for suboptimal decoders, whereas optimal decoding can avoid that phase up to maximal coherence.
application_angle: These results guide decoder construction and calibration requirements for realistic fault-tolerant quantum processors and encoded quantum communication nodes.

title: Scalable Qauntum Interference from Indistinguishable Quantum Dots
abstract: Quantum interference of indistinguishable photons is the foundation of photonic quantum technologies, yet scaling from a few to many identical quantum light sources remains a major challenge. In solid-state platforms, spatial and spectral inhomogeneity and resource-intensive architectures impede scaling. As a result, interference between remote, independent quantum emitters has been thus far limited to pairs. Here we introduce a wavefront-shaping approach that enables scalable interference from multiple indistinguishable quantum dots on the same chip. Using programmable spatial light modulators, we independently excite, collect, and route emission from spatially distinct, yet spectrally degenerate dots. Scaling from two to five indistinguishable emitters, we verify interference through cooperative-emission phenomena and Hong-Ou-Mandel two-photon interference, thereby establishing a route towards large-scale, programmable quantum photonic architectures.
one_liner: Wavefront shaping enables on-chip quantum interference from up to five indistinguishable quantum dots instead of only pairs.
why_it_matters: It directly addresses the scaling bottleneck for solid-state multi-emitter photonic quantum hardware using programmable optical control.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a programmable wavefront-shaping architecture that independently excites, collects, and routes emission from multiple spatially distinct but spectrally degenerate quantum dots on the same chip. Spatial light modulators are used to control remote emitters without requiring bespoke static routing for each source. The authors scale interference experiments from two to five emitters and verify indistinguishability via cooperative-emission signatures and Hong-Ou-Mandel interference. This demonstrates a practical route to larger arrays of mutually interfering solid-state single-photon sources.
main_result: The study demonstrates scalable interference among as many as five indistinguishable quantum-dot emitters using programmable wavefront shaping.
application_angle: Programmable multi-emitter control is a key step toward larger photonic quantum processors, simulators, and networked single-photon source arrays.

title: Electrostatic Photoluminescence Tuning in All-Solid-State Perovskite Transistors
abstract: We demonstrate an all solid state semiconductor device, based on epitaxial single crystalline metal halide perovskites, enabling reversible control of a perovskite photoluminescence with a gate voltage. Fundamentally distinct from electroluminescent diodes, such a photoluminescence field effect transistor uses the gate electric field to electrostatically modulate the interfacial density of mobile charges, thereby affecting the radiative and nonradiative recombination channels of photocarriers. Varying the gate voltage in such transistors efficiently changes the rate of nonradiative interfacial recombination and modulates the photoluminescence intensity by 65 to 98 percent (depending on temperature). At favorable gating, nearly complete elimination of non-radiative losses can be achieved. This functionality, coupled with the strong visible-range absorption and emission, possible due to the high absorption coefficient, as well as controllable thickness and macroscopically homogeneous morphology of epitaxial perovskite films, leads to high external photoluminescence quantum efficiencies realized in large-area, thin-film devices. Such high-efficiency, scalable, electrostatically tunable optoelectronic switches broaden the potential applications of metal-halide perovskites in photonics and optoelectronics.
one_liner: Gate-controlled perovskite transistors reversibly tune photoluminescence by suppressing interfacial nonradiative recombination.
why_it_matters: It provides a scalable solid-state path to efficient, voltage-tunable optical switching in thin-film perovskite photonic devices.
detailed_summary: The authors realize all-solid-state transistors based on epitaxial single-crystal metal-halide perovskites in which gate voltage modulates photoluminescence rather than driving electroluminescence. Electrostatic gating changes the interfacial mobile-charge density and thereby alters radiative versus nonradiative recombination pathways for photocarriers. Depending on temperature, the photoluminescence intensity is modulated by 65% to 98%, and favorable bias conditions can nearly eliminate nonradiative losses. The platform combines this tunability with large-area thin-film geometry and high external photoluminescence quantum efficiency.
main_result: The device achieves reversible gate-voltage control of perovskite photoluminescence with up to near-complete suppression of nonradiative interfacial losses.
application_angle: Such electrostatically tunable, efficient thin-film emitters could serve as practical optical switches and adaptive optoelectronic components in integrated photonics.

title: A Dipolar Chiral Spin Liquid on the Breathed Kagome Lattice
abstract: Continuous control over lattice geometry, when combined with long-range interactions, offers a powerful yet underexplored tool to generate highly frustrated quantum spin systems. By considering long-range dipolar antiferromagnetic interactions on a breathed Kagome lattice, we demonstrate how these tools can be leveraged to stabilize a chiral spin liquid. We support this prediction with large-scale density-matrix renormalization group calculations and explore the surrounding phase diagram, identifying a route to adiabatic preparation via a locally varying magnetic field. At the same time, we identify the relevant low-energy degrees of freedom in each unit cell, providing a complementary language to study the chiral spin liquid. Finally, we carefully analyze its stability and signatures in finite-sized clusters, proposing direct, experimentally viable measurements of the chiral edge mode in both Rydberg atom and ultracold polar molecule arrays.
one_liner: Long-range dipolar interactions on a breathed Kagome lattice are shown to stabilize a chiral spin liquid with an experimentally accessible preparation route.
why_it_matters: It connects frustrated topological magnetism to realistic Rydberg and polar-molecule hardware, with concrete signatures for preparing and detecting a chiral spin liquid.
detailed_summary: The work studies antiferromagnetic long-range dipolar spins on a breathed Kagome lattice and uses large-scale density-matrix renormalization group calculations to map the phase diagram. It identifies a stable chiral spin liquid regime and develops a low-energy unit-cell description that clarifies the effective degrees of freedom. The authors also propose adiabatic preparation using a locally varying magnetic field and analyze finite-size observables relevant to experiments. Direct measurements of chiral edge modes are outlined for both Rydberg atom arrays and ultracold polar molecule platforms.
platform_or_system: Rydberg atom and polar molecule Kagome arrays
main_result: A dipolar breathed-Kagome model is shown to host a chiral spin liquid, together with a practical adiabatic preparation protocol and measurable edge-mode signatures.
application_angle: The results provide a concrete route for quantum simulators to engineer and probe topological spin liquids in programmable dipolar hardware.

title: Ultrabroadband Passive Laser Noise Suppression to Quantum Noise Limit through on-chip Second Harmonic Generation
abstract: Laser intensity noise limits performance in quantum sensing, metrology, and computing. Existing stabilization methods face a trade-off between bandwidth and complexity: electronic feedback loops are speed-limited, while optical resonators are constrained by narrow linewidths and locking requirements. Here, we demonstrate an all-optical "noise eater" that passively suppresses intensity fluctuations from DC to >10 gigahertz. By leveraging high-efficiency second-harmonic generation in nanophotonic lithium niobate waveguides, we operate at a pump-depletion stationary point where input fluctuations are decoupled from the output to first order. This passive and nonresonant nanophotonic device suppresses relative intensity noise by 25 to 60 dB over the full measurement bandwidth and stabilizes a noisy fiber amplifier output to the shot-noise limit. Our results establish a scalable, wide-bandwidth paradigm for laser stabilization essential for high-throughput quantum technologies and deployable photonic sensing systems.
one_liner: An on-chip lithium-niobate SHG device passively suppresses laser intensity noise from DC to beyond 10 GHz, reaching the shot-noise limit.
why_it_matters: Wideband, feedback-free laser stabilization directly benefits deployable quantum sensors, photonic processors, and metrology systems that are limited by technical laser noise.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates an all-optical passive noise eater based on high-efficiency second-harmonic generation in nanophotonic lithium niobate waveguides. Operating at a pump-depletion stationary point, the device decouples input intensity fluctuations from the transmitted output to first order without resonant locking or electronic feedback. Measured relative intensity noise suppression spans DC to greater than 10 GHz, with 25-60 dB reduction across the band. The approach stabilizes a noisy fiber-amplifier output down to the quantum shot-noise limit.
main_result: The device achieves passive ultrabroadband laser intensity-noise suppression over DC to >10 GHz and brings a noisy source to the shot-noise limit.
application_angle: This enables compact, scalable laser-noise conditioning for high-throughput quantum sensing, metrology, and integrated photonic quantum systems.

title: Theory of (Co)homological Invariants on Quantum LDPC Codes
abstract: With recent breakthroughs in the construction of good qLDPC codes and nearly good qLTCs, the study of (co)homological invariants of quantum code complexes, which fundamentally underlie their logical operations, has become evidently important. In this work, we establish a systematic framework for mathematically analyzing these invariants across a broad spectrum of constructions, from HGP codes to sheaf codes, by synthesizing advanced math tools. We generalize the notion of canonical logical representatives from HGP codes to the sheaf code setting, resolving a long-standing challenge in explicitly characterizing sheaf codewords. Building on this foundation, we present the first comprehensive computation of cup products within the intricate framework of sheaf codes. Given Artin's primitive root conjecture which holds under the generalized Riemann hypothesis, we prove that $\tildeΘ(N)$ independent cup products can be supported on almost good qLDPC codes and qLTCs of length N, opening the possibility of achieving linearly many parallel, nontrivial, constant-depth multi-controlled-Z gates. Moreover, by interpreting sheaf codes as covering spaces of HGP codes via graph lifts, we propose a scheme that inductively generates families of both HGP and sheaf codes in an interlaced fashion from a constant-size HGP code. Notably, the induction preserves all (co)homological invariants of the initial code. This provides a general framework for lifting invariants or logical gates from small codes to infinite code families, and enables efficient verification of such features by checking on small instances. Our theory provides a substantive methodology for studying invariants in HGP codes and extends it to sheaf codes. In doing so, we reveal deep and unexpected connections between qLDPC codes and math, thereby laying the groundwork for future advances in quantum coding, fault tolerance, and physics.
one_liner: A general framework computes cohomological invariants and cup products for qLDPC and sheaf codes, enabling scalable logical-gate analysis.
why_it_matters: Understanding invariant structure in qLDPC families is key to fault-tolerant hardware architectures that need explicit, parallelizable logical operations.
detailed_summary: This theory paper builds a unified mathematical framework for analyzing homological and cohomological invariants across hypergraph-product and sheaf quantum LDPC codes. It generalizes canonical logical representatives to sheaf codes and gives the first comprehensive computation of cup products in that setting. Under Artin's primitive root conjecture with GRH, it shows that almost-good qLDPC codes of length N can support about Theta-tilde(N) independent cup products, suggesting many parallel constant-depth multi-controlled-Z operations. It also introduces an interlaced graph-lift construction that preserves invariants while generating infinite code families from a constant-size seed code.
main_result: The authors provide the first broad framework for explicit invariant and cup-product analysis in sheaf and related qLDPC codes, with implications for parallel logical gate implementation.
application_angle: The methods could reduce the design and verification burden for scalable fault-tolerant quantum processors based on low-overhead qLDPC codes.

title: Self-Organized Optical Pathways in Optofluidic Photonic Crystals
abstract: This paper reports FDTD simulations of optofluidic reconfiguration in two-dimensional silicon photonic crystal waveguides, treating structural plasticity (the creation and destruction of optical pathways) via selective fluid infiltration. Using MPB eigenmode analysis, we decouple bandgap narrowing from defect-mode weakening, showing that defect weakening dominates (2.4 times faster transmission decay than bandgap narrowing at CS_2 indices). Infiltration topology controls signal routing (L-bend selectivity S = 0.98), though modulation depth is weak (Delta varepsilon/ varepsilon_ textSi = 11 %). A phenomenological optothermal feedback model produces self-organized pathways that achieve 63 % of a hand-designed waveguide's bandgap transmission (7.6 times the heavily suppressed empty-crystal baseline). Amplitude competition between counter-propagating sources produces strong, monotonic pathway steering (DeltaCOM_x from +0.03 to +4.92 ;a), while pulsed spike-timing-dependent plasticity yields a predictable null result: the timing-sensitive cross-term is suppressed by >10^2 when pulse delays exceed the temporal pulse width. The results provide benchmarks and identify physical limits for bio-inspired reconfigurable optofluidic photonics.
one_liner: Simulations show optothermal feedback can self-organize reconfigurable light pathways in optofluidic silicon photonic crystals.
why_it_matters: It quantifies physical limits and control mechanisms for adaptive photonic routing elements inspired by structural plasticity and neuromorphic behavior.
detailed_summary: Using FDTD and MPB eigenmode analysis, the paper studies selective fluid infiltration in 2D silicon photonic-crystal waveguides as a mechanism for reconfigurable pathway formation. The analysis separates bandgap narrowing from defect-mode weakening and finds defect weakening drives transmission decay about 2.4 times faster for the studied CS2 indices. Infiltration topology enables strong routing selectivity, including L-bend selectivity near 0.98, while a phenomenological optothermal feedback model yields self-organized pathways with 63% of the transmission of a hand-designed guide. The work also characterizes pathway steering under counter-propagating excitation and explains why spike-timing-dependent effects vanish when pulse delays exceed pulse width.
main_result: Self-organized optothermal reconfiguration can form usable optical pathways in optofluidic photonic crystals, reaching substantial fractions of designed-waveguide transmission.
application_angle: The benchmarks inform practical adaptive-routing and neuromorphic-photonics components where fluidic reconfiguration and optical feedback replace fixed layouts.

title: Modular Theory and the Bell-CHSH inequality in relativistic scalar Quantum Field Theory
abstract: The Tomita-Takesaki modular theory is employed to discuss the Bell-CHSH inequality in wedge regions. By using the Bisognano-Wichmann results, the construction of a set of wedge localized vectors in the one-particle Hilbert space of a relativistic massive scalar field in $1+1$ dimensions is devised to establish whether violations of the Bell-CHSH inequality might occur for different choices of Bell's operators. In particular, the construction of the wedge localized vectors employed in the seminal work by Summers-Werner is scrutinized and applied to Weyl and other operators. We also outline a possible path towards the saturation of Tsirelson's bound.
one_liner: Modular-theoretic constructions are used to analyze Bell-CHSH violations for wedge-localized states in relativistic scalar quantum field theory.
why_it_matters: It sharpens the link between relativistic localization, entanglement structure, and nonlocal correlations relevant to secure quantum communication theory.
detailed_summary: The authors apply Tomita-Takesaki modular theory together with the Bisognano-Wichmann theorem to study Bell-CHSH inequality violations in wedge regions of a massive scalar field in 1+1 dimensions. They construct wedge-localized one-particle vectors and revisit the Summers-Werner setup for evaluating Bell operators built from Weyl and related observables. The analysis clarifies which operator choices can produce nonclassical correlations under relativistic localization constraints. It also sketches a route toward saturating Tsirelson's bound in this field-theoretic setting.
platform_or_system: massive scalar quantum field in 1+1D wedge regions
main_result: The paper develops a modular-theoretic framework for testing Bell-CHSH violations with wedge-localized states and identifies conditions relevant to strong nonlocal correlations.
application_angle: While foundational, the results inform relativistic models of entanglement and nonlocality that underpin long-distance and high-security quantum communication concepts.

title: Impact of Topology on Multipartite Entanglement Distribution Protocols in Quantum Networks
abstract: Quantum networks will rely on entanglement distribution to enable multi-user applications such as distributed quantum computing and cryptography. While multipartite entanglement distribution routing protocols have been extensively studied on idealised grid topologies, less is understood about how real network structure shapes their performance and resource requirements. We present a systematic study of four routing protocols for multipartite entanglement distribution, each characterised by the number of paths (single-path and multi-path) and routing strategy (star-based and tree-based), over 81 real network topologies. We identified four distinct topology-dependent performance regimes, where: (i) all protocols perform poorly, (ii) tree-based protocols dominate, (iii) multi-path protocols dominate, or (iv) all protocols perform well. By correlating clusters with graph metrics, we also provide structural explanations for the varied performance of specific protocols. Additionally, motivated by the anticipated high cost of repeaters, we investigated the impact of repeater trimming on the performance of multi-path protocols. Topology strongly governs how far repeater nodes can be removed from the network while maintaining a given performance (distribution rate). For instance, in networks where only 80% of nodes operate as repeaters, well-performing topologies are able to retain over 90% of the distribution rate; whereas sparse, weakly connected graphs exhibit rapid performance degradation, retaining less than half of the distribution rate. Our results provide a topology-aware framework for protocol selection and infrastructure optimisation in future quantum networks, bridging routing design with cost-aware deployment strategies.
one_liner: A large-scale study across 81 real network topologies maps when different multipartite entanglement-routing protocols succeed or fail.
why_it_matters: It turns quantum-network design into a topology-aware engineering problem, showing how to choose routing and repeater placement under realistic cost constraints.
detailed_summary: The paper compares four multipartite entanglement-distribution protocols spanning single-path versus multi-path and star-based versus tree-based routing on 81 real-world graph topologies. It finds four distinct topology-dependent performance regimes, ranging from universally poor behavior to cases where tree-based or multi-path methods dominate. By correlating these regimes with graph metrics, the authors explain why specific structures favor specific routing strategies. They further show that repeater trimming has strongly topology-dependent impact: well-connected graphs can keep over 90% of distribution rate with only 80% of nodes acting as repeaters, while sparse graphs drop below half.
main_result: Topology is shown to be a primary determinant of multipartite entanglement-routing performance and of how aggressively repeater nodes can be removed without major loss.
application_angle: The results directly support cost-aware deployment and protocol selection for future metropolitan and wide-area quantum communication networks.

title: Modulating nonlinear optical responses in 3R-MoS$_2$ Fabry-Pérot microcavities
abstract: Rhombohedrally stacked transition metal dichalcogenides such as 3R-MoS$_2$ offer an exceptional platform for nonlinear optics, naturally forming Fabry-Pérot (FP) microcavities due to their giant dielectric contrast with the surrounding media. However, rigorously tracking the evolution of multiple harmonic fields within these unpatterned monolithic crystals remains a fundamental challenge. Here, we establish a self-consistent framework, spanning from linear broadband reflectance to second- and third-harmonic generation (SHG and THG), to systematically decode these nonlinear behaviors. Moving beyond conventional models, we demonstrate that the nonlinear emission is dictated by a delicate interplay among the intrinsic material absorption, the FP effects at the fundamental frequency, as well as those at the harmonic frequencies. When harmonic photons lie below the bandgap, weak absorption allows the nonlinear spectra to exhibit a complex modulation driven by the synergistic contribution of FP effects from both fundamental and harmonic waves. In stark contrast, severe intrinsic absorption of higher-energy photons heavily damps the FP effects of the harmonic fields, reducing the nonlinear response to an absorption-limited regime modulated almost exclusively by the FP effects at the fundamental frequency. By successfully decoupling these geometric and material contributions across different harmonic orders, our findings provide a precise design paradigm for engineering next-generation van der Waals photonic architectures.
one_liner: A self-consistent model shows how Fabry-Perot resonances and absorption jointly shape SHG and THG in 3R-MoS2 microcavities.
why_it_matters: It gives a practical design rule for tuning harmonic generation in van der Waals microcavities by separating cavity-enhancement effects from absorption losses.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a unified framework linking broadband linear reflectance with second- and third-harmonic generation in unpatterned 3R-MoS2 crystals that act as intrinsic Fabry-Perot microcavities. It shows that the nonlinear output depends on the combined action of cavity effects at the pump and harmonic frequencies together with intrinsic material absorption. When harmonic photons are below the bandgap, both pump and harmonic Fabry-Perot resonances modulate the spectrum strongly; above the bandgap, harmonic-field cavity effects are quenched by absorption and the response is dominated by pump-frequency resonances. This decoupling of geometric and absorptive contributions yields a concrete route to engineer nonlinear van der Waals photonic devices.
main_result: The main result is a self-consistent description that identifies when harmonic emission is jointly controlled by pump and harmonic cavity modes versus when it becomes absorption-limited.
application_angle: The framework can guide thickness and spectral design of compact nonlinear photonic components based on layered semiconductors.

title: Synchronization-induced flat bands in driven-dissipative dimer-waveguide chains
abstract: Flat bands in driven-dissipative systems offer a route to engineer strongly localized, long-lived excitations, yet their selective population via incoherent pumping remains an open challenge. We study a one-dimensional chain of coupled lasing dimers arranged in a cross-stitch geometry and show that the synchronization regime of the individual dimers, controllable through pump intensity or inter-resonator distance, determines the character of the flat band hosted by the chain. In the in-phase (ferromagnetic) regime, the flat band appears as a subdominant, damped mode in the linear excitation spectrum. In the antiphase (antiferromagnetic) regime, by contrast, the dimers decouple and the flat band becomes the dominant, neutrally stable mode: it corresponds to an infinite family of Goldstone modes arising from the independent phase rotations of non-interacting dimers, and its compact localized states are directly observable in the noise response spectrum. Switching between these two regimes via pump control constitutes a pump-induced phase transition of the lasing lattice. Our results establish synchronization engineering as a practical mechanism for selective flat-band population in driven-dissipative optical systems, and open new avenues for studying flat-band physics, including nonlinear effects, Fano resonances, and excitation coherence in experimentally accessible laser and polariton platforms.
one_liner: Driven dimer-waveguide chains switch flat-band behavior via synchronization, enabling selective population of localized optical modes.
why_it_matters: It offers a pump-tunable mechanism to access flat-band localized states in realistic laser or polariton lattices without fine structural reconfiguration.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze a one-dimensional cross-stitch chain of coupled lasing dimers in a driven-dissipative setting. They find that the internal synchronization state of each dimer controls the nature of the chain's flat band: in-phase synchronization makes it a damped subdominant mode, while antiphase synchronization decouples the dimers and promotes the flat band to a neutrally stable manifold of Goldstone modes. In the antiphase regime, compact localized states become visible in the noise response spectrum. The transition between these regimes is controlled by pump intensity or inter-resonator spacing, giving a pump-induced phase transition in the optical lattice.
main_result: The paper shows that synchronization control can convert a hidden damped flat band into a dominant neutrally stable flat-band mode with observable compact localized states.
application_angle: This provides a practical knob for reconfigurable localization and mode selection in laser-array and polariton photonic platforms.

title: Control of emission interval and timing in triggered periodic superradiance
abstract: To achieve more controllable development of coherence in solids, we investigated the effect of a trigger laser tuned to the superradiance transition wavelength on periodic superradiance observed in an Er:YSO crystal. For period control, applying the trigger laser reduced both the superradiance period and its variance, demonstrating enhanced controllability of coherence development dynamics. As the trigger laser power increased, both the period and the number of emitted superradiance photons decreased while maintaining a proportional relationship. This behavior is explained by a reduced superradiance threshold under a constant excitation rate and is reproduced by numerical simulations based on the Maxwell-Bloch equations. For timing control, we found that superradiance could be triggered even when the excitation laser alone was insufficient. This enabled us to control the emission timing of superradiance using short trigger pulses and provided a device capable of generating superradiance at desired timing.
one_liner: A trigger laser controls both the interval and emission time of periodic superradiance in an Er:YSO crystal.
why_it_matters: Controllable superradiant timing can turn cooperative emission in solids into a more usable source for precision timing and triggered optical sensing.
detailed_summary: This work studies triggered periodic superradiance in an erbium-doped yttrium orthosilicate crystal using a laser resonant with the superradiant transition. Increasing trigger power shortens the superradiance period and reduces its variance, while also reducing the emitted photon number in a proportional manner consistent with a lowered superradiance threshold under constant pumping. Maxwell-Bloch simulations reproduce the observed dynamics. The authors also demonstrate that short trigger pulses can initiate superradiance even below the self-start threshold, enabling on-demand timing control.
main_result: The main result is experimental and numerical demonstration that a resonant trigger laser can deterministically tune both repetition interval and start time of periodic superradiance.
application_angle: Triggered cooperative emission could be useful for synchronized light sources, timing references, and solid-state optical sensing schemes that need deterministic emission events.

title: Nonlinearity Selective Quasi Bound States in the Continuum via Symmetry Protected Decoupling in χ(2) Thin Films
abstract: Second-harmonic generation in resonant structures is commonly evaluated in terms of intracavity field enhancement at the fundamental and harmonic frequencies. Here, we formulate nonlinear frequency conversion within a symmetry-resolved overlap framework that explicitly separates resonant field buildup from nonlinear mode projection. Using a simple and analytically tractable Fabry--Perot thin-film-on-substrate geometry, we show that, even in the presence of spectrally bright resonances at both $ω$ and $2ω$, the emitted second-harmonic signal can be strongly suppressed when the spatial parity of the pump-induced nonlinear polarization is incompatible with that of the radiating $2ω$ standing-wave mode. This mechanism gives rise to nonlinearity-selective quasi-bound states in the continuum. Beyond providing a compact interpretation of these nonlinear dark states, the framework unifies pump enhancement, harmonic enhancement, and symmetry-controlled modal overlap within a single predictive metric. More broadly, it identifies thickness regimes in which resonant buildup is accompanied by constructive nonlinear coupling, and distinguishes them from regimes in which apparently favorable resonance conditions remain conversion-inactive because the nonlinear source is orthogonal to the radiating harmonic mode.
one_liner: Symmetry-selective quasi-BICs in chi(2) thin films suppress SHG even when both pump and harmonic resonances are bright.
why_it_matters: It reveals that mode symmetry, not just resonance enhancement, can determine whether a nonlinear photonic design actually converts efficiently.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates second-harmonic generation in terms of a symmetry-resolved overlap metric that separates intracavity enhancement from nonlinear source projection onto radiative modes. In a Fabry-Perot thin-film-on-substrate geometry, it identifies regimes where the nonlinear polarization induced by the pump has incompatible parity with the radiating 2w standing-wave mode. In those cases, SHG is strongly suppressed despite apparently favorable resonances at both the fundamental and harmonic frequencies, producing nonlinearity-selective quasi-bound states in the continuum. The framework therefore distinguishes truly conversion-active resonances from geometries that are bright linearly but dark nonlinearly.
main_result: The main result is a predictive symmetry-based criterion showing when SHG is extinguished or enabled in resonant thin films due to nonlinear mode-parity matching.
application_angle: The method helps designers avoid false-positive resonant conditions and optimize compact frequency converters and nonlinear metasurface-style devices.

title: Distributed Quantum Discrete Logarithm Algorithm
abstract: Solving the discrete logarithm problem (DLP) with quantum computers is a fundamental task with important implications. Beyond Shor's algorithm, many researchers have proposed alternative solutions in recent years. However, due to current hardware limitations, the scale of DLP instances that can be addressed by quantum computers remains insufficient. To overcome this limitation, we propose a distributed quantum discrete logarithm algorithm that reduces the required quantum register size for solving DLPs. Specifically, we design a distributed quantum algorithm to determine whether the solution is contained in a given set. Based on this procedure, our method solves DLPs by identifying the intersection of sets containing the solution. Compared with Shor's original algorithm, our approach reduces the register size and can improve the success probability, while requiring no quantum communication.
one_liner: A distributed quantum algorithm solves discrete logarithms with smaller local quantum registers and no quantum communication.
why_it_matters: Reducing per-node qubit requirements could make cryptographically relevant quantum algorithms more deployable on near-term networked quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors propose a distributed quantum discrete logarithm algorithm that partitions the search through tests of whether the solution lies within specified sets. The overall DLP is then solved by identifying intersections of sets that contain the correct value. Compared with standard Shor-style approaches, the method reduces the size of the quantum register required on each processor and can improve success probability. A notable feature is that the protocol does not require quantum communication between nodes, relying instead on distributed processing structure.
main_result: The main result is a discrete-log algorithm that trades monolithic register size for distributed execution while avoiding inter-node quantum communication.
application_angle: This could broaden the range of quantum-network or multi-processor platforms able to run useful cryptanalytic workloads under limited qubit counts.

title: Noise modelling of waveguide based squeezed light sources
abstract: Squeezed states of light are used for precision metrology and quantum-enhanced measurements, with applications spanning communication and sensing. State-of-the-art squeezed-light sources typically rely on optical cavities to achieve high, usable levels of squeezing. Recently, waveguide-based squeezed-light sources have demonstrated significant improvements in achievable squeezing, with performance currently limited by fabrication-induced losses. In this work, we present a detailed analysis of waveguide-based squeezers, examining the effects of phase noise, multiple loss mechanisms, and fundamental light leakage seeding the squeezer. We further investigate a cascaded squeezer architecture, in which a second waveguide operates as a phase-sensitive amplifier to mitigate out-coupling and detection losses. Owing to their ease of integration, robustness to high pump powers, and low intrinsic phase noise, we propose waveguide-based squeezed-light sources as a promising alternative for quantum noise reduction in future gravitational wave detectors, such as the Einstein Telescope.
one_liner: A detailed noise model for waveguide squeezers maps phase noise, loss, and leakage limits, and evaluates cascaded loss mitigation.
why_it_matters: It strengthens the case for integrated squeezed-light sources in quantum metrology, especially where rugged high-power operation matters.
detailed_summary: This paper analyzes waveguide-based squeezed-light generation with explicit treatment of phase noise, distributed and out-coupling losses, and leakage that seeds the nonlinear process. It also studies a cascaded architecture where a second waveguide acts as a phase-sensitive amplifier to recover squeezing degraded by output and detection loss. The analysis targets integrated platforms that can tolerate high pump powers and exhibit low intrinsic phase noise compared with cavity-based sources. The resulting model clarifies which fabrication and detection imperfections dominate usable squeezing in practical devices.
main_result: The main result is a quantitative noise framework showing how loss, phase noise, and leakage constrain waveguide squeezing and how cascaded amplification can offset downstream losses.
application_angle: These results directly support integrated quantum-noise reduction sources for precision sensing, including future gravitational-wave detectors such as the Einstein Telescope.

title: On the interpretation of Hahn echo measurements in electron spin resonance scanning tunneling microscopy
abstract: Electron spin resonance scanning tunneling microscopy (ESR-STM) has become a powerful tool for probing spin dynamics and coherence of individual atoms and molecules on surfaces. In this work, we perform Rabi oscillation and Hahn echo pulse protocols on individual iron phthalocyanine (FePc) molecules on MgO/Ag(001) using ESR-STM. While Hahn echo protocols are widely used to extract spin coherence times, we show that in ESR-STM they are particularly susceptible to misinterpretation due to tunneling electrons generated by the applied radio-frequency (RF) voltage. The RF voltage not only drives the spin, but simultaneously probes and relaxes it, which consequently leads to an exponential decay that reflects spin relaxation rather than intrinsic phase coherence. We moreover show that varying both delay times in the refocusing pulse sequence is a reliable way to ensure a coherent nature of the echo signal. The extracted decay for the latter protocol suggests that T2 is approximately 30 ns and is thus closer to the decoherence time observed in Rabi oscillation measurements. This is significantly shorter than values reported in previous echo measurements. Our findings underscore the need for caution in interpreting T2 times from Hahn echo and Carr-Purcell protocols in ESR-STM and provide practical criteria for distinguishing true spin echoes from tunneling-induced relaxometry signals.
one_liner: ESR-STM Hahn echo data on single FePc molecules can mimic coherence decay because the RF drive also probes and relaxes the spin.
why_it_matters: It provides a practical protocol to separate true spin coherence from tunneling-induced relaxometry, improving reliability of single-spin coherence measurements.
detailed_summary: The work performs Rabi and Hahn echo pulse sequences on individual FePc molecules on MgO/Ag(001) using ESR-STM and shows that the applied RF voltage has a dual role: it both drives and measures the spin through tunneling electrons. That coupling can produce an apparent exponential echo decay dominated by spin relaxation rather than intrinsic dephasing. By varying both free-evolution delays in the refocusing sequence, the authors identify a more reliable coherent echo signature and extract a T2 near 30 ns. The result revises earlier, longer ESR-STM echo-based coherence claims and sets a concrete validation criterion for pulsed STM spin spectroscopy.
platform_or_system: FePc molecules on MgO/Ag(001) in ESR-STM
main_result: The paper shows that standard ESR-STM Hahn echo decay can be misread as coherence time and finds a more credible T2 of about 30 ns using a modified delay-scan protocol.
application_angle: More trustworthy nanoscale spin-coherence metrology is directly relevant for atomically precise spin sensors and surface-spin quantum devices.

title: Linear Arrays of Metal-Coated Microspheres: a Polarization-Sensitive Hybrid Colloidal Plasmonic-Photonic Crystal
abstract: Colloidal plasmonic-photonic crystals represent a class of hybrid materials composed of a dielectric colloidal spheres photonic lattice and a metal plasmonic film. In this work, the optical properties of a linear array colloidal plasmonic-photonic crystal consisting of silver films deposited over linear arrays of polystyrene microspheres are analysed in detail. Experimental and simulated optical transmittance and reflectance spectra both with unpolarized and polarized light are used to investigate the optical response of the linear plasmonic-photonic crystal. Among the various photonic/plasmonic modes observed, the existence of both propagative plasmonic-photonic hybrid mode and localized surface plasmon mode can be mentioned. The spectral tunability of these structures is highlighted by studying the dependence of the optical response on geometrical parameters such as sphere diameter and grating period. Finally, the linear plasmonic-photonic crystal exhibits a polarization-selective surface-enhanced Raman scattering effect, making them of interest for both fundamental studies and development of applications based on surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy or surface-enhanced fluorescence.
one_liner: Silver-coated linear microsphere arrays form a tunable hybrid plasmonic-photonic crystal with polarization-selective optical and SERS response.
why_it_matters: The structure offers geometry-tunable, polarization-sensitive field enhancement that is attractive for Raman and fluorescence sensing platforms.
detailed_summary: This study analyzes linear arrays of polystyrene microspheres coated with silver films as hybrid colloidal plasmonic-photonic crystals using experiment and simulation. The spectra reveal both propagating hybrid plasmonic-photonic modes and localized surface plasmon resonances under polarized and unpolarized illumination. Optical response is tuned by sphere diameter and grating period, giving control over modal positions and polarization dependence. The arrays also show polarization-selective surface-enhanced Raman scattering, linking the modal engineering directly to sensing performance.
main_result: The authors demonstrate tunable hybrid plasmonic-photonic modes and polarization-selective SERS in linear metal-coated colloidal crystal arrays.
application_angle: The platform is a compact route to polarization-aware Raman or fluorescence sensors with geometry-programmable spectral response.

title: Decomposition of Multi-Qubit Gates for Circuit Cutting
abstract: A large-scale quantum circuit can be partitioned into multiple subcircuits through circuit cutting, where each subcircuit is executed multiple times and the expectation value of the original circuit is reconstructed by classical post-processing from their measurement (sampling) results. In this process, appropriate cut locations are identified after the user-designed quantum circuit, including multi-qubit gates that act on three or more qubits, has been decomposed into single-qubit gates and two-qubit gates such as the CNOT gate. Here, we present a method for reducing the sampling overhead, which refers to the increase in the number of samples required due to the cutting process, by modifying the decomposition strategy of multi-qubit gates. Using MCX and CCCX gates as representatives of multi-qubit gates, we demonstrate that the proposed decomposition method, which introduces a small number of ancilla qubits according to the identified cut locations, effectively decreases the sampling overhead.
one_liner: A cut-aware decomposition of multi-qubit gates reduces circuit-cutting sampling overhead by inserting a small number of ancillas.
why_it_matters: Lower reconstruction overhead makes circuit cutting more practical for running larger algorithms on limited quantum hardware.
detailed_summary: The paper targets the classical sampling cost introduced when partitioning large quantum circuits into smaller subcircuits for circuit cutting. Instead of using a fixed decomposition of multi-qubit gates into one- and two-qubit primitives before selecting cuts, it adapts the decomposition strategy to the cut locations. For representative MCX and CCCX gates, the method uses a small ancilla budget to reshape the decomposition and reduce the number of terms needed in reconstruction. This decreases the effective sampling overhead while preserving compatibility with hardware that natively executes smaller subcircuits.
platform_or_system: gate-model quantum circuits with circuit cutting
main_result: The proposed ancilla-assisted decomposition strategy for multi-qubit gates reduces sampling overhead in circuit-cutting workflows.
application_angle: It can extend the usable problem size of near-term processors by making distributed or partitioned execution significantly more sample-efficient.

title: Majorana-XYZ subsystem code
abstract: We present a new type of a quantum error correction code, termed Majorana-XYZ code, where the logical quantum information scales macroscopically yet is protected by topologically non-trivial degrees of freedom. It is a $[n,k,g,d]$ subsystem code with $n=L^2$ physical qubits, $k= \lfloor L/2 \rfloor$ logical qubits, $g \sim L^2$ gauge qubits, and distance $d = L$. The physical check operations, i.e. the measurements needed to obtain the error syndrome, are $3$-local and nearest-neighbour. The code detects every 1- and 2-qubit error, and every error of weight 3 and higher (constrained by the distance) that is not a product of the 3-qubit check operations, however, these products act only on the gauge qubits leaving the code space invariant. The undetected weight-3 and higher operators are confined to the gauge group and do not affect logical information. While the code does not have local stabiliser generators, the logical qubits cannot be modified locally by an undetectable error, and in this sense the Majorana-XYZ code combines notions of both topological and local gauge codes while providing a macroscopic number of topological logical qubits. Taken as a non-gauge stabiliser code we can encode $k \sim L^2 - 3L$ logical qubits into $L^2$ physical qubits; however, the check operators then become weight $2L$. The code is derived from an experimentally promising system of Majorana fermions on the honeycomb lattice with only nearest-neighbour interactions.
one_liner: A new Majorana-XYZ subsystem code encodes a macroscopic number of logical qubits with local 3-body nearest-neighbor checks and distance scaling as L.
why_it_matters: It combines experimentally plausible local measurements with topological-style protection, addressing scalability in Majorana-based error correction.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a subsystem code on an LxL architecture with n=L^2 physical qubits, k=floor(L/2) logical qubits, O(L^2) gauge qubits, and code distance d=L. Its syndrome extraction uses only 3-local nearest-neighbor check operators, and all 1- and 2-qubit errors are detected, while undetected higher-weight operators are confined to the gauge group and do not alter logical information. The construction therefore blends local gauge-code measurement structure with nontrivial topological protection of logical qubits. The code is derived from a honeycomb-lattice Majorana-fermion setting with only nearest-neighbor interactions, strengthening its hardware relevance.
platform_or_system: Majorana fermions on a honeycomb lattice
main_result: The paper presents a Majorana-based subsystem code with extensive logical scaling, linear distance, and only 3-local nearest-neighbor check operations.
application_angle: If realized in Majorana hardware, it could enable denser, more local fault-tolerant memory and processing architectures than conventional nonlocal-check schemes.

title: Controlled-Z gates with giant atoms in structured waveguides
abstract: Giant atoms are quantum emitters coupled to waveguides at multiple, spatially separated points, enabling interference effects that fundamentally change their light-matter interactions. A notable consequence of the interference is the emergence of decoherence-free interaction (DFI), which allows coherent excitation exchange between giant atoms via the waveguide without radiative loss. Leveraging DFI offers a promising route to implementing two-qubit quantum gates without the need for additional resources, positioning giant atoms as a versatile platform for scalable universal quantum simulators. However, existing work has focused primarily on continuous, Markovian waveguides; in structured waveguides, where non-Markovian effects become significant, only iSWAP gates have been explored. To address this gap, we introduce and analyze a protocol for implementing controlled-Z (CZ) gates with giant atoms in structured waveguides. We first show that while a minimal two-point coupling scheme supports DFI, it also exhibits strong non-Markovian effects that substantially degrade gate fidelity. To overcome this limitation, we propose an extended design featuring a third coupling point. This configuration suppresses non-Markovian effects and enables CZ gates with fidelities up to $97.7\%$ (assuming typical values for experimental imperfections). Our results broaden the accessible gate set for giant atoms in structured waveguides to include both iSWAP and CZ gates, advancing these systems as a pathway toward universal quantum simulators operating in non-Markovian environments.
one_liner: Adding a third coupling point lets giant atoms in structured waveguides implement CZ gates with predicted fidelities up to 97.7%.
why_it_matters: It expands giant-atom gate primitives beyond iSWAP and mitigates non-Markovian degradation in a realistic waveguide setting.
detailed_summary: The work studies two-qubit entangling gates for giant atoms coupled at multiple points to structured waveguides, where retardation and band-structure effects make the dynamics non-Markovian. A minimal two-point coupling geometry supports decoherence-free interaction but suffers fidelity loss from strong non-Markovian effects. The authors propose a three-point coupling design that suppresses those effects while retaining coherent interaction pathways needed for a controlled-Z operation. Under typical experimental imperfections, the protocol reaches simulated CZ fidelities up to 97.7%, extending the gate set available in structured-waveguide giant-atom platforms.
main_result: The main result is a three-coupling-point protocol that enables high-fidelity CZ gates in non-Markovian giant-atom waveguide systems.
application_angle: This improves the viability of waveguide-QED hardware for scalable quantum simulation and computation using resource-light photonic interconnects.

title: High-Visibility Franson Interference Enabled by Passive Photonic Integrated Interferometers at Telecom Wavelengths
abstract: High-visibility Franson interference at telecom C-band wavelengths is achieved using a cascaded periodically poled lithium niobate (PPLN) waveguide photon-pair source combined with fully passive, path-imbalanced Mach-Zehnder interferometers implemented on photonic integrated circuits (PICs). The interferometers require neither on-chip phase shifters nor active stabilization; instead, the phase is scanned via thermal tuning of the chip. By employing a narrow-linewidth continuous-wave (CW) pump and dense wavelength-division multiplexing (DWDM) filtering, energy-time entangled photon pairs with high spectral indistinguishability are generated. We achieve a 4.8% heralding efficiency and a two-photon interference visibility of 97.1% from sinusoidal fringe fitting (raw visibility 95.2% and background-corrected visibility 95.6%), alongside a coincidence-to-accidental ratio (CAR) exceeding 1000 at only 1.7 mW of pump power. These results represent one of the highest Franson-interference visibilities reported for a PIC-based analyzer within a compact, fiber-integrated platform.
one_liner: Passive integrated Mach-Zehnder analyzers at telecom C-band achieve 97.1% Franson visibility without active phase stabilization.
why_it_matters: High-quality energy-time entanglement in a compact passive PIC format is valuable for deployable fiber-network quantum links.
detailed_summary: The experiment combines a cascaded PPLN waveguide photon-pair source with passive path-imbalanced Mach-Zehnder interferometers fabricated on photonic integrated circuits at telecom wavelengths. Using a narrow-linewidth CW pump and DWDM filtering, the system generates spectrally indistinguishable energy-time entangled photon pairs while avoiding active on-chip stabilization; phase is scanned thermally at the chip level. Reported metrics include 4.8% heralding efficiency, raw and fitted two-photon visibilities around 95-97.1%, and CAR above 1000 at 1.7 mW pump power. The result is among the best Franson visibilities for a PIC-based analyzer in a compact fiber-integrated architecture.
platform_or_system: PPLN source with passive telecom PIC interferometers
main_result: The paper demonstrates high-visibility telecom Franson interference on passive integrated photonic analyzers, reaching 97.1% fitted visibility and CAR > 1000.
application_angle: The architecture is directly relevant to practical quantum communication because it supports stable, compact, fiber-compatible entanglement analysis at telecom wavelengths.

title: In-Situ Differential-Light-Shift Cancellation for Trapped-Atom Clocks
abstract: Differential light shifts (DLS) induced by optical trapping fields fundamentally limit the stability and accuracy of trapped-atom microwave clocks. We demonstrate an in-situ method to cancel DLS by simultaneously interrogating multiple spatially separated atomic ensembles at different trap intensities generated from a common light source. By operating the ensembles at set intensity ratios and performing Ramsey spectroscopy, the intensity-dependent frequency shifts are measured within each experimental cycle and extrapolated to the zero-intensity limit. This approach effectively enables shot-to-shot determination of a DLS-free frequency without requiring magic wavelengths or species-specific cancellation schemes. We validate the method for Rb atoms trapped in time-averaged potentials by introducing controlled variations of the total trap power and show that the extrapolated frequency remains insensitive to these fluctuations. The technique is general and can be extended to other systematic shifts, providing a scalable route toward improved stability and accuracy in compact trapped-atom clocks and related quantum sensors relying on optical dipole traps
one_liner: A multi-ensemble Ramsey protocol cancels trap-induced differential light shifts in trapped-atom clocks on every experimental cycle.
why_it_matters: It removes a major optical-trap systematic without magic wavelengths, improving compact atomic clocks and other dipole-trap sensors.
detailed_summary: The paper demonstrates in-situ differential-light-shift cancellation by interrogating multiple spatially separated Rb ensembles held at different trap intensities derived from the same light source. From Ramsey measurements within each cycle, the frequency is extrapolated to the zero-intensity limit, yielding a shot-to-shot DLS-free estimate. The method is validated in time-averaged optical potentials under deliberate total-power variations, where the extrapolated clock frequency remains insensitive to trap-power noise. Because it relies on differential sampling rather than species-specific magic conditions, the approach is generalizable to other trap-induced systematics.
platform_or_system: Rb atoms in time-averaged optical dipole traps
main_result: They show shot-resolved extrapolation to a DLS-free clock frequency that is robust against controlled trap-power fluctuations.
application_angle: The technique offers a scalable path to more stable compact trapped-atom clocks and related quantum sensors operating in optical traps.

title: Reconstructing Quantum Dot Charge Stability Diagrams with Diffusion Models
abstract: Efficiently characterizing quantum dot (QD) devices is a critical bottleneck when scaling quantum processors based on confined spins. Measuring high-resolution charge stability diagrams (or CSDs, data maps which crucially define the occupation of QDs) is time-consuming, particularly in emerging architectures where CSDs must be acquired with remote sensors that cannot probe the charge of the relevant dots directly. In this work, we present a generative approach to accelerate acquisition by reconstructing full CSDs from sparse measurements, using a conditional diffusion model. We evaluate our approach using two experimentally motivated masking strategies: uniform grid-based sampling, and line-cut sweeps. Our lightweight architecture, trained on approximately 9,000 examples, successfully reconstructs CSDs, maintaining key physically important features such as charge transition lines, from as little as 4\% of the total measured data. We compare the approach to interpolation methods, which fail when the task involves reconstructing large unmeasured regions. Our results demonstrate that generative models can significantly reduce the characterization overhead for quantum devices, and provides a robust path towards an experimental implementation.
one_liner: A conditional diffusion model reconstructs quantum-dot charge stability diagrams from as little as 4% measured data.
why_it_matters: Fast, sparse reconstruction can reduce calibration overhead in spin-qubit hardware, especially when direct charge sensing is limited.
detailed_summary: The authors train a lightweight conditional diffusion model on roughly 9,000 experimentally motivated charge-stability diagrams and use it to infer full maps from sparse measurements. They test both uniform grid masks and line-cut acquisition strategies, reflecting practical measurement constraints in quantum-dot tuning. The model preserves physically important features such as charge-transition lines even when only 4% of the pixels are sampled, outperforming standard interpolation for large missing regions. This directly targets one of the main characterization bottlenecks in scaling semiconductor spin-qubit devices.
main_result: The model reconstructs high-fidelity charge stability diagrams from sparse acquisitions while retaining key transition features from only 4% sampled data.
application_angle: This could shorten automated tuning and bring-up time for large quantum-dot processors and remote-sensor measurement setups.

title: Cd(Zn)O on SiC: epsilon-near-zero modes and plasmon-phonon coupling
abstract: Cd(Zn)O stands out as probably the best plasmonic material in the mid-IR, but it is usually grown on sapphire or other passive substrates. In this work we introduce SiC as a novel, highly polar, dopable substrate for Cd(Zn)O. The Cd(Zn)O/SiC system is analyzed as a function of the Zn concentration and thin film thickness, and the results are compared to those obtained in the Cd(Zn)O/sapphire system. XRD and reflectance measurements show that the alloy with 10 % Zn nominal concentration has the best crystalline and plasmonic quality, with optical losses as good as 13 % of the plasma frequency. The thin films show two surface polariton modes: a purely plasmonic symmetric mode at higher energies with negligible frequency dispersion and pinning at the plasma frequency for the thinnest films, characteristic of an ideal epsilon-near-zero mode; and a plasmonic-phononic hybridized antisymmetric mode at lower energies, which thanks to the large value of the higher frequency dielectric constant of SiC compared to sapphire, shows much lower frequency dispersion, indicative of the stronger epsilon-near-zero character. Hence, Cd(Zn)O/SiC offers a promising platform for the development of ENZ devices on an active substrate.
one_liner: Cd(Zn)O films on SiC support low-loss ENZ and plasmon-phonon hybrid modes with improved confinement behavior.
